arXiv:2604.01009v2 [math.SG] 22 Apr 2026

Compactness of Moduli Spaces of Gradient Flow Lines in the Uniform Topology

Tom Stalljohann∗
(Date: 2026)
Abstract.

We prove a compactness result for gradient flow lines in a general set-up which comprises both the situation of Morse gradient flow lines as well as Floer cylinders converging to a critical submanifold respectively. For the compactness result we have to impose two conditions. Both are readily verified in the Morse case but establishing the second condition in the Floer case poses a technical challenge and relies on an exponential decay estimate for Floer cylinders, with coefficient function continuously depending on the initial loop. This is a result of independent interest.

∗\ast Universität Heidelberg, tstalljohann@mathi.uni-heidelberg.de

1. Introduction

1.1. Main Compactness Result

Let MM be a metrizable topological space which we keep fixed throughout. Suppose we are moreover given

  1. (1)

    continuous functions f,G∈C0​(M,ℝ)f,G\in C^{0}(M,\mathbb{R}) with G≥0G\geq 0\,,

  2. (2)

    a compact subset Z⊆G−1​(0)Z\subseteq G^{-1}(0) on which ff is constant with value ζ∈ℝ\zeta\in\mathbb{R}\,.

Definition 1.1 (Spectral Gap).

The spectral gap 𝔖f,G+​(Z)\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z) (for positive ends) is

𝔖f,G+(Z):=inf{ζ−f(x)|x∈G−1​(0)\Zwith ​f​(x)≤ζ}∈[0,∞].\displaystyle\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z):=\inf\left\{\,\zeta-f(x)\,\Big|\,\begin{array}[]{c}x\in G^{-1}(0)\backslash Z\\ \text{with }f(x)\leq\zeta\end{array}\right\}\in[0,\infty]\,\,.

(We follow the convention that the infimum of the empty set is +∞+\infty\,.)

By a closed interval we mean an interval that is a closed subset of ℝ\mathbb{R}. For convenience, we do not consider single points to be intervals. That is, the closed intervals are of the form [a,b][a,b]\,, with a<ba<b\,, or (−∞,a](-\infty,a] or [a,∞)[a,\infty)\,, for a∈ℝa\in\mathbb{R}\,, or the entire real line ℝ\mathbb{R}.

Definition 1.2.

For γ∈C0​(I,M)\gamma\in C^{0}(I,M)\,, defined on a closed interval I⊆ℝI\subseteq\mathbb{R}\,, its energy E​(γ)E(\gamma) is defined by

E​(γ):=∫IG∘γ​(s)​𝑑s∈[0,∞].E(\gamma):=\int_{I}G\circ\gamma(s)\,ds\,\,\in[0,\infty]\,\,.
Definition 1.3 (Class of Gradient Flow Lines).

A class of (f,G)(f,G)-gradient flow lines (or just gradient flow line class) is a family Γ={Γ​(I)}I\Gamma=\{\Gamma(I)\}_{I} of sets, indexed over the closed intervals I⊆ℝI\subseteq\mathbb{R}\,, so that

  1. (1)

    Γ​(I)⊆C0​(I,M)\Gamma(I)\subseteq C^{0}(I,M) for every II\,,

  2. (2)

    for every pair J⊆IJ\subseteq I of closed intervals, the restriction γ↦γ|J\gamma\mapsto\gamma|_{J} gives a well-defined map Γ​(I)→Γ​(J)\Gamma(I)\rightarrow\Gamma(J)\,,

  3. (3)

    Γ\Gamma is translation invariant, that is

    {γ(⋅+s0)|γ∈Γ(I)}=Γ(I−s0)∀I,s0∈ℝ,\left\{\,\gamma(\,\cdot+s_{0})\,\big|\,\gamma\in\Gamma(I)\right\}=\Gamma(I-s_{0})\qquad\forall\,I\,,\,s_{0}\in\mathbb{R}\,\,,

and so that moreover for every closed interval II and every γ∈Γ​(I)\gamma\in\Gamma(I) it holds

  1. (4)

    f∘γ∈C1​(I,ℝ)f\circ\gamma\in C^{1}(I,\mathbb{R}) and (f∘γ)′​(s)=G∘γ​(s)(f\circ\gamma)^{\prime}(s)=G\circ\gamma(s) for all s∈Is\in I\,.

An element γ∈Γ​(I)\gamma\in\Gamma(I) is called Γ\Gamma-gradient flow line on II (or just gradient flow line if Γ\Gamma is understood).

Remark 1.4.

Given a Γ\Gamma-gradient flow line γ:I→M\gamma:I\rightarrow M\,.

  1. (i)

    Since GG is nonnegative, the function f∘γf\circ\gamma is increasing.

  2. (ii)

    Let J⊆IJ\subseteq I be a closed subinterval. Then f∘γf\circ\gamma is constant on JJ if and only if γ​(J)⊆G−1​(0)\gamma(J)\subseteq G^{-1}(0)\,.

  3. (iii)

    Suppose I=[a,b]I=[a,b] is compact. The energy of γ\gamma is

    E​(γ)=∫ab(f∘γ)′​(s)​𝑑s=f∘γ​(b)−f∘γ​(a)≥0.E(\gamma)=\int_{a}^{b}(f\circ\gamma)^{\prime}(s)\,ds=f\circ\gamma(b)-f\circ\gamma(a)\geq 0\,\,.

    If I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty) is unbounded, then E​(γ)=lims→∞f∘γ​(s)−f∘γ​(a)E(\gamma)=\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}f\circ\gamma(s)-f\circ\gamma(a)\,, where lims→∞f∘γ​(s)\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}f\circ\gamma(s) may be +∞+\infty\,.

Example 1.5.
  1. (i)

    For every closed interval II\,, let Γ​(I)\Gamma(I) be the set consisting of those γ∈C0​(I,M)\gamma\in C^{0}(I,M) for which (4) in Definition 1.3 holds. Then Γ\Gamma is a gradient flow line class.

  2. (ii)

    Let Γ\Gamma be a gradient flow line class and S⊆MS\subseteq M be a subset, endowed with the subspace topology. For every closed interval II define

    ΓS​(I):={γ∈Γ​(I)|γ​(I)⊆S}.\Gamma_{S}(I):=\left\{\,\gamma\in\Gamma(I)\,\big|\,\gamma(I)\subseteq S\right\}\,\,.

    Then ΓS\Gamma_{S} is both a class of (f,G)(f,G)- and of (f|S,G|S)(f|_{S},G|_{S})-gradient flow lines.

  3. (iii)

    Let (M,g)(M,g) be a Riemannian manifold and f∈C∞​(M)f\in C^{\infty}(M) be a smooth function. Let G​(x):=|∇f​(x)|2G(x):=|\nabla f(x)|^{2} be the square of the norm of the gradient, so that G−1​(0)=Crit​(f)G^{-1}(0)=\mathrm{Crit}(f)\,. In particular, ff is constant on Z⊆Crit​(f)Z\subseteq\mathrm{Crit}(f) if ZZ is connected. Now let Γ​(I)\Gamma(I) be the set of gradient flow lines of ff, that is

    Γ​(I):={γ∈C∞​(I,M)|γ′=∇f∘γ}.\Gamma(I):=\left\{\,\gamma\in C^{\infty}(I,M)\,\big|\,\gamma^{\prime}=\nabla f\circ\gamma\right\}\,\,.

    Then Γ\Gamma is a class of (f,G)(f,G)-gradient flow lines since

    (f∘γ)′​(s)=|∇f∘γ​(s)|2=|γ′​(s)|2∀s∈I,γ∈Γ​(I).(f\circ\gamma)^{\prime}(s)=|\nabla f\circ\gamma(s)|^{2}=|\gamma^{\prime}(s)|^{2}\qquad\forall\,s\in I\,,\,\gamma\in\Gamma(I)\,\,.

    The energy of γ∈Γ​(I)\gamma\in\Gamma(I) is the usual energy E​(γ)=∫I|γ′​(s)|2​𝑑sE(\gamma)=\int_{I}|\gamma^{\prime}(s)|^{2}\,ds\,.

  4. (iv)

    Let (W,ω)(W,\omega) be a symplectic manifold that is symplectically aspherical. The space of contractible loops M:=Ccontr∞​(𝕊1,W)M:=C^{\infty}_{\mathrm{contr}}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W) with the C∞C^{\infty}-topology is metrizable. Given a Hamiltonian H:W×𝕊1→ℝH:W\times\mathbb{S}^{1}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}\,, consider the Hamiltonian action functional f:=𝒜H:M→ℝf:=\mathcal{A}_{H}:M\rightarrow\mathbb{R} defined via

    𝒜H​(x):=∫𝔻2x¯∗​ω−∫01Ht​(x​(t))​𝑑t,\mathcal{A}_{H}(x):=\int_{\mathbbm{D}^{2}}\overline{x}^{*}\omega-\int_{0}^{1}H_{t}(x(t))\,dt\,\,,

    where x¯:𝔻2→W\overline{x}:\mathbbm{D}^{2}\rightarrow W is an arbitrary choice of filling disk, i.e. x¯|𝕊1=x\overline{x}|_{\mathbb{S}^{1}}=x\,. A fixed one-periodic family J=(Jt)t∈𝕊1J=(J_{t})_{t\in\mathbb{S}^{1}} of ω\omega-compatible almost complex structures on WW induces an L2L^{2}-metric on MM, which at x∈Mx\in M is given by

    ⟨ξ1,ξ2⟩x:=∫01ωx​(ξ1,Jt​(x)​ξ2)​𝑑t∀ξ1,ξ2∈C∞​(𝕊1,x∗​T​W).\langle\xi_{1},\xi_{2}\rangle_{x}:=\int_{0}^{1}\omega_{x}(\xi_{1},J_{t}(x)\,\xi_{2})\,dt\qquad\forall\,\xi_{1},\xi_{2}\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},x^{*}TW)\,\,.

    The gradient of 𝒜H\mathcal{A}_{H} at xx with respect to this metric is

    ∇𝒜H​(x)=−Jt​(x)​(x˙−XHt∘x)∈C∞​(𝕊1,x∗​T​W),\nabla\mathcal{A}_{H}(x)=-J_{t}(x)\,\big(\dot{x}-X_{H_{t}}\circ x\big)\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},x^{*}TW)\,\,,

    where we use the sign convention ω​(⋅,XHt)=d​Ht\omega(\,\cdot\,,\,X_{H_{t}})=dH_{t} for the Hamiltonian vector field XHtX_{H_{t}}\,. Let I⊆ℝI\subseteq\mathbb{R} be a closed interval and identify a map I→MI\rightarrow M with a cylinder I×𝕊1→WI\times\mathbb{S}^{1}\rightarrow W\,. Then the gradient flow lines of 𝒜H\mathcal{A}_{H} on II are precisely the solutions u∈C∞​(I×𝕊,W)u\in C^{\infty}(I\times\mathbb{S},W) of the Floer equation

    (1.1) ∂su+Jt​(u)​(∂tu−XHt​(u))=0\partial_{s}u+J_{t}(u)\,(\partial_{t}u-X_{H_{t}}(u))=0

    for which u​(s,⋅)u(s,\,\cdot\,) is contractible for some (and hence every) s∈Is\in I\,. Now define G​(x):=|∇𝒜H​(x)|L22G(x):=|\nabla\mathcal{A}_{H}(x)|_{L^{2}}^{2}\,. Then

    Γ(I):={γ:I→M|uγ:I×𝕊1→W​ satisfies Eq. (1.1),where ​uγ​(s,t):=γ​(s)​(t)}\displaystyle\Gamma(I):=\left\{\,\gamma:I\rightarrow M\,\Big|\,\begin{array}[]{c}u_{\gamma}:I\times\mathbb{S}^{1}\rightarrow W\text{ satisfies Eq.\penalty 10000\ \eqref{eq: Floer},}\\ \text{where }u_{\gamma}(s,t):=\gamma(s)(t)\end{array}\right\}

    defines a class of (f,G)(f,G)-gradient flow lines. The energy of γ∈Γ​(I)\gamma\in\Gamma(I) is the usual energy E​(uγ)=∫I×𝕊1|∂suγ|Jt2​𝑑s​𝑑tE(u_{\gamma})=\int_{I\times\mathbb{S}^{1}}|\partial_{s}u_{\gamma}|^{2}_{J_{t}}\,ds\,dt considered in Floer theory.

Back to the general setting, for E0≥0E_{0}\geq 0 and an unbounded interval I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty) we define the moduli space

(1.4) ℳ(E0,I):=ℳ(E0,I)(Γ,Z):={γ∈Γ(I)|E​(γ)≤E0​ andthe limit ​γ​(∞)∈Z​ exists}\displaystyle\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)}:=\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)}(\Gamma,Z):=\left\{\,\gamma\in\Gamma(I)\,\Big|\,\begin{array}[]{c}E(\gamma)\leq E_{0}\text{ and}\\ \text{the limit }\gamma(\infty)\in Z\text{ exists}\end{array}\right\}

of Γ\Gamma-gradient flow lines on II of energy bounded by E0E_{0} and converging to a point in ZZ. In Section 2.2 we introduce two conditions (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}). Our main theorem then reads as follows.

Theorem A.

Suppose 0≤E0<𝔖f,G+​(Z)0\leq E_{0}<\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z) and I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty) satisfy (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}). Then for every sequence (γn)n∈ℕ⊆ℳ(E0,I)(\gamma_{n})_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\subseteq\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} there exists a subsequence which converges in C0​(I,M)C^{0}(I,M) to some γ∈ℳ(E0,I)\gamma\in\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)}\,.

The uniform convergence in C0​(I,M)C^{0}(I,M) is independent of the used metric on MM (see Definition 2.1). Observe that ℳ(E0,I)\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} embeds naturally into C0​([a,∞],M)C^{0}([a,\infty],M) and so it makes sense to speak of compactness of ℳ(E0,I)\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} with respect to the compact-open topology on C0​([a,∞],M)C^{0}([a,\infty],M). Since MM is metrizable, the compact-open topology on C0​([a,∞],M)C^{0}([a,\infty],M) is metrizable. So the next corollary is just a reformulation of Theorem A.

Corollary B.

Suppose 0≤E0<𝔖f,G+​(Z)0\leq E_{0}<\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z) and I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty) satisfy (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}). Then ℳ(E0,I)\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} is compact with respect to the compact-open topology on C0​([a,∞],M)C^{0}([a,\infty],M)\,.

1.2. Motivation and Outlook

The above compactness theorem is the result of the author’s attempts to adapt a homotopy stretching argument for Morse-Bott gradient flow lines by Albers-Hein [1] to the setting in which the Rabinowitz action functional takes the place of the Morse-Bott function. Such a homotopy stretching argument relies fundamentally on compactness of a suitably defined moduli space, which in the Morse-Bott case is established by Albers-Hein in [1, Thm. 3.7].

Trying to adapt a Morse theoretic proof for compact moduli spaces to Floer theory naturally led to the general framework of gradient flow line classes, which encode both classical gradient flow lines as well as Floer cylinders. Of course, a compactness result as stated in Theorem A in such a general set-up needs somewhat strong assumptions to begin with. These are encapsulated in assumptions (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}). To be able to apply Theorem A both in the (finite-dimensional) Morse and the (infinite-dimensional) Floer setting, we have to verify those two assumptions in both settings respectively.

Broadly speaking, assumption (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) is ensured by the existence of a Cloc∞C^{\infty}_{\mathrm{loc}}-convergent subsequence of a sequence of Morse trajectories respectively Floer cylinders. In the Morse case this is by a bootstrapping argument and in the Floer case by Gromov compactness (which is standard, although nontrivial). The actual crux to apply Theorem A is the second condition (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}). We establish it for Morse-Bott functions, by quoting an exponential decay result for gradient flow lines of Morse-Bott functions. Importantly, we need an exponential decay estimate in which the coefficient in front of the exponential depends continuously on the initial point of the gradient flow line. This unsurprisingly holds true in the Morse-Bott situation because we are working with the flow of a vector field.

Here is the crucial point: To establish (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) in the Floer case, under a standard Morse-Bott assumption on the Hamiltonian (see assumption (MB(H,N))), one again wants an exponential decay estimate for Floer cylinders with coefficient function in front of the exponential map depending continuously on the initial point of the cylinder in the loopspace. Since the L2L^{2}-gradient of the action functional is not an actual vector field, such a result is far less obvious than in the Morse case. It still holds true though and this will be our second main result, stated in Theorem C.

For the sake of brevity, in this work we restrict the verification of the assumptions (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and the application of Theorem A to the classical Hamiltonian action functional. However, one can analogously proceed with the Rabinowitz action functional instead. As mentioned above, in an upcoming work, we will be concerned with a homotopy stretching argument for the latter functional and, to this end, will make use of Theorem A.

1.3. Organization of the Paper

In Subsection 2.1 we review the uniform convergence appearing in Theorem A and define the notion of a coarse metric. The latter will reappear in Subsection 2.2, in which we introduce the crucial assumptions (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) for Theorem A. Because (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) is a quite strong assumption, we state a sufficient condition for it in Subsection 2.3. The proof of Theorem A will then be given in Subsection 2.4. To conclude this abstract general section, we present a slight generalization (Theorem 2.12) of Theorem A in Subsection 2.5, which often is needed when working in the Floer setting.

Section 3 deals with the finite-dimensional Morse-Bott case on a Riemannian manifold. Both conditions (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) can be checked in a standard way, as we explain in Subsection 3.1, so that Theorem A immediately reproves a well-known compactness result about gradient flow lines converging to a critical submanifold of the Morse-Bott function (Corollary 3.5). Often the compactness of a moduli space of interest is meant with respect to the topology of an ambient Banach manifold of loops. In Subsection 3.2 we illustrate how one easily obtains such compactness from the C0C^{0}-compactness in Theorem A and exponential decay (which is needed to ensure condition (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) anyway). The section is concluded in Subsection 3.3 with an example showing that the upper bound for the energy in Theorem A is optimal.

Section 4 is concerned with illustrating Theorem A in the Floer case. Subsection 4.1 introduces the general setting, the relevant Morse-Bott assumption (MB(H,N)) and states Theorem C. In Subsection 4.2, we then apply Theorem C to verify that conditions (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) in the natural set-up for symplectic homology are satisfied. This will allow us to conclude the compactness result in Theorem 4.9.

The proof of Theorem C is extremely technical and for this reason deferred to Section 5. It comprises a substantial part of the paper.

1.4. Acknowledgments

Foremost, I am indebted to my supervisor, P. Albers, who originally got me in touch with the intriguing homotopy stretching argument which motivated the present compactness result. Second, I would like to express my deep gratitude to R. Siefring for pointing out and kindly explaining to me in great detail and outstanding clarity the diagonalization argument for exponential decay of higher order derivatives of Floer cylinders. I would also like to thank both L. Dahinden, A. Fauck and J. Wiśniewska for helpful discussions.

2. The Compactness Result for Moduli Spaces

2.1. Uniform convergence and Coarse Metrics

Let MM be a metrizable topological space. Given a closed interval I⊆ℝI\subseteq\mathbb{R}\,, we denote by Cloc0​(I,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M) the topological space of continuous functions C0​(I,M)C^{0}(I,M) endowed with the compact-open topology.

Definition 2.1 (Uniform convergence).

Given an unbounded interval I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty) and continuous functions γn,γ:I→M,n∈ℕ\gamma_{n},\gamma:I\rightarrow M\,,\,n\in\mathbb{N}\,, for which the limits γn​(∞):=lims→∞γn​(s)\gamma_{n}(\infty):=\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}\gamma_{n}(s) and γ​(∞)\gamma(\infty) in MM exist. Let γ¯n,γ¯:[a,∞]→M\bar{\gamma}_{n},\bar{\gamma}:[a,\infty]\rightarrow M be the unique continuous extensions of γn\gamma_{n} respectively γ\gamma\,. We define

γn→𝑛γ in C0(I,M):⟺γ¯n→𝑛γ¯ in C0([a,∞],M).\displaystyle\gamma_{n}\overset{n}{\rightarrow}\gamma\text{ in }C^{0}(I,M)\quad:\Longleftrightarrow\quad\bar{\gamma}_{n}\overset{n}{\rightarrow}\bar{\gamma}\text{ in }C^{0}([a,\infty],M)\,\,.

Here C0​([a,∞],M)C^{0}([a,\infty],M) is endowed with the compact-open topology.

The above definitions are independent of any metric on MM. However, the presence of a metric makes matters more explicit, as the next remark shows.

Remark 2.2.

Let dMd_{M} be a metric inducing the topology on MM and I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty) be an unbounded interval.

  1. (i)

    γn​→𝑛​γ\gamma_{n}\overset{n}{\rightarrow}\gamma in Cloc0​(I,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M) if and only if γn\gamma_{n} converges uniformly to γ\gamma (with respect to dMd_{M}) on every compact subinterval of II.

  2. (ii)

    Suppose the limits γn​(∞)\gamma_{n}(\infty) and γ​(∞)\gamma(\infty) exist. Then γn​→𝑛​γ\gamma_{n}\overset{n}{\rightarrow}\gamma in C0​(I,M)C^{0}(I,M) if and only if γn\gamma_{n} converges uniformly to γ\gamma (with respect to dMd_{M}) on II.

Recall that a topology 𝒪1\mathcal{O}_{1} is called coarser than a topology 𝒪2\mathcal{O}_{2} if 𝒪1⊆𝒪2\mathcal{O}_{1}\subseteq\mathcal{O}_{2}\,.

Definition 2.3 (Coarse Metric).

A coarse metric d:M×M→[0,∞)d:M\times M\rightarrow[0,\infty) on MM is a metric whose induced topology is coarser than the given topology on MM.

Convention.

When working with a coarse metric, all topological notions on MM (like openness, continuity, convergence etc.) without further qualification refer to the given (original) topology on MM.

Remark 2.4.
  1. (i)

    Let S⊆MS\subseteq M be a subset endowed with the subspace topology. If dd is a coarse metric on MM, then d|S×Sd|_{S\times S} is a coarse metric on SS.

  2. (ii)

    A metric dd on MM is a coarse metric on MM if and only if for every x∈Mx\in M and every sequence (xn)n⊆M(x_{n})_{n}\subseteq M

    xn​→𝑛​x​ in the given topology on ​M⟹d​(xn,x)​→𝑛​0.\displaystyle x_{n}\overset{n}{\rightarrow}x\text{ in the given topology on }M\quad\Longrightarrow\quad d(x_{n},x)\overset{n}{\rightarrow}0\,\,.

    The “if”-direction uses that MM is first-countable.

2.2. Conditions (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)})

Suppose we are in the situation of Subsection 1.1. That is we have fixed a metrizable topological space MM together with continuous functions f:M→ℝf:M\rightarrow\mathbb{R} and G:M→[0,∞)G:M\rightarrow[0,\infty) and moreover a compact subset Z⊆G−1​(0)Z\subseteq G^{-1}(0) on which f|Z≡ζf|_{Z}\equiv\zeta\,. Moreover, we fix a class Γ\Gamma of (f,G)(f,G)-gradient flow lines. In the following, by a gradient flow line we always mean a Γ\Gamma-gradient flow line. For E0≥0E_{0}\geq 0 and an unbounded interval I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty)\,, recall from (1.4) the definition of the moduli space

ℳ(E0,I):=ℳ(E0,I)(Γ,Z):={γ∈Γ(I)|E​(γ)≤E0​ andthe limit ​γ​(∞)∈Z​ exists}\displaystyle\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)}:=\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)}(\Gamma,Z):=\left\{\,\gamma\in\Gamma(I)\,\Big|\,\begin{array}[]{c}E(\gamma)\leq E_{0}\text{ and}\\ \text{the limit }\gamma(\infty)\in Z\text{ exists}\end{array}\right\}

For E0≥0E_{0}\geq 0 and I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty) consider the following two assumptions:

  1. (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)})

    For every sequence (γn)n∈ℕ⊆Γ​(I)(\gamma_{n})_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\subseteq\Gamma(I) of gradient flow lines with energy E​(γn)≤E0E(\gamma_{n})\leq E_{0} for every n∈ℕn\in\mathbb{N}\,, there exists a subsequence (γnk)k∈ℕ(\gamma_{n_{k}})_{k\in\mathbb{N}} converging in Cloc0​(I,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M) to a gradient flow line γ∈Γ​(I)\gamma\in\Gamma(I)\,.

  2. (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)})

    For every z∈Zz\in Z there exist an open neighborhood U⊆MU\subseteq M of zz and a coarse metric d:U×U→[0,∞)d:U\times U\rightarrow[0,\infty) on UU with the following significance:
    Let (sn)n∈ℕ⊆I(s_{n})_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\subseteq I be a sequence tending to infinity. Let (γn)n∈ℕ⊆ℳ(E0,I)(\gamma_{n})_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\subseteq\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} be a sequence in the moduli space with γn​(s)∈U\gamma_{n}(s)\in U for every sn≤s≤∞s_{n}\leq s\leq\infty and n∈ℕn\in\mathbb{N}. Then

    (γn​(sn))n​ converges to some point in ​Z∩U⟹d​(γn​(sn),γn​(∞))​→𝑛​0.\displaystyle(\gamma_{n}(s_{n}))_{n}\text{ converges to some point in }Z\cap U\,\,\,\Longrightarrow\,\,\,d(\gamma_{n}(s_{n}),\gamma_{n}(\infty))\overset{n}{\rightarrow}0\,\,.

We remind the reader of our stipulated convention that, in the presence of a coarse metric, all topological notions without further qualification are with respect to the original topology. So, in (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}), UU being open and the limit limn→∞γn​(sn)\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}\gamma_{n}(s_{n}) are both with respect to the original topology on MM. In light of the above and the definition of uniform convergence (Definition 2.1), we now repeat our main theorem.

Theorem A.

Suppose 0≤E0<𝔖f,G+​(Z)0\leq E_{0}<\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z) and I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty) satisfy (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}). Then for every sequence (γn)n∈ℕ⊆ℳ(E0,I)(\gamma_{n})_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\subseteq\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} there exists a subsequence which converges in C0​(I,M)C^{0}(I,M) to some γ∈ℳ(E0,I)\gamma\in\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)}\,.

By assumption (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}), every sequence of gradient flow lines in ℳ(E0,I)\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} converges up to a subsequence in Cloc0​(I,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M) to some gradient flow line in Γ​(I)\Gamma(I). So the non-trivial part of the theorem is the assertion that the limit again lies in ℳ(E0,I)\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} and that the convergence is uniform on II.

2.3. Sufficient Condition for (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)})

The next, essentially trivial, lemma gives a sufficient condition for (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}).

Lemma 2.5.

Let I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty) and E0≥0E_{0}\geq 0 be fixed. Suppose for every z∈Zz\in Z there exist an open neighborhood U⊆MU\subseteq M of zz, a coarse metric dd on UU and a continuous function Ξ:U→ℝ\Xi:U\rightarrow\mathbb{R} vanishing on Z∩UZ\cap U with the following significance: For every interval J=[b,∞)⊆IJ=[b,\infty)\subseteq I and γ∈ℳ(E0,J)\gamma\in\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},J)} with γ​(s)∈U\gamma(s)\in U for every b≤s≤∞b\leq s\leq\infty\,, it holds

d​(γ​(b),γ​(∞))≤Ξ​(γ​(b)).d(\gamma(b),\gamma(\infty))\leq\Xi(\gamma(b))\,\,.

Then condition (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) holds.

Following our convention, Ξ\Xi is meant to be continuous with respect to the original topology on MM.

Proof.

For given z∈Zz\in Z, choose a neighborhood U⊆MU\subseteq M of zz, a coarse metric dd and a function Ξ\Xi as in the assumptions. Given sequences (γn)n⊆ℳ(E0,I)(\gamma_{n})_{n}\subseteq\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} and (sn)n(s_{n})_{n} as in (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and so that γn​(sn)\gamma_{n}(s_{n}) converges to some point z1∈Z∩Uz_{1}\in Z\cap U\,. Then Ξ​(γn​(sn))\Xi(\gamma_{n}(s_{n})) converges to Ξ​(z1)=0\Xi(z_{1})=0\,. Moreover γn|[sn,∞)\gamma_{n}|_{[s_{n},\infty)} is an element of ℳ(E0,[sn,∞))\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},\,[s_{n},\infty))}\,. Hence

d​(γn​(sn),γn​(∞))≤Ξ​(γn​(sn))​→𝑛​0.d(\gamma_{n}(s_{n}),\gamma_{n}(\infty))\leq\Xi(\gamma_{n}(s_{n}))\overset{n}{\rightarrow}0\,\,.

∎

2.4. Proof of Theorem A

This section is devoted to the proof of Theorem A. We fix, once and for all, E0≥0E_{0}\geq 0 with E0<𝔖f,G+​(Z)E_{0}<\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z) and I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty) for which assumptions (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) hold. In particular the spectral gap is positive

0<𝔖f,G+​(Z)≤∞.0<\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z)\leq\infty\,\,.
Lemma 2.6.

For every gradient flow line γ∈ℳ(E0,I)\gamma\in\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} it holds

ζ−E0≤f∘γ​(s)≤ζ∀s≥a.\zeta-E_{0}\leq f\circ\gamma(s)\leq\zeta\qquad\forall\,s\geq a\,\,.
Proof.

Recall that f∘γf\circ\gamma is increasing and that lims→∞f∘γ​(s)=ζ\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}f\circ\gamma(s)=\zeta since γ​(∞)∈Z\gamma(\infty)\in Z. The bound on the energy gives

f∘γ​(∞)−f∘γ​(a)=E​(γ)≤E0f\circ\gamma(\infty)-f\circ\gamma(a)=E(\gamma)\leq E_{0}

and this finishes the proof. ∎

Lemma 2.7.

Let γ∈Γ​(I)\gamma\in\Gamma(I) be a gradient flow line with E​(γ)≤E0E(\gamma)\leq E_{0} and

ζ−E0≤f∘γ​(s)≤ζ∀s≥a.\zeta-E_{0}\leq f\circ\gamma(s)\leq\zeta\qquad\forall\,s\geq a\,\,.
  1. (a)

    Given a sequence (sn)n∈ℕ⊆[0,∞)(s_{n})_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\subseteq[0,\infty) tending to infinity. Then a subsequence of γ(⋅+sn)\gamma(\,\cdot\,+s_{n}) converges in Cloc0​(I,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M) to a gradient flow line γ¯∈Γ​(I)\bar{\gamma}\in\Gamma(I) whose image is contained in ZZ.

  2. (b)

    There exists a sequence (sn)n∈ℕ⊆I(s_{n})_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\subseteq I tending to infinity so that γ​(sn)\gamma(s_{n}) converges to a point in ZZ.

Proof.

Part (b) is obviously a direct consequence of part (a).
The proof of part (a) follows the lines of [1, Thm. 3.7]. Let (sn)n(s_{n})_{n} be sequence of positive numbers tending to infinity. By properties (2) and (3) in the definition of a gradient flow line class, the curves γ(⋅+sn)|I\gamma(\,\cdot\,+s_{n})|_{I} are gradient flow lines. Their energy is bounded above by E0E_{0}\,. By assumption (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}), up to a subsequence (which we suppress in the notation), the sequence γ(⋅+sn)|I\gamma(\,\cdot\,+s_{n})|_{I} converges to a gradient flow line γ¯∈Γ​(I)\bar{\gamma}\in\Gamma(I) in Cloc0​(I,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M).

Claim.

γ¯​(I)⊆G−1​(0)\bar{\gamma}(I)\subseteq G^{-1}(0)\,.

Proof of the claim.

We adopt an argument from [4, Lemma 2.1]. By Remark 1.4 (ii), it suffices to show that f∘γ¯f\circ\bar{\gamma} is constant on II. Assume by contradiction that this is not the case. Then, since f∘γ¯f\circ\bar{\gamma} is increasing, there exists b>ab>a with

ε:=f∘γ¯​(b)−f∘γ¯​(a)>0.\varepsilon:=f\circ\bar{\gamma}(b)-f\circ\bar{\gamma}(a)>0\,\,.

By pointwise convergence of γ(⋅+sn)\gamma(\,\cdot\,+s_{n}) to γ¯\bar{\gamma}\,, there exists n1>0n_{1}>0 so that

f∘γ​(b+sn)−f∘γ​(a+sn)≥ε2∀n≥n1.f\circ\gamma(b+s_{n})-f\circ\gamma(a+s_{n})\geq\frac{\varepsilon}{2}\qquad\forall\,n\geq n_{1}\,\,.

Now define recursively a sequence (nk)k∈ℕ(n_{k})_{k\in\mathbb{N}} of natural numbers starting at above n1n_{1} and with

nk+1:=min⁡{n>nk​|sn−snk>​b−a}.n_{k+1}:=\min\left\{\,n>n_{k}\,\big|\,s_{n}-s_{n_{k}}>b-a\right\}\,\,.

This is well-defined since (sn)n(s_{n})_{n} tends to infinity. By definition a+snk+1>b+snka+s_{n_{k+1}}>b+s_{n_{k}}\,. Choose a natural number k0>2​E​(γ)/εk_{0}>2E(\gamma)/\varepsilon\,. We estimate

E​(γ)=∫a∞G∘γ​(s)​𝑑s\displaystyle E(\gamma)=\int_{a}^{\infty}G\circ\gamma(s)\,ds ≥∑k=1k0∫a+snkb+snkG∘γ​(s)​𝑑s=∑k=1k0∫a+snkb+snk(f∘γ)′​(s)​𝑑s\displaystyle\geq\sum_{k=1}^{k_{0}}\int_{a+s_{n_{k}}}^{b+s_{n_{k}}}G\circ\gamma(s)\,ds=\sum_{k=1}^{k_{0}}\int_{a+s_{n_{k}}}^{b+s_{n_{k}}}(f\circ\gamma)^{\prime}(s)\,ds
=∑k=1k0f∘γ​(b+snk)−f∘γ​(a+snk)\displaystyle=\sum_{k=1}^{k_{0}}f\circ\gamma(b+s_{n_{k}})-f\circ\gamma(a+s_{n_{k}})
≥∑k=1k0ε2>E​(γ).\displaystyle\geq\sum_{k=1}^{k_{0}}\frac{\varepsilon}{2}>E(\gamma)\,\,.

This contradiction finishes the proof of the claim. ∎

Now let s≥as\geq a be arbitrary. By assumption and convergence of γ​(s+sn)\gamma(s+s_{n}) to γ¯​(s)\bar{\gamma}(s)\,, we have

ζ−E0≤f∘γ¯​(s)≤ζ.\zeta-E_{0}\leq f\circ\bar{\gamma}(s)\leq\zeta\,\,.

Using the definition of the spectral gap 𝔖f,G+​(Z)\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z) and that E0<𝔖f,G+​(Z)E_{0}<\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z)\,, we conclude that γ¯​(s)∈G−1​(0)\bar{\gamma}(s)\in G^{-1}(0) lies in ZZ. This finishes the proof of part (a). ∎

As is common, by a neighborhood of a subset S⊆MS\subseteq M, we mean a subset of MM whose interior contains SS.

Lemma 2.8.

For every open neighborhood U⊆MU\subseteq M of ZZ there exists δ=δ​(U)>0\delta=\delta(U)>0 so that for every γ∈ℳ(E0,I)\gamma\in\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} it holds

∀s≥a:ζ−f∘γ(s)≤δ⟹γ(s)∈U.\forall\,s\geq a:\quad\zeta-f\circ\gamma(s)\leq\delta\,\,\,\Longrightarrow\,\,\,\gamma(s)\in U\,\,.
Proof.

Fix an arbitrary open neighborhood UU of ZZ. Suppose by contradiction that no δ\delta as claimed exists. Then we can choose sequences (δn)n⊆(0,∞)(\delta_{n})_{n}\subseteq(0,\infty) and (γn)n⊆ℳ(E0,I)(\gamma_{n})_{n}\subseteq\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} as well as (sn)n⊆I=[a,∞)(s_{n})_{n}\subseteq I=[a,\infty) so that

{δn→0​ as ​n→∞,0≤ζ−f∘γn​(sn)≤δn​ for all ​n,γn​(sn)∉U​ for all ​n.\displaystyle\begin{cases}\delta_{n}\rightarrow 0\,\,\text{ as }n\rightarrow\infty\,,\\ 0\leq\zeta-f\circ\gamma_{n}(s_{n})\leq\delta_{n}\,\,\text{ for all }n\,,\\ \gamma_{n}(s_{n})\notin U\,\,\text{ for all }n\,.\end{cases}

It follows that f∘γn​(sn)f\circ\gamma_{n}(s_{n}) tends to ζ\zeta\,. Using assumption (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}), up to a subsequence (which we suppress in the notation), the sequence of gradient flow lines γn(⋅+sn−a)|I\gamma_{n}(\,\cdot\,+s_{n}-a)|_{I} converges in Cloc0​(I,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M) to a gradient flow line γ∈Γ​(I)\gamma\in\Gamma(I)\,. Then

(2.1) f∘γ​(a)=limn→∞f∘γn​(sn)=ζ.f\circ\gamma(a)=\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}f\circ\gamma_{n}(s_{n})=\zeta\,\,.

Due to Lemma 2.6, the functions f∘γnf\circ\gamma_{n} are bounded above by ζ\zeta\,, hence also f∘γ≤ζf\circ\gamma\leq\zeta by pointwise convergence. Combining this with (2.1) and the fact that f∘γ:I=[a,∞)→ℝf\circ\gamma:I=[a,\infty)\rightarrow\mathbb{R} is increasing since γ\gamma is a gradient flow line, we infer that f∘γf\circ\gamma must be constant. By Remark 1.4 (ii), thus γ​(a)\gamma(a) lies in G−1​(0)G^{-1}(0). Again by (2.1) and since the spectral gap is positive, γ​(a)\gamma(a) must in fact lie in ZZ\,. Since γn​(sn)\gamma_{n}(s_{n}) tends to γ​(a)∈Z⊆U\gamma(a)\in Z\subseteq U\,, almost all γn​(sn)\gamma_{n}(s_{n}) must lie in UU, contradicting our choices above. ∎

Lemma 2.9.

For every open neighborhood U⊆MU\subseteq M of ZZ there exists s0≥as_{0}\geq a with

∀γ∈ℳ(E0,I):γ([s0,∞))⊆U.\forall\,\gamma\in\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)}:\quad\gamma([s_{0},\infty))\subseteq U\,\,.
Proof.

Let UU be arbitrary but fixed and choose δ=δ​(U)>0\delta=\delta(U)>0 as in Lemma 2.8. Suppose by contradiction that no s0s_{0} as claimed exists. Then we can choose sequences (sn)n⊆I(s_{n})_{n}\subseteq I tending to infinity and (γn)n⊆ℳ(E0,I)(\gamma_{n})_{n}\subseteq\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} with γn​(sn)∉U\gamma_{n}(s_{n})\notin U for all n∈ℕn\in\mathbb{N}. By assumption (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}), up to a subsequence (suppressed in the notation), the sequence γn\gamma_{n} converges in Cloc0​(I,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M) to a gradient flow line γ∈Γ​(I)\gamma\in\Gamma(I)\,. Due to Lemma 2.6 and pointwise convergence γn→γ\gamma_{n}\rightarrow\gamma, we have

ζ−E0≤f∘γ​(s)≤ζ∀s≥a\zeta-E_{0}\leq f\circ\gamma(s)\leq\zeta\qquad\forall\,s\geq a

and, by Fatou’s lemma and since the γn∈ℳ(E0,I)\gamma_{n}\in\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} have energy bounded by E0E_{0}\,, moreover

E​(γ)≤lim infn→∞E​(γn)≤E0.E(\gamma)\leq\liminf_{n\rightarrow\infty}E(\gamma_{n})\leq E_{0}\,\,.

By Lemma 2.7 (b), there exists a sequence (s¯k)k⊆I(\bar{s}_{k})_{k}\subseteq I tending to infinity so that γ​(s¯k)\gamma(\bar{s}_{k}) converges to a point in ZZ. We can thus choose a sufficiently large k0∈ℕk_{0}\in\mathbb{N} with

ζ−δ2≤f∘γ​(s¯k0)≤ζ.\zeta-\frac{\delta}{2}\leq f\circ\gamma(\bar{s}_{k_{0}})\leq\zeta\,\,.

By pointwise convergence of γn\gamma_{n} to γ\gamma\,, there exists n0∈ℕn_{0}\in\mathbb{N} so that

ζ−δ≤f∘γn​(s¯k0)≤ζ∀n≥n0.\zeta-\delta\leq f\circ\gamma_{n}(\bar{s}_{k_{0}})\leq\zeta\qquad\forall\,n\geq n_{0}\,\,.

Now f∘γnf\circ\gamma_{n} is increasing and hence

ζ−δ≤f∘γn​(s)≤ζ∀s≥s¯k0,n≥n0.\zeta-\delta\leq f\circ\gamma_{n}(s)\leq\zeta\qquad\forall\,s\geq\bar{s}_{k_{0}}\,,\,n\geq n_{0}\,\,.

By our choice of δ\delta this implies that γn​([s¯k0,∞))⊆U\gamma_{n}([\bar{s}_{k_{0}},\infty))\subseteq U for every n≥n0n\geq n_{0}\,. This contradicts the fact that (sn)n(s_{n})_{n} tends to infinity and satisfies γn​(sn)∉U\gamma_{n}(s_{n})\notin U for every nn. ∎

Lemma 2.10.

Given z∈Zz\in Z and a sequence (γn)n⊆ℳ(E0,I)(\gamma_{n})_{n}\subseteq\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} for which (γn​(∞))n(\gamma_{n}(\infty))_{n} converges to zz. Then for every neighborhood V⊆MV\subseteq M of zz there exist s¯≥a\bar{s}\geq a and n0∈ℕn_{0}\in\mathbb{N} with

∀n≥n0:γn([s¯,∞))⊆V.\forall\,n\geq n_{0}:\quad\gamma_{n}([\bar{s},\infty))\subseteq V\,\,.
Proof.

Let z∈Zz\in Z be fixed and let (γn)n⊆ℳ(E0,I)(\gamma_{n})_{n}\subseteq\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} be a sequence for which the limits γn​(∞)∈Z\gamma_{n}(\infty)\in Z converge to zz as n→∞n\rightarrow\infty\,. Choose an open neighborhood U⊆MU\subseteq M of zz and a coarse metric dd on UU as in (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}). It obviously suffices to show the statement of the lemma for a neighborhood basis of zz. Let V⊆MV\subseteq M be an arbitrary open neighborhood of zz whose closure V¯\overline{V} is contained in UU\,. (Such VV constitute a neighborhood basis of zz since MM is metrizable). Because γn​(∞)\gamma_{n}(\infty) converge to zz, there exists n0∈ℕn_{0}\in\mathbb{N} with γn​(∞)∈V\gamma_{n}(\infty)\in V for n≥n0n\geq n_{0}\,. For n≥n0n\geq n_{0} define

sn:=inf{s≥a|γn​([s,∞))⊆V}.s_{n}:=\inf\left\{\,s\geq a\,\big|\,\gamma_{n}([s,\infty))\subseteq V\right\}\,\,.

Notice that sns_{n} is finite. The lemma follows if we manage to show that

supn≥n0sn<∞.\sup_{n\geq n_{0}}s_{n}<\infty\,\,.

We assume by contradiction that this is not the case. Then, up to extracting a subsequence (which we suppress in the notation), we can assume that (sn)n(s_{n})_{n} tends to infinity and that sn>as_{n}>a for every nn. By definition of sns_{n} we have

γn​([sn,∞))⊆V¯⊆U\gamma_{n}([s_{n},\infty))\subseteq\overline{V}\subseteq U

and moreover γn​(sn)∈∂V\gamma_{n}(s_{n})\in\partial V since sn>as_{n}>a\,.

Claim.

A subsequence of γn​(sn)\gamma_{n}(s_{n}) converges to a point in ZZ.

Proof of the claim.

By (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}), a subsequence (suppressed in the notation) of γn(⋅+sn−a)|I\gamma_{n}(\,\cdot\,+s_{n}-a)|_{I} converges in Cloc0​(I,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M). In particular γn​(sn)\gamma_{n}(s_{n}) converges to some point z∗∈Mz_{*}\in M\,. Assume by contradiction that z∗∉Zz_{*}\notin Z\,. As metrizable space, MM is a regular Hausdorff space, so we can choose an open neighborhood UZ⊆MU_{Z}\subseteq M of the compactum ZZ whose closure does not contain z∗z_{*}\,. By Lemma 2.9, there exists s¯≥a\bar{s}\geq a with γn​([s¯,∞))⊆UZ\gamma_{n}([\bar{s},\infty))\subseteq U_{Z} for every nn. As (sn)n(s_{n})_{n} tends to infinity, we see that γn​(sn)∈UZ\gamma_{n}(s_{n})\in U_{Z} for almost all nn and hence that z∗=limnγn​(sn)z_{*}=\lim_{n}\gamma_{n}(s_{n}) lies in the closure of UZU_{Z}\,. Contradiction. ∎

By the claim, up to a further subsequence (suppressed in the notation), we can assume that γn​(sn)\gamma_{n}(s_{n}) converges to some point z∗∈Zz_{*}\in Z. As limit of the sequence (γn​(sn))n(\gamma_{n}(s_{n}))_{n} contained in ∂V\partial V, also z∗z_{*} lies in ∂V\partial V. In particular z∗z_{*} is distinct from z∈Vz\in V\,. On the other hand, we can estimate

d​(z∗,z)\displaystyle d(z_{*},z) ≤d​(z∗,γn​(sn))+d​(γn​(sn),γn​(∞))+d​(γn​(∞),z).\displaystyle\leq d(z_{*},\gamma_{n}(s_{n}))+d(\gamma_{n}(s_{n}),\gamma_{n}(\infty))+d(\gamma_{n}(\infty),z)\,\,.

The first and third summand tend to zero as n→∞n\rightarrow\infty since γn​(sn)\gamma_{n}(s_{n}) converges to z∗z_{*} and γn​(∞)\gamma_{n}(\infty) converges to zz and since dd is a coarse metric (see Remark 2.4). The second summand d​(γn​(sn),γn​(∞))d(\gamma_{n}(s_{n}),\gamma_{n}(\infty)) tends to zero because the coarse metric dd has the property described in (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}), using additionally that γn​([sn,∞])⊆V¯⊆U\gamma_{n}([s_{n},\infty])\subseteq\overline{V}\subseteq U and again that γn​(sn)\gamma_{n}(s_{n}) tends to z∗∈Z∩Uz_{*}\in Z\cap U\,. Thus d​(z∗,z)=0d(z_{*},z)=0\,, contradicting our above observation that z∗z_{*} is distinct from zz. This contradiction finishes the proof. ∎

The proof idea of the above Lemma 2.10 is sketched in Figure 1. The compactness theorem is now an immediate consequence of the lemma.

Proof of Theorem A.

Given an arbitrary sequence (γn)n⊆ℳ(E0,I)(\gamma_{n})_{n}\subseteq\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)}\,. By (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}), a subsequence (suppressed in the notation) of (γn)n(\gamma_{n})_{n} converges to a gradient flow line γ∈Γ​(I)\gamma\in\Gamma(I) in Cloc0​(I,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M). By Fatou’s lemma

E​(γ)≤lim infn→∞E​(γn)≤E0.E(\gamma)\leq\liminf_{n\rightarrow\infty}E(\gamma_{n})\leq E_{0}\,\,.

Since ZZ is compact, up to a further subsequence, we can assume that the γn​(∞)∈Z\gamma_{n}(\infty)\in Z converge to some z∈Zz\in Z. From Lemma 2.10, applied to this zz and (γn)n(\gamma_{n})_{n}\,, and pointwise convergence γn→γ\gamma_{n}\rightarrow\gamma\,, we infer that γ​(s)→z\gamma(s)\rightarrow z as s→∞s\rightarrow\infty. Hence γ\gamma is an element of ℳ(E0,I)\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)}\,. The uniform convergence γn​→𝑛​γ\gamma_{n}\overset{n}{\rightarrow}\gamma in C0​(I,M)C^{0}(I,M) follows from Cloc0​(I,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M)-convergence and Lemma 2.10 again. ∎

2.5. Variants of the Compactness Theorem

In practical applications one sometimes need slight variations of Theorem A. We list them here for completeness. Their proofs are completely analogous and left to the reader.

Remark 2.11.

There are analogous versions of Theorem A (with the assumptions therein adapted mutatis mutandis) for

  1. (i)

    gradient flow lines on unbounded intervals of the form (−∞,a](-\infty,a]. For this one needs to replace the spectral gap 𝔖f,G+​(Z)\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z) for positive ends by the spectral gap for negative ends

    𝔖f,G−(Z):=inf{f(x)−ζ|x∈G−1​(0)\Zwith ​ζ≤f​(x)}∈[0,∞].\displaystyle\mathfrak{S}^{-}_{f,G}(Z):=\inf\left\{\,f(x)-\zeta\,\Big|\,\begin{array}[]{c}x\in G^{-1}(0)\backslash Z\\ \text{with }\zeta\leq f(x)\end{array}\right\}\in[0,\infty]\,\,.
  2. (ii)

    negative gradient flow lines.

In Floer theory, one often cannot show the precise condition (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) as the usual rescaling argument to show uniformly bounded gradients (by producing JJ-holomorphic bubbles if gradients explode) needs an arbitrarily small margin away from the boundary of the interval. For this reason, we introduce a modification of condition (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}): For intervals I1=[a1,∞)⊆I=[a,∞)I_{1}=[a_{1},\infty)\subseteq I=[a,\infty)\,, consider instead

  1. (A1’(E0,I,I1){}_{(E_{0},I,I_{1})})

    For every sequence (γn)n⊆Γ​(I)(\gamma_{n})_{n}\subseteq\Gamma(I) with energy uniformly bounded by E0E_{0} there exists a subsequence converging in Cloc0​(I1,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I_{1},M) to some γ∈Γ​(I1)\gamma\in\Gamma(I_{1})\,.

Replacing assumption (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) with (A1’(E0,I,I1){}_{(E_{0},I,I_{1})}), we obtain the following generalization of Theorem A.

Theorem 2.12.

Suppose 0≤E0<𝔖f,G+​(Z)0\leq E_{0}<\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z) and I1=[a1,∞)⊆I=[a,∞)I_{1}=[a_{1},\infty)\subseteq I=[a,\infty) satisfy (A1’(E0,I,I1){}_{(E_{0},I,I_{1})}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}). Then for every sequence (γn)n∈ℕ⊆ℳ(E0,I)(\gamma_{n})_{n\in\mathbb{N}}\subseteq\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I)} there exists a subsequence which converges in C0​(I1,M)C^{0}(I_{1},M) to some γ∈ℳ(E0,I1)\gamma\in\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},I_{1})}\,.

Proof.

The proof is completely analogous to the one of Theorem A. We just point out that one must prove a version of Lemma 2.7 which asserts that for every sequence (sn)n⊆[0,∞)(s_{n})_{n}\subseteq[0,\infty) tending to infinity and every γ∈Γ​(I1)\gamma\in\Gamma(I_{1}) with energy bounded by E0E_{0} and f∘γf\circ\gamma taking values in [ζ−E0,ζ][\zeta-E_{0},\zeta]\,, a subsequence of γ(⋅+sn)\gamma(\,\cdot\,+s_{n}) converges in Cloc0​(I1,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I_{1},M) to some γ¯∈Γ​(I1)\bar{\gamma}\in\Gamma(I_{1}) with image contained in ZZ. To this end, apply (A1’(E0,I,I1){}_{(E_{0},I,I_{1})}) to the sequence γ(⋅+sn)\gamma(\,\cdot\,+s_{n}) of gradient flow lines defined on [a1−sn,∞)⊇I[a_{1}-s_{n},\infty)\supseteq I (for nn sufficiently large). This version of Lemma 2.7 suffices for the proof of Lemma 2.9 to go through. ∎

3. Illustrating the Compactness Result in the Morse Setting

In this section, we illustrate Theorem A by reproving various compactness results in Morse-Bott theory. In Subsection 3.1 we show how to verify conditions (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) under a Morse-Bott assumption, so that Corollary 3.5 will be an immediate consequence of Theorem A. In the analytic approach to Morse theory, as carried out in [9], one is primarily interested in compactness of moduli spaces with respect to the subspace topology of an ambient Banach manifold of maps. In Subsection 3.2, we will illustrate how to easily derive the latter kind of compactness from the compactness in the topology of uniform convergence and an exponential decay estimate. Finally, Subsection 3.3 contains an example, showing that the upper bound for the energy in Theorem A is optimal.

3.1. Gradient Flow Lines of Morse-Bott Functions

Let (M,g)(M,g) be a (finite dimensional) Riemannian manifold and f∈C∞​(M)f\in C^{\infty}(M) be a smooth function which is bounded below and proper. (These conditions are automatically fulfilled if MM is a closed manifold.) In particular ff has compact sublevel sets. Let Z⊆Crit​(f)Z\subseteq\mathrm{Crit}(f) be a closed (i.e. compact and without boundary), connected submanifold of MM and suppose that ff is Morse-Bott along ZZ, that is

Tz​Z=ker⁡Hessz​(f)⊆Tz​M∀z∈Z.T_{z}Z=\ker\mathrm{Hess}_{z}(f)\subseteq T_{z}M\qquad\forall\,z\in Z\,\,.

Since ZZ is a connected critical component, ff is constant on ZZ, say with value ζ∈ℝ\zeta\in\mathbb{R}\,. As discussed in Example 1.5, assigning to each closed interval I⊆ℝI\subseteq\mathbb{R} the set

(3.1) {γ∈C∞​(I,M)|γ′=∇f∘γ​ and ​f∘γ≤ζ},\displaystyle\left\{\,\gamma\in C^{\infty}(I,M)\,\big|\,\gamma^{\prime}=\nabla f\circ\gamma\text{ and }f\circ\gamma\leq\zeta\right\}\,\,,

defines a gradient flow line class with respect to ff and G:=|∇f|2G:=|\nabla f|^{2}\,. The spectral gap 𝔖f,G+​(Z)\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z) for positive ends (see Definition 1.1) is just the ordinary spectral gap for gradient flow lines ending at ZZ, namely

𝔖f+​(Z):=inf{ζ−f​(x)|x∈Crit​(f)\Z​ with ​f​(x)≤ζ}=𝔖f,G+​(Z).\displaystyle\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f}(Z):=\inf\left\{\,\zeta-f(x)\,\big|\,x\in\mathrm{Crit}(f)\backslash Z\text{ with }f(x)\leq\zeta\right\}=\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z)\,\,.

We also have a spectral gap for gradient flow lines beginning at ZZ, namely

𝔖f−​(Z):=inf{f​(x)−ζ|x∈Crit​(f)\Z​ with ​ζ≤f​(x)}=𝔖f,G−​(Z)\displaystyle\mathfrak{S}^{-}_{f}(Z):=\inf\left\{\,f(x)-\zeta\,\big|\,x\in\mathrm{Crit}(f)\backslash Z\text{ with }\zeta\leq f(x)\right\}=\mathfrak{S}^{-}_{f,G}(Z)

and the ordinary spectral gap

𝔖f​(Z):=inf{|ζ−f​(x)||x∈Crit​(f)\Z}=min⁡{𝔖f+​(Z),𝔖f−​(Z)}.\displaystyle\mathfrak{S}_{f}(Z):=\inf\left\{\,|\zeta-f(x)|\,\big|\,x\in\mathrm{Crit}(f)\backslash Z\right\}=\min\{\mathfrak{S}_{f}^{+}(Z),\mathfrak{S}_{f}^{-}(Z)\}\,\,.

Next we ensure that conditions (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) hold for all energy levels E0E_{0} and closed intervals I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty) with respect to the gradient flow line class given in (3.1).

Lemma 3.1.

For every E0>0E_{0}>0 and every interval I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty)\,, condition (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) holds. The convergence of the subsequence can even be arranged to be in Cloc∞​(I,M)C^{\infty}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M)\,.

Proof.

The gradient |∇f||\nabla f| is bounded on the compact sublevel set {f≤ζ}⊆M\{f\leq\zeta\}\subseteq M. Given a sequence (γn)n(\gamma_{n})_{n} of gradient flow lines on II with image contained in {f≤ζ}\{f\leq\zeta\}\,. Then a subsequence converges to some γ\gamma in the Cloc0​(I,M)C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M)-topology by the Arzelà-Ascoli theorem. Deriving the equation γn′=∇f∘γn\gamma_{n}^{\prime}=\nabla f\circ\gamma_{n} iteratively, we see inductively that the higher order derivatives of the γn\gamma_{n} (in finitely many coordinate charts covering γ​(I)\gamma(I)) are uniformly bounded. Again by Arzelà-Ascoli, we conclude that a subsequence of (γn)n(\gamma_{n})_{n} converges in Cloc∞​(I,M)C^{\infty}_{\mathrm{loc}}(I,M) to γ\gamma. It follows that γ\gamma itself is a gradient flow line with f∘γ≤ζf\circ\gamma\leq\zeta\,. ∎

To establish (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}), we need the following exponential decay lemma which crucially relies on the assumption that ff is Morse-Bott along ZZ. We denote by dg:M×M→[0,∞)d_{g}:M\times M\rightarrow[0,\infty) the Riemannian distance function induced by the metric gg.

Lemma 3.2 (Lemma 3.6 in [1]).

There exist a tubular neighborhood UZ⊆MU_{Z}\subseteq M of ZZ and constants A,B>0A,B>0 with the following significance. For every interval I=[s0,∞)I=[s_{0},\infty) and every γ∈C∞​(I,UZ)\gamma\in C^{\infty}(I,U_{Z}) with γ′=∇f∘γ\gamma^{\prime}=\nabla f\circ\gamma and existing limit γ​(∞)=lims→∞γ​(s)∈Z\gamma(\infty)=\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}\gamma(s)\in Z, it holds

dg​(γ​(s),γ​(∞))≤A​ζ−f∘γ​(s0)​e−B​(s−s0)∀s≥s0.d_{g}(\gamma(s),\gamma(\infty))\leq A\,\sqrt{\zeta-f\circ\gamma(s_{0})}\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\qquad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,.

The lemma is formulated slightly differently in [1], notably for negative gradient flow lines and under the additional assumption that ζ=0\zeta=0\,.

Remark 3.3.

Choosing UZU_{Z} smaller if necessary, we may assume that it is precompact. Hence |∇f||\nabla f| is Lipschitz continuous on UZU_{Z} with some Lipschitz constant C>0C>0\,. Given a gradient flow line γ∈C∞​(I,UZ)\gamma\in C^{\infty}(I,U_{Z}) with limit γ​(∞)∈Z\gamma(\infty)\in Z, the estimate

|γ′​(s)|=||∇f∘γ​(s)|−|∇f∘γ​(∞)||≤C​dg​(γ​(s),γ​(∞))|\gamma^{\prime}(s)|=\big|\,|\nabla f\circ\gamma(s)|-|\nabla f\circ\gamma(\infty)|\,\big|\leq C\,d_{g}(\gamma(s),\gamma(\infty))

shows that also |γ′||\gamma^{\prime}| exhibits exponential decay with same exponential coefficient BB\,.

Lemma 3.4.

For every E0>0E_{0}>0 and every interval I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty) condition (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) holds.

Proof.

We want to invoke Lemma 2.5. Choose the tubular neighborhood UZU_{Z} and the constants A,B>0A,B>0 as in Lemma 3.2. Consider the coarse metric dgd_{g} on MM (the topology induced by dgd_{g} is not just coarser but in fact equal to the given topology on MM) which restricts to a coarse metric on UZU_{Z}\,. As continuous function Ξ:UZ→ℝ\Xi:U_{Z}\rightarrow\mathbb{R} take

Ξ​(x):=A​|ζ−f​(x)|≥0.\Xi(x):=A\,\sqrt{|\zeta-f(x)|}\geq 0\,\,.

Then Ξ\Xi vanishes on Z=Z∩UZZ=Z\cap U_{Z} and

dg​(γ​(s0),γ​(∞))≤A​ζ−f∘γ​(s0)​e−B​(s0−s0)=Ξ​(γ​(s0))d_{g}(\gamma(s_{0}),\gamma(\infty))\leq A\,\sqrt{\zeta-f\circ\gamma(s_{0})}\,e^{-B(s_{0}-s_{0})}=\Xi(\gamma(s_{0}))

for every gradient flow line γ:[s0,∞)→M\gamma:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow M with image contained in UZU_{Z} and limit in ZZ. Hence the above choices meet the requirements in Lemma 2.5 for every z∈Zz\in Z\,. ∎

Having verified conditions (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}), we can apply Theorem A to obtain the next corollary.

Corollary 3.5.

Given E0<𝔖f+​(Z)E_{0}<\mathfrak{S}_{f}^{+}(Z) and an interval I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty)\,. For every sequence (γn)n⊆C∞​(I,M)(\gamma_{n})_{n}\subseteq C^{\infty}(I,M) of gradient flow lines with energy uniformly bounded by E0E_{0} and limits γn​(∞)∈Z\gamma_{n}(\infty)\in Z\,, there exists a subsequence converging in C∞​(I,M)C^{\infty}(I,M) to a gradient flow line γ∈C∞​(I,M)\gamma\in C^{\infty}(I,M) of energy E​(γ)≤E0E(\gamma)\leq E_{0} and with limit γ​(∞)∈Z\gamma(\infty)\in Z.

Proof.

By Theorem A, a subsequence (γnk)k(\gamma_{n_{k}})_{k} converges in C0​(I,M)C^{0}(I,M) to a gradient flow line γ∈C∞​(I,M)\gamma\in C^{\infty}(I,M) of energy bounded by E0E_{0} and with existing limit γ​(∞)∈Z\gamma(\infty)\in Z\,. Now, analogous to the proof of Lemma 3.1, the gradient flow equation γnk′=∇f∘γnk\gamma_{n_{k}}^{\prime}=\nabla f\circ\gamma_{n_{k}} and C0​(I,M)C^{0}(I,M)-convergence implies C∞​(I,M)C^{\infty}(I,M)-convergence γnk→γ\gamma_{n_{k}}\rightarrow\gamma as k→∞k\rightarrow\infty\,. ∎

3.2. Compactness of Moduli Spaces in an Ambient Banach Manifold

Continue in the setting of the previous Subsection 3.1. In the analytic approach to Morse theory, one is often not primarily interested in compactness of the moduli space with respect to uniform convergence but the ambient topology of the Banach manifold of maps containing the moduli space. In this subsection we show by an example (see Theorem 3.8 below) how one can still easily deduce compactness with respect to the latter topology from compactness with respect to the former topology. For the Morse case this is similarly stated in [9, Lemma 2.39] and the Morse-Bott case is a straightforward adaption.

3.2.1. Moduli Space of Unparametrized Trajectories

Again let f∈C∞​(M)f\in C^{\infty}(M) be a proper, bounded below function on an mm-dimensional Riemannian manifold (M,g)(M,g). Let Z−,Z+⊆Crit​(f)Z_{-},Z_{+}\subseteq\mathrm{Crit}(f) be disjoint connected components of Crit​(f)\mathrm{Crit}(f) that are closed submanifolds of MM and suppose ff is Morse-Bott along Z−Z_{-} and Z+Z_{+}\,. On Z±Z_{\pm} the function ff is constant, say with value ζ±≡f|Z±\zeta_{\pm}\equiv f|_{Z_{\pm}}\,. We suppose moreover that there exists a gradient flow line γ:ℝ→M\gamma:\mathbb{R}\rightarrow M with existing limits γ​(±∞)∈Z±\gamma(\pm\infty)\in Z_{\pm}\,. From this it follows that ζ−<ζ+\zeta_{-}<\zeta_{+}\,. We assume ζ0∈(ζ−,ζ+)\zeta_{0}\in(\zeta_{-},\zeta_{+}) is a value for which

(3.2) ζ+−ζ0<𝔖f+​(Z+) and ​ζ0−ζ−<𝔖f−​(Z−).\displaystyle\zeta_{+}-\zeta_{0}<\mathfrak{S}_{f}^{+}(Z_{+})\quad\text{ and }\zeta_{0}-\zeta_{-}<\mathfrak{S}_{f}^{-}(Z_{-})\,\,.

If 𝔖f+​(Z+)=𝔖f−​(Z−)=ζ+−ζ−\mathfrak{S}_{f}^{+}(Z_{+})=\mathfrak{S}_{f}^{-}(Z_{-})=\zeta_{+}-\zeta_{-}, which holds for instance if Z−Z_{-} and Z+Z_{+} are the only components of Crit​(f)\mathrm{Crit}(f), then every value ζ0∈(ζ−,ζ+)\zeta_{0}\in(\zeta_{-},\zeta_{+}) has the above property. Now consider the moduli space of unparametrized trajectories from Z−Z_{-} to Z+Z_{+}

ℳ^(Z−,Z+,ζ0):={γ∈C∞(ℝ,M)|γ′=∇f∘γ,f∘γ​(0)=ζ0,limits ​γ​(±∞)∈Z±​ exist}.\displaystyle\widehat{\mathcal{M}}(Z_{-},Z_{+},\zeta_{0}):=\left\{\,\gamma\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{R},M)\,\Big|\,\begin{array}[]{c}\gamma^{\prime}=\nabla f\circ\gamma\,,\,\,\,f\circ\gamma(0)=\zeta_{0}\,,\\ \text{limits }\gamma(\pm\infty)\in Z_{\pm}\text{ exist}\end{array}\right\}\,\,.

Elements in ℳ^​(Z−,Z+,ζ0)\widehat{\mathcal{M}}(Z_{-},Z_{+},\zeta_{0}) have energy ζ+−ζ0<𝔖f+​(Z+)\zeta_{+}-\zeta_{0}<\mathfrak{S}_{f}^{+}(Z_{+}) on [0,∞)[0,\infty) and energy ζ0−ζ−<𝔖f−​(Z−)\zeta_{0}-\zeta_{-}<\mathfrak{S}_{f}^{-}(Z_{-}) on (−∞,0](-\infty,0]. Therefore, Corollary 3.5 and an analogous version for the interval (−∞,0](-\infty,0] yield the following result.

Corollary 3.6.

Suppose (3.2) holds. Then the moduli space ℳ^​(Z−,Z+,ζ0)\widehat{\mathcal{M}}(Z_{-},Z_{+},\zeta_{0}) is compact with respect to the topology of C∞​(ℝ,M)C^{\infty}(\mathbb{R},M)-convergence.

The above corollary is a special case of a well-known compactness result for moduli spaces of unparametrized trajectories up to breaking, cf. [9, Prop. 2.35] for the Morse case. Under (3.2), breaking cannot happen.

3.2.2. The Ambient Banach manifold

Since we are in a Morse-Bott situation, we have to work with weighted Sobolev spaces, the definition of which we recall now.

Definition 3.7 (Weighted Sobolev Space).

Given δ>0,p>1,k∈ℕ0\delta>0\,,\,p>1\,,\,k\in\mathbb{N}_{0} and an unbounded closed interval I⊆ℝI\subseteq\mathbb{R}\,. The δ\delta-weighted Sobolev space Wδk,p​(I,ℝn)W^{k,p}_{\delta}(I,\mathbb{R}^{n}) is defined as

Wδk,p​(I,ℝm):=\displaystyle W^{k,p}_{\delta}(I,\mathbb{R}^{m}):= {u∈Wk,p​(I,ℝm)|(s↦eδ​|s|​u​(s))∈Wk,p​(I,ℝn)}\displaystyle\left\{\,u\in W^{k,p}(I,\mathbb{R}^{m})\,\Big|\,(s\mapsto e^{\delta|s|}\,u(s))\in W^{k,p}(I,\mathbb{R}^{n})\right\}
=\displaystyle= {u∈Wk,p​(I,ℝm)|eδ​|s|​∂siu∈Lp​(I,ℝm)​ for all ​0≤i≤k}\displaystyle\left\{\,u\in W^{k,p}(I,\mathbb{R}^{m})\,\Big|\,e^{\delta|s|}\,\partial_{s}^{i}u\in L^{p}(I,\mathbb{R}^{m})\text{ for all }0\leq i\leq k\right\}

and is a Banach space with the norm ∥u∥Wδk,p​(I):=∥eδ​|s|​u∥Wk,p​(I)\lVert u\rVert_{W^{k,p}_{\delta}(I)}:=\lVert e^{\delta|s|}\,u\rVert_{W^{k,p}(I)}\,.
Instead of Wδk,2W^{k,2}_{\delta} we also write HδkH^{k}_{\delta}\,.

A norm equivalent to ∥⋅∥Wδk,p\lVert\,\cdot\,\rVert_{W^{k,p}_{\delta}} is given by ∥u∥:=∑i=0k∥eδ​|s|​∂siu∥Lp​(I)\lVert u\rVert:=\sum_{i=0}^{k}\lVert e^{\delta|s|}\,\partial_{s}^{i}u\rVert_{L^{p}(I)}\,. We interchange these norms whenever it feels more convenient.

Given γ∈Hloc1​(ℝ,M)⊆C0​(ℝ,M)\gamma\in H^{1}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\mathbb{R},M)\subseteq C^{0}(\mathbb{R},M) with existing limits γ​(±∞)=lims→±∞γ​(s)\gamma(\pm\infty)=\lim_{s\rightarrow\pm\infty}\gamma(s) and coordinate charts φ±:U±→φ±​(U±)⊆ℝm\varphi_{\pm}:U_{\pm}\rightarrow\varphi_{\pm}(U_{\pm})\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{m} around γ​(±∞)\gamma(\pm\infty)\,, the condition

φ+∘γ−φ+​(γ​(∞))∈Hδ1​([s0,∞),ℝm)and\displaystyle\varphi_{+}\circ\gamma-\varphi_{+}(\gamma(\infty))\in H^{1}_{\delta}([s_{0},\infty),\mathbb{R}^{m})\quad\hskip 28.45274pt\text{and }
(3.3) φ−∘γ−φ−​(γ​(−∞))∈Hδ1​((−∞,−s0],ℝm) for some ​s0>0​ sufficiently large.\displaystyle\varphi_{-}\circ\gamma-\varphi_{-}(\gamma(-\infty))\in H^{1}_{\delta}((-\infty,-s_{0}],\mathbb{R}^{m})\quad\text{ for some }s_{0}>0\text{ sufficiently large.}

is independent of the charts (U±,φ±)(U_{\pm},\varphi_{\pm}) around γ​(±∞)\gamma(\pm\infty)\,. We now define the Banach manifold ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+}) by

(3.6) ℬδ(Z−,Z+):={γ∈Hloc1(ℝ,M)|γ​(±∞)∈Z±​ exist and (3.3) holds for anychoice of charts ​(U±,φ±)​ around ​γ​(±∞)}.\displaystyle\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+}):=\left\{\,\gamma\in H^{1}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\mathbb{R},M)\,\Big|\,\begin{array}[]{c}\gamma(\pm\infty)\in Z_{\pm}\text{ exist and \eqref{eq: Morse setting - def Bcal limit condition} holds for any}\\ \text{choice of charts }(U_{\pm},\varphi_{\pm})\text{ around }\gamma(\pm\infty)\end{array}\right\}\,\,.

3.2.3. Charts for ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+})

Next we construct charts for ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+})\,. To this end, we first define a distinguished class of elements where our charts will be centered. Following Schwarz [9], we give ℝ¯:=ℝ∪{±∞}\overline{\mathbb{R}}:=\mathbb{R}\cup\{\pm\infty\} the structure of a smooth manifold with boundary by declaring the homeomorphism

ℝ¯→[−1,1],s↦s1+s2\overline{\mathbb{R}}\rightarrow[-1,1]\,,\,\quad s\mapsto\frac{s}{\sqrt{1+s^{2}}}

to be a diffeomorphism. We construct charts around every γ0∈ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)∩C∞​(ℝ¯,M)\gamma_{0}\in\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+})\cap C^{\infty}(\overline{\mathbb{R}},M) which altogether constitute an atlas for ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+}).
Let us temporarily write d±:=dim(Z±)d_{\pm}:=\dim(Z_{\pm}) and fix, once and for all, a monotone cut-off function β∈C∞​(ℝ,[0,1])\beta\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{R},[0,1]) with

{β​(s)=0, if ​s≤εβ​(s)=1, if ​s≥1−ε\displaystyle\begin{cases}\beta(s)=0&\text{, if }s\leq\varepsilon\\ \beta(s)=1&\text{, if }s\geq 1-\varepsilon\\ \end{cases}

for some small ε>0\varepsilon>0\,. For given γ0∈ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)∩C∞​(ℝ¯,M)\gamma_{0}\in\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+})\cap C^{\infty}(\overline{\mathbb{R}},M) fix slice charts (U±,φ±)(U_{\pm},\varphi_{\pm}) for Z±Z_{\pm} centered at z±:=γ0​(±∞)∈Zz_{\pm}:=\gamma_{0}(\pm\infty)\in Z, that is φ±​(z±)\varphi_{\pm}(z_{\pm}) is the origin in ℝm\mathbb{R}^{m} and

V±:=φ±​(U±∩Z±)=φ±​(U±)∩(ℝd±×{0})⊆ℝmV_{\pm}:=\varphi_{\pm}(U_{\pm}\cap Z_{\pm})=\varphi_{\pm}(U_{\pm})\cap(\mathbb{R}^{d_{\pm}}\times\{0\})\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{m}

is the intersection of the chart image with ℝd±×{0}⊆ℝm\mathbb{R}^{d_{\pm}}\times\{0\}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{m}\,. We choose U−U_{-} and U+U_{+} so small that the charts φ±\varphi_{\pm} can be extended over the disjoint compact closures of U±U_{\pm}\,. We fix an auxiliary Riemannian metric g¯\bar{g} on MM which, pulled back via the charts φ±−1\varphi_{\pm}^{-1}\,, is the Euclidean metric on φ±​(U±)⊆ℝm\varphi_{\pm}(U_{\pm})\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{m}\,. Moreover, we fix an open, fiberwise convex neighborhood 𝒟⊆T​M\mathcal{D}\subseteq TM of the zero section for which

expg¯:𝒟→M×M\exp^{\bar{g}}:\mathcal{D}\rightarrow M\times M

is a diffeomorphism onto an open neighborhood of the diagonal and a smaller open, fiberwise convex neighborhood 𝒟′⊆T​M\mathcal{D}^{\prime}\subseteq TM of the zero section with closure contained in 𝒟\mathcal{D}\,. Since φ±\varphi_{\pm} is an isometry from (U±,g¯)(U_{\pm},\bar{g}) to Euclidean space, we have

(3.7) φ±∘expxg¯⁡(w)=φ±​(x)+(d​φ±)x⋅w for ​w∈𝒟x⊆Tx​M,x∈U±,\displaystyle\varphi_{\pm}\circ\exp^{\bar{g}}_{x}(w)=\varphi_{\pm}(x)+(d\varphi_{\pm})_{x}\cdot w\qquad\text{ for }w\in\mathcal{D}_{x}\subseteq T_{x}M\,,\,x\in U_{\pm}\,\,,

the term on the right-hand side being the Euclidean exponential map at φ±​(x)\varphi_{\pm}(x)\,. We define

Hδ1(ℝ,γ0∗𝒟′):={ξ∈Γ0(ℝ,γ0∗TM)|ξ​(ℝ)⊆𝒟′​ and ​Ψ⋅ξ∈Hδ1​(ℝ,ℝm)​ for anysmooth trivialization ​Ψ:γ0∗​T​M​→∼​ℝ¯×ℝm},H_{\delta}^{1}(\mathbb{R},\gamma_{0}^{*}\mathcal{D}^{\prime}):=\left\{\,\xi\in\Gamma^{0}(\mathbb{R},\gamma_{0}^{*}TM)\,\Big|\,\begin{array}[]{c}\xi(\mathbb{R})\subseteq\mathcal{D}^{\prime}\text{ and }\Psi\cdot\xi\in H^{1}_{\delta}(\mathbb{R},\mathbb{R}^{m})\text{ for any}\\ \text{smooth trivialization }\Psi:\gamma_{0}^{*}TM\overset{\sim}{\rightarrow}\overline{\mathbb{R}}\times\mathbb{R}^{m}\end{array}\right\}\,\,,

the space of continuous sections ξ:ℝ→γ0∗​T​M\xi:\mathbb{R}\rightarrow\gamma_{0}^{*}TM of γ0∗​T​M\gamma_{0}^{*}TM which have image in 𝒟′\mathcal{D}^{\prime} and lie in Hδ1H^{1}_{\delta} with respect to a trivialization. We note that γ0∗​T​M→ℝ¯\gamma_{0}^{*}TM\rightarrow\overline{\mathbb{R}} admits a smooth global trivialization since ℝ¯\overline{\mathbb{R}} is contractible and that every smooth map A∈C∞​(ℝ¯,ℝm×m)A\in C^{\infty}(\overline{\mathbb{R}},\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}) induces a well-defined map Hδ1​(ℝ,ℝm)→Hδ1​(ℝ,ℝm),ξ↦A⋅ξH^{1}_{\delta}(\mathbb{R},\mathbb{R}^{m})\rightarrow H^{1}_{\delta}(\mathbb{R},\mathbb{R}^{m})\,,\,\xi\mapsto A\cdot\xi\,, so that the above condition is independent of the trivialization Ψ\Psi\,.

We fix s0>1s_{0}>1 sufficiently large so that both γ0​([s0,+∞))⊆U+\gamma_{0}([s_{0},+\infty))\subseteq U_{+} and γ0​((−∞,−s0])⊆U−\gamma_{0}((-\infty,-s_{0}])\subseteq U_{-}\,. For every ξ∈Hδ1​(ℝ,γ0∗​𝒟′)\xi\in H_{\delta}^{1}(\mathbb{R},\gamma_{0}^{*}\mathcal{D}^{\prime}) and vectors v±∈V±v_{\pm}\in V_{\pm} we now define the section ξ~(ξ,v−,v+)\widetilde{\xi}_{(\xi,v_{-},v_{+})} of γ0∗​T​M\gamma_{0}^{*}TM over ℝ\mathbb{R} by

ξ~(ξ,v−,v+)​(s)={ξ​(s), if ​s∈[−s0,s0]ξ​(s)+β​(s−s0)​(d​φ+)γ0​(s)−1⋅v+, if ​s≥s0ξ​(s)+β​(−s−s0)​(d​φ−)γ0​(s)−1⋅v−, if ​s≤−s0.\displaystyle\widetilde{\xi}_{(\xi,v_{-},v_{+})}(s)=\begin{cases}\xi(s)&\text{, if }s\in[-s_{0},s_{0}]\\ \xi(s)+\beta(s-s_{0})\,(d\varphi_{+})_{\gamma_{0}(s)}^{-1}\cdot v_{+}&\text{, if }s\geq s_{0}\\ \xi(s)+\beta(-s-s_{0})\,(d\varphi_{-})_{\gamma_{0}(s)}^{-1}\cdot v_{-}&\text{, if }s\leq-s_{0}\,\,.\end{cases}

We lastly fix sufficiently small relatively open neighborhoods V±′⊆V±V_{\pm}^{\prime}\subseteq V_{\pm} of the origin (of dimension d±d_{\pm}) so that ξ~(ξ,v−,v+)\widetilde{\xi}_{(\xi,v_{-},v_{+})} has image in 𝒟\mathcal{D} for every v±∈V±′v_{\pm}\in V_{\pm}^{\prime} and ξ∈Hδ1​(ℝ,γ0∗​𝒟′)\xi\in H^{1}_{\delta}(\mathbb{R},\gamma_{0}^{*}\mathcal{D}^{\prime})\,.
Using (3.7) and that φ±\varphi_{\pm} are slice charts for Z±Z_{\pm} centered at z±z_{\pm}\,, we immediately see that

(3.8) expγ0​(±∞)g¯⁡(ξ~(ξ,v−,v+)​(±∞))=φ±−1​(v±)∈Z±\exp^{\bar{g}}_{\gamma_{0}(\pm\infty)}(\widetilde{\xi}_{(\xi,v_{-},v_{+})}(\pm\infty))=\varphi_{\pm}^{-1}(v_{\pm})\in Z_{\pm}

for ξ∈Hδ1​(ℝ,γ0∗​𝒟′)\xi\in H^{1}_{\delta}(\mathbb{R},\gamma_{0}^{*}\mathcal{D}^{\prime}) and v±∈V±′v_{\pm}\in V_{\pm}^{\prime}\,. Again by (3.7), the curve

s↦γ​(s):=expγ0​(s)g¯⁡(ξ~(ξ,v−,v+)​(s))s\mapsto\gamma(s):=\exp^{\bar{g}}_{\gamma_{0}(s)}(\widetilde{\xi}_{(\xi,v_{-},v_{+})}(s))

satisfies (3.3) for the charts (U±,φ±)(U_{\pm},\varphi_{\pm}) and therefore is an element of ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+})\,.

We are finally ready to define the charts. The map

Φ:Hδ1​(ℝ,γ0∗​𝒟′)×V−′×V+′→ℬδ​(Z−,Z+),(ξ,v−,v+)↦expγ0g¯⁡(ξ~(ξ,v−,v+))\displaystyle\Phi:\,\,H^{1}_{\delta}(\mathbb{R},\gamma_{0}^{*}\mathcal{D}^{\prime})\times V_{-}^{\prime}\times V_{+}^{\prime}\rightarrow\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+})\,,\,\quad(\xi,v_{-},v_{+})\mapsto\exp^{\bar{g}}_{\gamma_{0}}(\widetilde{\xi}_{(\xi,v_{-},v_{+})})

has C0C^{0}-open image in ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+})\,. Consider the collection {Φ}\{\Phi\} of all such charts, indexed over the γ0\gamma_{0}, slice charts (U±,φ±)(U_{\pm},\varphi_{\pm}) centered at γ0​(±∞)\gamma_{0}(\pm\infty), admissible auxiliary metrics g¯\bar{g} on MM, zero section neighborhoods 𝒟′⊆𝒟⊆T​M\mathcal{D}^{\prime}\subseteq\mathcal{D}\subseteq TM\,, admissible s0>1s_{0}>1 and V±′⊆φ±​(U±∩Z±)V_{\pm}^{\prime}\subseteq\varphi_{\pm}(U_{\pm}\cap Z_{\pm}) as above. We give ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+}) the final topology with respect to this collection. The collection {Φ−1}\{\Phi^{-1}\} then constitutes a smooth atlas for ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+})\,. Figure 2 illustrates the asymptotics of a trajectory in the domain of a chart Φ−1\Phi^{-1}\,.

The above charts Φ−1\Phi^{-1} are modeled on open subsets of second-countable Banach spaces. It is not difficult to show that the above atlas admits a countable subatlas, so that ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+}) is a second-countable Banach manifold. In particular, by a result in [8], ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+}) is metrizable.

3.2.4. Compactness of ℳ^​(Z−,Z+,ζ0)\widehat{\mathcal{M}}(Z_{-},Z_{+},\zeta_{0}) in ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+})

We continue with the setting of Subsection 3.2.1. In particular, we assume ff is Morse-Bott along both Z−Z_{-} and Z+Z_{+} and have fixed ζ0∈(ζ−,ζ+)\zeta_{0}\in(\zeta_{-},\zeta_{+}) satisfying (3.2). Let B>0B>0 be a constant as in Lemma 3.2, simultaneously for Z−Z_{-} and Z+Z_{+}\,.

Theorem 3.8.

For every δ∈(0,B)\delta\in(0,B), the moduli space ℳ^​(Z−,Z+,ζ0)\widehat{\mathcal{M}}(Z_{-},Z_{+},\zeta_{0}) is a compact subset of ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+})\,.

Proof.

Fix an arbitrary δ∈(0,B)\delta\in(0,B). Having chosen the constant BB as in Lemma 3.2 and using also Remark 3.3, we conclude that ℳ^​(Z−,Z+,ζ0)\widehat{\mathcal{M}}(Z_{-},Z_{+},\zeta_{0}) is a subset of ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+})\,. Because ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+}) is metrizable, to show compactness of ℳ^​(Z−,Z+,ζ0)\widehat{\mathcal{M}}(Z_{-},Z_{+},\zeta_{0}) it suffices to show sequential compactness. Given a sequence (γn)n⊆ℳ^​(Z−,Z+,ζ0)(\gamma_{n})_{n}\subseteq\widehat{\mathcal{M}}(Z_{-},Z_{+},\zeta_{0}), we know from Corollary 3.6 that a subsequence (which we suppress in the notation) converges both in Cloc∞​(ℝ,M)C^{\infty}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\mathbb{R},M) and in C0​(ℝ,M)C^{0}(\mathbb{R},M) to an element γ∈ℳ^​(Z−,Z+,ζ0)\gamma\in\widehat{\mathcal{M}}(Z_{-},Z_{+},\zeta_{0})\,. It remains to show that the convergence γn​→𝑛​γ\gamma_{n}\overset{n}{\rightarrow}\gamma also is with respect to the given topology on ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+})\,. To this end observe that γ\gamma is an element of C∞​(ℝ¯,M)C^{\infty}(\overline{\mathbb{R}},M) since (in local coordinates) all higher order derivatives of γ\gamma exhibit exponential decay.111This can be seen as in Remark 3.3. Hence we can verify convergence of γn\gamma_{n} to γ\gamma in a chart Φ−1\Phi^{-1} for ℬδ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{B}_{\delta}(Z_{-},Z_{+}) centered at γ\gamma\,, as constructed in the previous subsection. Choosing the charts (U±,φ±)(U_{\pm},\varphi_{\pm}) smaller if necessary, we may assume that they are contained in tubular neighborhoods around Z±Z_{\pm} as in the exponential decay lemma (Lemma 3.2). Let (ξn,vn−,vn+)(\xi_{n},v^{-}_{n},v^{+}_{n}) be defined by

γn=Φ​(ξn,vn−,vn+)=expγg¯⁡(ξ~(ξn,vn−,vn+)).\gamma_{n}=\Phi(\xi_{n},v^{-}_{n},v^{+}_{n})=\exp^{\bar{g}}_{\gamma}(\widetilde{\xi}_{(\xi_{n},v^{-}_{n},v^{+}_{n})})\,\,.

We have to show that

(ξn,vn−,vn+)​→𝑛​(0,0,0)​ in ​Hδ1​(ℝ,γ∗​T​M)×(ℝd−×{0})×(ℝd+×{0}).(\xi_{n},v^{-}_{n},v^{+}_{n})\overset{n}{\rightarrow}(0,0,0)\text{ in }H^{1}_{\delta}(\mathbb{R},\gamma^{*}TM)\times(\mathbb{R}^{d_{-}}\times\{0\})\times(\mathbb{R}^{d_{+}}\times\{0\})\,\,.

From (3.8) and the C0​(ℝ,M)C^{0}(\mathbb{R},M)-convergence of γn\gamma_{n} to γ\gamma, we immediately conclude that vn−v_{n}^{-} and vn+v_{n}^{+} tend to the origin respectively. Again by the C0​(ℝ,M)C^{0}(\mathbb{R},M)-convergence, we can choose s¯>s0+1\bar{s}>s_{0}+1 so that γn​([s¯,∞])⊆U+\gamma_{n}([\bar{s},\infty])\subseteq U_{+} and γn([−∞,−s¯]⊆U−\gamma_{n}([-\infty,-\bar{s}]\subseteq U_{-} for every nn sufficiently large. The differential (d​φ+)γ(d\varphi_{+})_{\gamma} of the chart φ+\varphi_{+} gives a local trivialization of γ∗​T​M\gamma^{*}TM over [s¯,∞][\bar{s},\infty] and we consider the trivialized section ξnφ+:=(d​φ+)γ⋅ξn∈Hδ1​([s¯,∞),ℝm)\xi_{n}^{\varphi_{+}}:=(d\varphi_{+})_{\gamma}\cdot\xi_{n}\in H^{1}_{\delta}([\bar{s},\infty),\mathbb{R}^{m})\,. The Cloc∞C^{\infty}_{\mathrm{loc}}-convergence γn→γ\gamma_{n}\rightarrow\gamma and the already proven convergence of vn+v_{n}^{+} to the origin imply that

ξnφ+=(d​φ+)γ⋅ξ~(ξn,vn−,vn+)−vn+​=(3.7)​φ+∘γn−φ+∘γ−vn+\xi_{n}^{\varphi_{+}}=(d\varphi_{+})_{\gamma}\cdot\widetilde{\xi}_{(\xi_{n},v_{n}^{-},v_{n}^{+})}-v_{n}^{+}\overset{\text{\eqref{eq: Morse setting - exp gaux vs Euclidean exp}}}{=}\varphi_{+}\circ\gamma_{n}-\varphi_{+}\circ\gamma-v_{n}^{+}

converges to the zero function in Cloc∞C^{\infty}_{\mathrm{loc}}\,. We claim that

(3.9) {∫s¯∞e2​δ​s​|ξnφ+​(s)|2​𝑑s​→𝑛​0and∫s¯∞e2​δ​s​|(ξnφ+)′​(s)|2​𝑑s​→𝑛​0.\displaystyle\begin{cases}\int_{\bar{s}}^{\infty}e^{2\delta s}\,|\xi_{n}^{\varphi_{+}}(s)|^{2}\,ds\,\overset{n}{\rightarrow}0&\text{and}\\ \int_{\bar{s}}^{\infty}e^{2\delta s}\,|(\xi_{n}^{\varphi_{+}})^{\prime}(s)|^{2}\,ds\,\overset{n}{\rightarrow}0\,\,.\end{cases}

The integrable function s↦C​e−2​(B−δ)​ss\mapsto C\,e^{-2(B-\delta)s}\,, for some suitable constant C>0C>0 independent of nn, is a dominating function for both e2​δ​s​|ξnφ+​(s)|2e^{2\delta s}\,|\xi_{n}^{\varphi_{+}}(s)|^{2} and e2​δ​s​|(ξnφ+)′​(s)|2e^{2\delta s}\,|(\xi_{n}^{\varphi_{+}})^{\prime}(s)|^{2} by exponential decay of gradient flow lines (Lemma 3.2 and Remark 3.3). Hence (3.9) follows from pointwise convergence of |ξnφ+||\xi_{n}^{\varphi_{+}}| and |(ξnφ+)′||(\xi_{n}^{\varphi_{+}})^{\prime}| to the zero function and the dominated convergence theorem. Combining (3.9) and an analogous version on the interval (−∞,−s¯](-\infty,-\bar{s}] with Cloc∞​(ℝ,M)C^{\infty}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\mathbb{R},M)-convergence of γn\gamma_{n} to γ\gamma, we conclude that ξn\xi_{n} tends to the zero section in Hδ1​(ℝ,γ∗​T​M)H^{1}_{\delta}(\mathbb{R},\gamma^{*}TM). This finishes the proof of the theorem. ∎

3.3. Optimality of Upper Bound for the Energy

The spectral gap as upper bound for the energy in Theorem A is optimal. We will illustrate this briefly in the setting of the previous Subsection 3.2. Recall that f|Z±=ζ±f|_{Z_{\pm}}=\zeta_{\pm} and that we assumed that there is at least one gradient flow line from Z−Z_{-} to Z+Z_{+}\,. Suppose Crit​(f)\mathrm{Crit}(f) consists precisely of the components Z−Z_{-} and Z+Z_{+}, so that

E0:=𝔖f−​(Z−)=𝔖f+​(Z+)=ζ+−ζ−>0.E_{0}:=\mathfrak{S}_{f}^{-}(Z_{-})=\mathfrak{S}_{f}^{+}(Z_{+})=\zeta_{+}-\zeta_{-}>0\,\,.

Examples as above exist in abundance. (For instance the sphere with the height function.) Consider the moduli space of parametrized trajectories from Z−Z_{-} to Z+Z_{+}

ℳ​(Z−,Z+):={γ∈C∞​(ℝ,M)|γ′=∇f∘γ​ and ​γ​(±∞)∈Z±​ exist}.\displaystyle\mathcal{M}(Z_{-},Z_{+}):=\left\{\,\gamma\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{R},M)\,\Big|\,\gamma^{\prime}=\nabla f\circ\gamma\text{ and }\gamma(\pm\infty)\in Z_{\pm}\text{ exist}\right\}\,\,.

Elements in ℳ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{M}(Z_{-},Z_{+}) have energy E0=ζ+−ζ−E_{0}=\zeta_{+}-\zeta_{-}\,, hence they restrict to elements of the moduli space ℳ(E0,[0,∞))​(Γ,Z+)\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},\,[0,\infty))}(\Gamma,Z_{+}) introduced in (1.4). Here Γ\Gamma is the gradient flow line class introduced in (3.1). Fix a gradient flow line γ∈ℳ​(Z−,Z+)\gamma\in\mathcal{M}(Z_{-},Z_{+}) and a sequence (sn)n(s_{n})_{n} tending to +∞+\infty\,. Then γ(⋅−sn)\gamma(\,\cdot-s_{n}) is a sequence in ℳ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{M}(Z_{-},Z_{+}) which converges in Cloc∞​(ℝ,M)C^{\infty}_{\mathrm{loc}}(\mathbb{R},M) to the constant flow line sitting at γ​(−∞)\gamma(-\infty)\,. In particular, neither ℳ​(Z−,Z+)\mathcal{M}(Z_{-},Z_{+}) nor ℳ(E0,[0,∞))​(Γ,Z+)\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},\,[0,\infty))}(\Gamma,Z_{+}) are compact for the topology of Cloc0C^{0}_{\mathrm{loc}}-convergence. A fortiori, ℳ(E0,[0,∞))\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},\,[0,\infty))} is not compact for the topology of uniform convergence.

Remark 3.9.

The above example also shows that one cannot expect a compactness statement as in Theorem A for the moduli space of gradient flow lines γ\gamma converging to points in ZZ as s→∞s\rightarrow\infty and with energy E​(γ)<𝔖f,G+​(Z)E(\gamma)<\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z)\,. That is, for compactness one needs a uniform energy bound strictly smaller than 𝔖f,G+​(Z)\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z).

4. Illustrating the Compactness Result in the Floer Setting

The advantage of the rather general definition of gradient flow lines in Definition 1.3 will become apparent in the Floer case below. Usually, even if a proof from Morse theory carries over to Floer theory, one has to formally repeat the analogous arguments in the Floer case. This has various reasons, one prominent being that the L2L^{2}-gradient of the action functional is not a vector field on the underlying Banach manifold H1​(𝕊1,W)H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W), so that Floer cylinders are not actually gradient flow lines. The advantage of the rather general definition of gradient flow line classes we have given (Definition 1.3) is that they encompass Floer cylinders as trajectories into the loopspace H1​(𝕊1,W)H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W) or even better into the metrizable space of smooth loops C∞​(𝕊1,W)C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W). So we can indeed apply our compactness result in the Floer realm as well, once conditions (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) have been verified. Condition (A1(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) essentially follows directly from Gromov compactness. Condition (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}), however, is much harder to ensure and requires our second main theorem (Theorem C), the proof of which will be deferred to Section 5.

4.1. Morse-Bott set up for condition (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)})

In this section let (W,ω)(W,\omega) be a symplectic manifold of dimension 2​n2n that is symplectically aspherical. The latter means

∫𝕊2f∗​ω=0∀f∈C∞​(𝕊2,W).\int_{\mathbb{S}^{2}}f^{*}\omega=0\qquad\forall\,f\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{2},W)\,\,.

An important special case are exact symplectic manifolds (W,ω=d​λ)(W,\omega=d\lambda)\,. Let H:W→ℝH:W\rightarrow\mathbb{R} be an autonomous Hamiltonian. Assuming symplectic asphericity, we can define its Hamiltonian action functional 𝒜H:Hcontr1​(𝕊1,W)→ℝ\mathcal{A}_{H}:H^{1}_{\mathrm{contr}}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W)\rightarrow\mathbb{R} on the space of contractible loops in the Sobolev space via

𝒜H​(x):=∫𝔻2x¯∗​ω−∫01H​(x​(t))​𝑑t,\displaystyle\mathcal{A}_{H}(x):=\int_{\mathbbm{D}^{2}}\bar{x}^{*}\omega-\int_{0}^{1}H(x(t))\,dt\,\,,

where x¯∈C∞​(𝔻2,W)\bar{x}\in C^{\infty}(\mathbbm{D}^{2},W) is any choice of filling disk for xx, that is x¯|𝕊1=x\bar{x}|_{\mathbb{S}^{1}}=x\,. In the case of an exact symplectic manifold with primitive λ\lambda, we may extend the domain of 𝒜H\mathcal{A}_{H} to H1​(𝕊1,W)H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W) by setting

(4.1) 𝒜H​(x):=∫𝕊1x∗​λ−∫01H​(x)​𝑑t∀x∈H1​(𝕊1,W).\mathcal{A}_{H}(x):=\int_{\mathbb{S}^{1}}x^{*}\lambda-\int_{0}^{1}H(x)\,dt\qquad\forall\,x\in H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W)\,\,.

Let J=(Jt)t∈𝕊1J=(J_{t})_{t\in\mathbb{S}^{1}} be a smooth 11-periodic family of ω\omega-compatible almost complex structures on WW. This gives rise to a smooth family gt:=ω(⋅,Jt⋅)g_{t}:=\omega(\,\cdot\,,J_{t}\,\cdot\,) of Riemannian metrics on WW and to an induced L2L^{2}-metric, which at x∈H1​(𝕊1,W)x\in H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W) is defined by

(4.2) (gJ)x​(X1,X2):=∫01(gt)x​(X1,X2)​𝑑t∀X1,X2∈L2​(𝕊1,x∗​T​W).\displaystyle(g_{J})_{x}(X_{1},X_{2}):=\int_{0}^{1}(g_{t})_{x}(X_{1},X_{2})\,dt\qquad\forall\,X_{1},X_{2}\in L^{2}(\mathbb{S}^{1},x^{*}TW)\,\,.

We emphasize that the L2L^{2}-metric gives a bundle metric, not a Riemannian metric. The gradient of 𝒜H\mathcal{A}_{H} with respect to this metric at the loop xx is the L2L^{2}-vector field along xx given by

∇gJ𝒜H​(x)=−Jt​(x)​(x˙−XH​(x)),\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H}(x)=-J_{t}(x)\,(\dot{x}-X_{H}(x))\,\,,

where the Hamiltonian vector field XHX_{H} is defined by d​H=ω​(⋅,XH)dH=\omega(\,\cdot\,,X_{H})\,. It is important to realize that, while 𝒜H\mathcal{A}_{H} might only be defined on the component Hcontr1​(𝕊1,V)H^{1}_{\mathrm{contr}}(\mathbb{S}^{1},V) of contractible loops, the definition of the gradient makes sense on the entire Sobolev space H1​(𝕊1,W)H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W)\,. For this reason, we can speak of the gradient ∇gJ𝒜H​(x)\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H}(x) at an arbitrary loop xx, not just at a contractible one. The zeroes of the gradient are the one-periodic solutions of x˙=XH​(x)\dot{x}=X_{H}(x) and are in one-to-one correspondence with fixed points of the time-11 flow ϕH1\phi_{H}^{1} of the Hamiltonian vector field. If xx is additionally contractible or we are working with an exact symplectic manifold, then xx is also a critical point of 𝒜H\mathcal{A}_{H}.
In the following, let us abridge

ℒ:=C∞​(𝕊1,W).\mathscr{L}:=C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W)\,\,.

The 𝒜H\mathcal{A}_{H}-gradient flow lines on an interval I⊆ℝI\subseteq\mathbb{R} are maps u:I→ℒu:I\rightarrow\mathscr{L} that, considered as cylinders u:I×𝕊1→Wu:I\times\mathbb{S}^{1}\rightarrow W, are smooth solutions of the Floer equation

(4.3) ∂su+Jt​(u)​(∂tu−XH​(u))=0.\partial_{s}u+J_{t}(u)\,(\partial_{t}u-X_{H}(u))=0\,\,.

(We do not require uu to take on values in the space of contractible loops.) To distinguish between uu as being defined on an interval versus on a cylinder, we also write

us:=u​(s,⋅)∈ℒ.u_{s}:=u(s,\,\cdot\,)\in\mathscr{L}\,\,.

We now make the following Morse-Bott type assumption.

  1. (MB(H,N))

    Let N⊆WN\subseteq W be a closed, connected submanifold so that NN is fibered by periodic Hamiltonian flow lines of HH of minimal period 11. Suppose moreover

    Tp​N=ker⁡(dp​ϕH1−id)∀p∈N.T_{p}N=\ker(d_{p}\phi_{H}^{1}-\mathrm{id})\quad\forall\,p\in N\,\,.
Remark 4.1.

The minimal period assumption can likely be dropped by lifting the charts in Lemma 5.1 to a covering.

One can naturally identify NN with the set of Hamiltonian flow lines starting at points of NN. Define

(4.4) 𝒩:={x∈ℒ|x​(t)=ϕHt​(x​(0)),x​(0)∈N},\displaystyle\mathcal{N}:=\left\{\,x\in\mathscr{L}\,\big|\,x(t)=\phi_{H}^{t}(x(0))\,,\,x(0)\in N\right\}\,\,,

so that 𝒩\mathcal{N} is contained in the zero set of ∇gJ𝒜H\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H}\,. The natural continuous injective map N→ℒ,p↦(ϕHt​(p))tN\rightarrow\mathscr{L}\,,\,\,\,p\mapsto(\phi_{H}^{t}(p))_{t}\,, is a topological embedding onto its image 𝒩\mathcal{N}. (Here and always, ℒ\mathscr{L} carries the C∞C^{\infty}-topology.) Thus 𝒩\mathcal{N} is a compact and connected topological manifold. If 𝒩\mathcal{N} consists of contractible loops or we are in the exact case, then 𝒩⊆Crit​(𝒜H)\mathcal{N}\subseteq\mathrm{Crit}(\mathcal{A}_{H}) and 𝒜H\mathcal{A}_{H} is constant on its connected critical set 𝒩\mathcal{N}\,.

We can now state our second main result.

Theorem C.

Suppose (MB(H,N)) holds. Then for every x0∈𝒩x_{0}\in\mathcal{N} there exists a C∞C^{\infty}-open neighborhood 𝒰⊆ℒ\mathcal{U}\subseteq\mathscr{L} of x0x_{0}\,, a C∞C^{\infty}-continuous function Ξ:𝒰→[0,∞)\Xi:\mathcal{U}\rightarrow[0,\infty) vanishing on 𝒩∩𝒰\mathcal{N}\cap\mathcal{U} and a constant B>0B>0 with the following significance.
For every s0∈ℝs_{0}\in\mathbb{R} and every 𝒜H\mathcal{A}_{H}-gradient flow line u:[s0,∞)→ℒu:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow\mathscr{L} with image contained in 𝒰\mathcal{U} and existing limit (in the C∞C^{\infty}-topology) u∞=lims→∞us∈𝒩u_{\infty}=\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}u_{s}\in\mathcal{N}\,, it holds

|∂su​(s,t)|≤Ξ​(us0)​e−B​(s−s0)∀s≥s0,t∈𝕊1.\displaystyle|\partial_{s}u(s,t)|\leq\Xi(u_{s_{0}})\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\qquad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,,\,t\in\mathbb{S}^{1}\,\,.

The conclusion of the theorem is independent of the Riemannian metric used on WW to measure the norm of ∂su​(s,t)\partial_{s}u(s,t)\,, which is why we did not specify it. The proof of Theorem C will be given in Section 5. As an immediate consequence, we see that condition (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) is satisfied. Let us state this as a separate result.

Corollary 4.2.

Suppose (MB(H,N)) holds.

  1. (a)

    In the general case, assume additionally that the periodic Hamiltonian flow lines in NN are contractible loops. Endow M:=Ccontr∞​(𝕊1,W)M:=C^{\infty}_{\mathrm{contr}}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W) with the C∞C^{\infty}-topology. Set Z:=𝒩Z:=\mathcal{N} and f:=𝒜H:M→ℝf:=\mathcal{A}_{H}:M\rightarrow\mathbb{R} and define G:M→[0,∞)G:M\rightarrow[0,\infty) by G​(x):=|∇gJ𝒜H​(x)|gJ2G(x):=|\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H}(x)|_{g_{J}}^{2}\,. Fix the gradient flow line class, which assigns to a closed interval I⊆ℝI\subseteq\mathbb{R} the set

    {u:I→M|u:I×𝕊1→W​ satisfies (4.3)}.\displaystyle\left\{\,u:I\rightarrow M\,\Big|\,u:I\times\mathbb{S}^{1}\rightarrow W\textit{ satisfies \eqref{eq: Floer eq - Floer illustration}}\right\}\,\,.
  2. (b)

    In the exact case ω=d​λ\omega=d\lambda\,, we can even take M:=ℒ=C∞​(𝕊1,W)M:=\mathscr{L}=C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W). Again, let Z:=𝒩Z:=\mathcal{N} and let f=𝒜H:M→ℝf=\mathcal{A}_{H}:M\rightarrow\mathbb{R} be defined by (4.1). Define G:M→[0,∞)G:M\rightarrow[0,\infty) and the gradient flow line class by the same formulae as above.

Then condition (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) holds for every E0≥0E_{0}\geq 0 and every interval I=[a,∞)I=[a,\infty)\,.

Proof.

It suffices to verify the assumptions of Lemma 2.5. Fix a Riemannian metric on WW with induced distance function dW:W×W→[0,∞)d_{W}:W\times W\rightarrow[0,\infty)\,. Given x0∈𝒩x_{0}\in\mathcal{N}\,, choose an open neighborhood 𝒰⊆M\mathcal{U}\subseteq M of x0x_{0}\,, a continuous function Ξ:𝒰→[0,∞)\Xi:\mathcal{U}\rightarrow[0,\infty) vanishing on 𝒩∩𝒰\mathcal{N}\cap\mathcal{U} and a constant B>0B>0 with the property described in Theorem C. As coarse metric take the sup-metric dC0d_{C^{0}} on MM, defined by

dC0​(x,y):=maxt∈𝕊1⁡dW​(x​(t),y​(t)) for ​x,y∈M.d_{C^{0}}(x,y):=\max_{t\in\mathbb{S}^{1}}\,d_{W}(x(t),y(t))\quad\text{ for }x,y\in M\,\,.

Clearly dC0d_{C^{0}} induces the C0C^{0}-topology on MM, which is coarser than the given C∞C^{\infty}-topology. Now let u:[s0,∞)→Mu:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow M be any gradient flow line with image contained in 𝒰\mathcal{U} and existing limit u∞∈𝒩∩𝒰u_{\infty}\in\mathcal{N}\cap\mathcal{U} (in the C∞C^{\infty}-topology). We can now estimate

dC0​(us0,u∞)≤supt∈𝕊1∫s0∞|∂su​(s,t)|​𝑑s≤Ξ​(us0)​∫s0∞e−B​(s−s0)​𝑑s=Ξ​(us0)B.\displaystyle d_{C^{0}}(u_{s_{0}},u_{\infty})\leq\sup_{t\in\mathbb{S}^{1}}\int_{s_{0}}^{\infty}|\partial_{s}u(s,t)|\,ds\leq\Xi(u_{s_{0}})\int_{s_{0}}^{\infty}e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\,ds=\frac{\Xi(u_{s_{0}})}{B}\,\,.

Thus the function 1B​Ξ:𝒰→[0,∞)\frac{1}{B}\,\Xi:\mathcal{U}\rightarrow[0,\infty) has the required property stated in Lemma 2.5. ∎
