The exponential coefficient B>0B>0 in Theorem C can be chosen uniformly for every loop x0∈𝒩x_{0}\in\mathcal{N}\,, which is relevant if one wants to embed moduli spaces of Floer cylinders into a Banach manifold of Sobolev maps of weighted exponential decay (choosing the weight smaller than a uniform exponential decay coefficient BB), similarly to Theorem 3.8. This can be deduced from abstract nonsense only. To this end, we first state a version of the well-known tube lemma from topology.

Lemma 4.3.

Let XX be a compact topological space. Let 𝐏\mathbf{P} be a predicate taking arguments in (0,∞)×X(0,\infty)\times X and satisfying

  1. (𝐏1\mathbf{P}_{1})

    For all 0<r<R0<r<R and x∈Xx\in X:  𝐏​(R,x)⟹𝐏​(r,x).\mathbf{P}(R,x)\,\,\Longrightarrow\,\,\mathbf{P}(r,x)\,\,.

  2. (𝐏2\mathbf{P}_{2})

    For every x∈Xx\in X there exists a neighborhood U⊆XU\subseteq X of xx and r>0r>0 so that 𝐏​(r,y)\mathbf{P}(r,y) holds for every y∈Uy\in U\,.

Then there exists r0>0r_{0}>0 so that 𝐏​(r,x)\mathbf{P}(r,x) holds for all 0<r≤r00<r\leq r_{0} and x∈Xx\in X\,.

Proof.

For each x∈Xx\in X choose a neighborhood U​(x)⊆XU(x)\subseteq X of xx and r​(x)>0r(x)>0 as in (𝐏2\mathbf{P}_{2}). By compactness, finitely many U​(x1),…,U​(xn)U(x_{1}),\ldots,U(x_{n}) cover XX. Then r0:=min1≤i≤n⁡r​(xi)>0r_{0}:=\min_{1\leq i\leq n}r(x_{i})>0 has the desired property by (𝐏1\mathbf{P}_{1}). ∎

Corollary 4.4.

The exponential decay constant B>0B>0 in Theorem C can be chosen uniformly for every loop x0∈𝒩x_{0}\in\mathcal{N}\,.

Proof.

Apply Lemma 4.3 to the compactum 𝒩\mathcal{N} and the obvious choice of predicate. To verify (𝐏2\mathbf{P}_{2}), observe that the choices (𝒰,Ξ,B)(\mathcal{U},\Xi,B)\,, working for a particular loop x0∈𝒩x_{0}\in\mathcal{N} in Theorem C, also work for every loop x1∈𝒩∩𝒰x_{1}\in\mathcal{N}\cap\mathcal{U}\,. ∎

4.2. Theorem A in the context of Symplectic Homology

We will give an application of Theorem A to Floer cylinders for Hamiltonians appearing naturally in the setting of symplectic homology. We refer to Seidel [10] for an excellent survey on symplectic homology and Bourgeois-Oancea [2] for the analytical details.

Let (W,λ)(W,\lambda) be a compact Liouville domain. This means that λ∈Ω1​(W)\lambda\in\Omega^{1}(W) is the primitive of a symplectic form ω:=d​λ\omega:=d\lambda on WW and that the global Liouville vector field XX on WW, defined by ιX​ω=λ\iota_{X}\omega=\lambda\,, is outward pointing on the boundary Σ:=∂W\Sigma:=\partial W\,. The restriction α:=λ|Σ\alpha:=\lambda|_{\Sigma} gives a contact form on Σ\Sigma whose Reeb vector field we denote by RαR_{\alpha}\,. We endow the completion W^:=W∪Σ[1,∞)×Σ\widehat{W}:=W\,\cup_{\Sigma}\,[1,\infty)\times\Sigma with the one-form

λ^:={λ on ​Wr​α on ​[1,∞)×Σ,\displaystyle\widehat{\lambda}:=\begin{cases}\lambda&\text{ on }W\\ r\alpha&\text{ on }[1,\infty)\times\Sigma\,\,,\end{cases}

where rr designates the coordinate on [1,∞)[1,\infty)\,, and the symplectic form ω^:=d​λ^\widehat{\omega}:=d\widehat{\lambda}\,.

Example 4.5.

An important example of a compact Liouville domain is the unit disk cotangent bundle over a closed Riemannian manifold, equipped with the canonical one-form. Its completion is the whole cotangent bundle.

For every T∈ℝT\in\mathbb{R} let NT⊆ΣN^{T}\subseteq\Sigma denote the fixed point set of the time-TT Reeb flow ϕRαT\phi_{R_{\alpha}}^{T}\,. For T>0T>0 consider the following Morse-Bott type assumption.

  1. (ReebMBT)

    Suppose NT⊆ΣN^{T}\subseteq\Sigma is a closed submanifold, that every point in NTN^{T} has minimal Reeb period TT and that

    Tp​NT=ker⁡(dp​ϕRαT−idTp​Σ)∀p∈NT.T_{p}N^{T}=\ker(d_{p}\phi_{R_{\alpha}}^{T}-\mathrm{id}_{T_{p}\Sigma})\qquad\forall\,p\in N^{T}\,\,.

To understand the Reeb orbits on Σ\Sigma in a certain spectrum of periods, one now studies Hamiltonians H:W^→ℝH:\widehat{W}\rightarrow\mathbb{R} with

  1. (1)

    On [1,∞)×Σ[1,\infty)\times\Sigma, the Hamiltonian HH is of the form H​(r,p)=h​(r)H(r,p)=h(r) for some smooth h:[1,∞)→ℝh:[1,\infty)\rightarrow\mathbb{R} with h′>0,h′′≥0h^{\prime}>0\,,\,h^{\prime\prime}\geq 0 and h′′>0h^{\prime\prime}>0 on [1,r¯)[1,\bar{r}) for some r¯>1\bar{r}>1\,.

  2. (2)

    H|WH|_{W} is a C2C^{2}-small Morse function with h​(1)−h′​(1)≤H≤0h(1)-h^{\prime}(1)\leq H\leq 0 on WW.

This is the usual set-up described in [2, §3] and [10, §3(c)], except the extra condition h​(1)−h′​(1)≤minW⁡Hh(1)-h^{\prime}(1)\leq\min_{W}H\,, which can be easily achieved. Since H|WH|_{W} is C2C^{2}-small, the only 11-periodic Hamiltonian orbits in WW are the critical points of HH. On the cylindrical end, the Hamiltonian vector field is given by XH​(r,p)=h′​(r)​Rα​(p)X_{H}(r,p)=h^{\prime}(r)\,R_{\alpha}(p)\,, so that the Hamiltonian flow is

ϕHt​(r,p)=(r,ϕRαh′​(r)​t​(p)).\displaystyle\phi_{H}^{t}(r,p)=(r,\phi_{R_{\alpha}}^{h^{\prime}(r)t}(p))\,\,.

For r≥1r\geq 1 let T:=h′​(r)T:=h^{\prime}(r)\,. Then we have a bijection between TT-periodic Reeb orbits yy and 11-periodic Hamiltonian orbits (r,y(T⋅))(r,y(T\,\cdot\,))\,. The action value of a critical point (r,y(T⋅))(r,y(T\,\cdot\,)) of the action functional 𝒜H\mathcal{A}_{H}, defined by (4.1), is

𝒜H(r,y(T⋅))=rh′(r)−h(r).\mathcal{A}_{H}(r,y(T\,\cdot\,))=rh^{\prime}(r)-h(r)\,\,.

The action value therefore only depends on rr and moreover is increasing in rr because

dd​r​(r​h′​(r)−h​(r))=r​h′′​(r)\displaystyle\frac{d}{dr}\big(rh^{\prime}(r)-h(r)\big)=rh^{\prime\prime}(r)

is nonnegative and even strictly positive for r<r¯r<\bar{r}\,. Since h′′>0h^{\prime\prime}>0 on [1,r¯)[1,\bar{r})\,, for every T∈[h′​(1),h′​(r¯))T\in[h^{\prime}(1),h^{\prime}(\bar{r})) there exists a uniquely determined r≥1r\geq 1 with h′​(r)=Th^{\prime}(r)=T\,.

Lemma 4.6.

For r∈[1,r¯)r\in[1,\bar{r}) set T:=h′​(r)T:=h^{\prime}(r)\,. If (ReebMBT) holds, then every connected component NN of {r}×NT\{r\}\times N^{T} satisfies condition (MB(H,N)).

Proof.

The argument is analogous to [2, Lemma 3.3]. The differential of ϕH1\phi_{H}^{1} at (r,p)∈{r}×NT(r,p)\in\{r\}\times N^{T} is

d(r,p)​ϕH1=(idℝ0h′′​(r)​Rα​(ϕRαh′​(r)​(p))dp​ϕRαh′​(r))=(idℝ0h′′​(r)​Rα​(p)dp​ϕRαT).\displaystyle d_{(r,p)}\phi_{H}^{1}=\left(\begin{matrix}\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{R}}&0\\ h^{\prime\prime}(r)\,R_{\alpha}(\phi_{R_{\alpha}}^{h^{\prime}(r)}(p))&d_{p}\phi_{R_{\alpha}}^{h^{\prime}(r)}\end{matrix}\right)=\left(\begin{matrix}\mathrm{id}_{\mathbb{R}}&0\\ h^{\prime\prime}(r)\,R_{\alpha}(p)&d_{p}\phi_{R_{\alpha}}^{T}\end{matrix}\right)\,\,.

Since h′′​(r)>0h^{\prime\prime}(r)>0\,, the vector ∂r\partial_{r} is not an eigenvector for eigenvalue 11. Keeping in mind that Rα​(p)R_{\alpha}(p) however is and that ξp=ker⁡(αp)\xi_{p}=\ker(\alpha_{p}) and span​{∂r,Rα​(p)}\mathrm{span}\{\partial_{r},\,R_{\alpha}(p)\} are invariant under d(r,p)​ϕH1d_{(r,p)}\phi_{H}^{1}\,, we conclude that the 11-eigenspace of d(r,p)​ϕH1d_{(r,p)}\phi_{H}^{1} is contained in Tp​ΣT_{p}\Sigma\,. By assumption (ReebMBT), it thus equals Tp​NT=T(r,p)​NT_{p}N^{T}=T_{(r,p)}N\,. ∎

Lemma 4.7.

For T>0T>0 suppose (ReebMBT) holds. Then TT is isolated in the spectrum

Spec​(Σ,α):={τ>0|there exists a ​τ​-periodic Reeb orbit}.\displaystyle\mathrm{Spec}(\Sigma,\alpha):=\left\{\,\tau>0\,\big|\,\text{there exists a }\tau\text{-periodic Reeb orbit}\right\}\,\,.
Proof.

This is well-known. See [5, Thm. 23] for a proof. ∎

Next we relate the above setting to the setting of our compactness theorem. We endow M:=C∞​(𝕊1,W^)M:=C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\widehat{W}) with the C∞C^{\infty}-topology. Consider a fixed T0∈(h′​(1),h′​(r¯))T_{0}\in(h^{\prime}(1),h^{\prime}(\bar{r})) for which (ReebMBT0{}_{T_{0}}) holds. Let r0∈(1,r¯)r_{0}\in(1,\bar{r}) be determined by h′​(r0)=T0h^{\prime}(r_{0})=T_{0}\,. Analogous to (4.4), we now define

Z:=\displaystyle Z:= {x∈C∞​(𝕊1,W^)|x​(t)=ϕHt​(x​(0)),x​(0)∈{r0}×NT0}\displaystyle\left\{\,x\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\widehat{W})\,\Big|\,x(t)=\phi_{H}^{t}(x(0))\,,\,x(0)\in\{r_{0}\}\times N^{T_{0}}\right\}
=\displaystyle= {(r0,y(T0⋅))|y is a T0-periodic Reeb orbit}.\displaystyle\left\{\,(r_{0},y(T_{0}\,\cdot\,))\,\Big|\,y\text{ is a }T_{0}\text{-periodic Reeb orbit}\right\}\,\,.

Then ZZ is homeomorphic to NT0N^{T_{0}} and in particular compact. Let f:=𝒜H:M→ℝf:=\mathcal{A}_{H}:M\rightarrow\mathbb{R} be the action functional, defined in (4.1), for primitive λ^\widehat{\lambda}\,. We have seen above that f=𝒜Hf=\mathcal{A}_{H} has constant action value ζ:=r0​h′​(r0)−h​(r0)\zeta:=r_{0}h^{\prime}(r_{0})-h(r_{0}) on ZZ\,. Fix a one-periodic family J=(Jt)t∈𝕊1J=(J_{t})_{t\in\mathbb{S}^{1}} of ω^\widehat{\omega}-compatible almost complex structures on W^\widehat{W}\,, which is of SFT-type222“SFT” stands for “Symplectic Field Theory” on the cylindrical end [r¯,∞)×Σ[\bar{r},\infty)\times\Sigma\,, that is

  1. (1)

    J|[r¯,∞)×ΣJ|_{[\bar{r},\infty)\times\Sigma} is tt-independent and invariant under the map (r,p)↦(c​r,p)(r,p)\mapsto(c\,r,p) for every c≥1c\geq 1\,,

  2. (2)

    J|[r¯,∞)×ΣJ|_{[\bar{r},\infty)\times\Sigma} leaves ξ=ker⁡(α)\xi=\ker(\alpha) invariant and J|ξJ|_{\xi} is d​α|ξd\alpha|_{\xi}-compatible,

  3. (3)

    J​(r​∂r)=RαJ(r\partial_{r})=R_{\alpha}\,.

As before, let G:M→[0,∞)G:M\rightarrow[0,\infty) be defined by G:=|∇gJ𝒜H|gJ2G:=|\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H}|_{g_{J}}^{2}\,. Consider the class Γ\Gamma of (f,G)(f,G)-gradient flow lines, given by

ℝ⊇I⟼Γ(I):={u:I→M|u:I×𝕊1→W^​ solves (4.3) andu​(I×𝕊1)⊆W^​(r¯):=W∪Σ[1,r¯]×Σ}.\displaystyle\mathbb{R}\supseteq I\longmapsto\Gamma(I):=\left\{\,u:I\rightarrow M\,\Big|\,\begin{array}[]{c}u:I\times\mathbb{S}^{1}\rightarrow\widehat{W}\text{ solves \eqref{eq: Floer eq - Floer illustration} and}\\ u(I\times\mathbb{S}^{1})\subseteq\widehat{W}({\bar{r}}):=W\,\cup_{\Sigma}\,[1,\bar{r}]\times\Sigma\end{array}\right\}\,\,.
Lemma 4.8.

Under assumption (ReebMBT0{}_{T_{0}}) and with the above choices it holds:

  1. (a)

    The spectral gap 𝔖f,G+​(Z)\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z) is strictly positive. In fact even

    0<𝔖f,G(Z):=inf{|ζ−f(x)||x∈G−1​(0)\Z}≤𝔖f,G+(Z).\displaystyle 0<\mathfrak{S}_{f,G}(Z):=\inf\left\{\,|\zeta-f(x)|\,\Big|\,\begin{array}[]{c}x\in G^{-1}(0)\backslash Z\end{array}\right\}\leq\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z)\,\,.

Let E0≥0E_{0}\geq 0 and the interval I=[s0,∞)I=[s_{0},\infty) be arbitrary.

  1. (b)

    Condition (A1’(E0,I,I1){}_{(E_{0},I,I_{1})}) holds for every interval I1=[s1,∞)I_{1}=[s_{1},\infty) with s0<s1s_{0}<s_{1}\,.

  2. (c)

    Condition (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) holds.

Proof.

We prove (a). For p∈Crit​(H|W)p\in\mathrm{Crit}(H|_{W})\,, the action value of the loop sitting constantly at pp is

−H​(p)≤h′​(1)−h​(1)≤r​h′​(r)−h​(r)∀r≥1.-H(p)\leq h^{\prime}(1)-h(1)\leq rh^{\prime}(r)-h(r)\qquad\forall\,r\geq 1\,\,.

As mentioned above, the action value at a 11-periodic Hamiltonian orbit in {r}×Σ\{r\}\times\Sigma is r​h′​(r)−h​(r)rh^{\prime}(r)-h(r)\,, which is increasing in rr and even strictly for 1≤r<r¯1\leq r<\bar{r}\,. Now (a) follows since T0T_{0} is isolated in the Reeb spectrum (Lemma 4.7), so that r0r_{0} is isolated in the set of r≥1r\geq 1 for which there exists a 11-periodic Hamiltonian orbit in {r}×Σ\{r\}\times\Sigma\,.
Part (b) follows from a standard Gromov compactness argument. In more detail, given a sequence of Floer cylinders un:[s0,∞)×𝕊1→W^u_{n}:[s_{0},\infty)\times\mathbb{S}^{1}\rightarrow\widehat{W} with energy bounded uniformly by E0E_{0} and image contained in the compactum W^​(r¯)\widehat{W}(\bar{r})\,. Then, for arbitrary ε>0\varepsilon>0\,, the derivatives of the unu_{n} are uniformly bounded on [s0+ε,∞)×𝕊1[s_{0}+\varepsilon,\infty)\times\mathbb{S}^{1}\,. (For otherwise one could construct a nonconstant JJ-holomorphic sphere bubbling off, contradicting the fact that ω^=d​λ^\widehat{\omega}=d\widehat{\lambda} is exact.) By elliptic regularity, a subsequence of (un)n(u_{n})_{n} converges in Cloc∞​([s1,∞)×𝕊1,W^)C^{\infty}_{\mathrm{loc}}([s_{1},\infty)\times\mathbb{S}^{1},\widehat{W}) to a Floer cylinder, whose image must again be contained in W^​(r¯)\widehat{W}(\bar{r})\,. This shows (b).
Lastly, part (c) follows from Corollary 4.2 (b), using that (MB(H,N)) holds for every connected component NN of {r0}×NT0\{r_{0}\}\times N^{T_{0}} due to Lemma 4.6. ∎

Theorem 4.9.

For T0∈(h′​(1),h′​(r¯))T_{0}\in(h^{\prime}(1),h^{\prime}(\bar{r})) assume (ReebMBT0{}_{T_{0}}) and let r0∈(1,r¯)r_{0}\in(1,\bar{r}) be determined by h′​(r0)=T0h^{\prime}(r_{0})=T_{0}\,. Given 0≤E0<𝔖f,G+​(Z)0\leq E_{0}<\mathfrak{S}^{+}_{f,G}(Z) and s0<s1s_{0}<s_{1} as well as a sequence un:[s0,∞)×𝕊1→W^u_{n}:[s_{0},\infty)\times\mathbb{S}^{1}\rightarrow\widehat{W} of Floer cylinders with

  1. (1)

    energy E​(un)=∫s0∞∫01|∂sun|Jt2​𝑑t​𝑑sE(u_{n})=\int_{s_{0}}^{\infty}\int_{0}^{1}|\partial_{s}u_{n}|_{J_{t}}^{2}\,dt\,ds uniformly bounded by E0E_{0}\,,

  2. (2)

    un​(s0,⋅)⊆W^​(r¯)=W∪Σ[1,r¯]×Σu_{n}(s_{0},\,\cdot\,)\subseteq\widehat{W}(\bar{r})=W\,\cup_{\Sigma}\,[1,\bar{r}]\times\Sigma and

  3. (3)

    (un)(s,⋅)→(r0,yn(T0⋅))(u_{n})(s,\,\cdot\,)\rightarrow(r_{0},y_{n}(T_{0}\,\cdot\,)) uniformly as s→∞s\rightarrow\infty\,, where yny_{n} is a T0T_{0}-periodic Reeb orbit.

Then a subsequence of (un)n(u_{n})_{n} converges in C∞​([s1,∞)×𝕊1,W^)C^{\infty}([s_{1},\infty)\times\mathbb{S}^{1},\widehat{W}) to a Floer cylinder uu with image contained in W^​(r¯)\widehat{W}(\bar{r}) and for which u​(s,⋅)u(s,\,\cdot\,) converges to some (r0,y(T0⋅))(r_{0},y(T_{0}\,\cdot\,)) as s→∞s\rightarrow\infty uniformly with all derivatives, where yy is a T0T_{0}-periodic Reeb orbit.

Proof.

Lemma 4.8 shows that assumptions (A1’(E0,I,I1){}_{(E_{0},I,I_{1})}) and (A2(E0,I){}_{(E_{0},I)}) in Theorem 2.12 (the generalization of Theorem A) are satisfied with I:=[s0,∞)I:=[s_{0},\infty) and I1:=[s1,∞)I_{1}:=[s_{1},\infty)\,.

Claim.

The unu_{n} are elements of the moduli space ℳ(E0,I)​(Γ,Z)\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},\,I)}(\Gamma,Z)\,, defined in (1.4).

Proof of the claim.

Because JJ is of SFT-type on [r¯,∞)×Σ[\bar{r},\infty)\times\Sigma, for any Floer cylinder uu the function ρ:=r∘u\rho:=r\circ u satisfies

Δ​ρ+ρ​h′′​(ρ)​∂sρ=|∂su|J2≥0 on ​u−1​((r¯,∞)×Σ),\Delta\rho+\rho\,h^{\prime\prime}(\rho)\,\partial_{s}\rho=|\partial_{s}u|^{2}_{J}\geq 0\quad\text{ on }u^{-1}((\bar{r},\infty)\times\Sigma)\,\,,

see the computation in [10, §(3c)]. Thus ρ\rho obeys a maximum principle and attains no interior local maximum on each nonconstant component of u−1​((r¯,∞)×Σ)u^{-1}((\bar{r},\infty)\times\Sigma). Using this and that the unu_{n} have asymptotics in W^​(r¯)\widehat{W}(\bar{r}) by (2) and (3), we conclude that the unu_{n} are confined to W^​(r¯)\widehat{W}(\bar{r}) and hence un∈Γ​(I)u_{n}\in\Gamma(I)\,. Moreover, the convergence un(s,⋅)→(r0,yn(T0⋅)),s→∞u_{n}(s,\,\cdot\,)\rightarrow(r_{0},\,y_{n}(T_{0}\,\cdot\,))\,,\,s\rightarrow\infty\,, in (3) even holds in the C∞​(𝕊1,W^)C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\widehat{W})-topology. Indeed, for every sequence (s¯k)k(\bar{s}_{k})_{k} tending to infinity, a subsequence of (un(⋅+s¯k,⋅))k(u_{n}(\,\cdot\,+\bar{s}_{k},\,\cdot\,))_{k} converges in Cloc∞​([s0,∞)×𝕊1,W^)C^{\infty}_{\mathrm{loc}}([s_{0},\infty)\times\mathbb{S}^{1},\widehat{W}) by Gromov compactness and its limit must be (s,t)↦(r0,yn​(T0​t))(s,t)\mapsto(r_{0},\,y_{n}(T_{0}t)) by (3). This shows that the unu_{n} are elements of ℳ(E0,I)​(Γ,Z)\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},\,I)}(\Gamma,Z)\,. ∎

We can therefore apply Theorem 2.12 and extract a subsequence (suppressed in the notation) of (un)n(u_{n})_{n} converging in C0​(I1,M)C^{0}(I_{1},M) to some u∈ℳ(E0,I1)​(Γ,Z)u\in\mathcal{M}_{(E_{0},\,I_{1})}(\Gamma,Z)\,. The convergence in

C0​(I1,M)=C0​([s1,∞),C∞​(𝕊1,W^))C^{0}(I_{1},M)=C^{0}([s_{1},\infty),\,C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\widehat{W}))

and the Floer equation (4.3) then imply that the unu_{n} converge to uu in C∞​([s1,∞)×𝕊1,W^)C^{\infty}([s_{1},\infty)\times\mathbb{S}^{1},\widehat{W})\,. ∎

5. Proof of Theorem C

The proof of Theorem C below is a parametrized version of a classic exponential decay estimate in the Morse-Bott setting. We follow Bourgeois-Oancea [2, App. A] regarding the classic decay estimate. In addition, we adopt some arguments presented in Fauck’s thesis [5, Ch. 2.2], although it is concerned with the Rabinowitz action functional instead of the classical action functional.

5.1. Preliminaries

In the following, we always work in the setting of Subsection 4.1. For a given closed, connected submanifold N⊆WN\subseteq W we assume (MB(H,N)) and define 𝒩⊆ℒ=C∞​(𝕊1,W)\mathcal{N}\subseteq\mathscr{L}=C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W) by (4.4). We abbreviate

2​n=dim(W)andd:=dim(N).\displaystyle 2n=\dim(W)\quad\text{and}\quad d:=\dim(N)\,\,.

We fix, once and for all, a loop x0∈𝒩x_{0}\in\mathcal{N} for which we need to find a C∞C^{\infty}-open neighborhood 𝒰=𝒰​(x0)⊆ℒ\mathcal{U}=\mathcal{U}(x_{0})\subseteq\mathscr{L}, a C∞C^{\infty}-continuous function Ξ:𝒰→[0,∞)\Xi:\mathcal{U}\rightarrow[0,\infty) vanishing on 𝒩∩𝒰\mathcal{N}\cap\mathcal{U} and a constant B>0B>0 with the significance described in Theorem C.

Lemma 5.1.

There exists a tubular neighborhood U⊆WU\subseteq W of x0x_{0} and coordinates

(ϑ,z)=(ϑ,z′,z′′):U→𝕊1×ℝd−1×ℝ2​n−d(\vartheta,z)=(\vartheta,z^{\prime},z^{\prime\prime}):\,U\rightarrow\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{d-1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-d}

so that U∩N={z′′=0}U\cap N=\{z^{\prime\prime}=0\} and ∂ϑ=XH\partial_{\vartheta}=X_{H} on U∩NU\cap N as well as (ϑ,z)∘x0​(t)=(t,0)(\vartheta,z)\circ x_{0}(t)=(t,0)\,.

Observe that, for a tubular neighborhood UU as above, the set U∩NU\cap N is invariant under the Hamiltonian flow ϕHt\phi_{H}^{t}\,. This lemma is the only time we use that the Hamiltonian orbits on NN have minimal period 11.

Proof.

We follow the argument in [5, Lemma 29]. The 𝕊1\mathbb{S}^{1}-action of the Hamiltonian flow on NN is free by assumption (MB(H,N)), so the slice theorem (see [3, Thm. 23.5]) gives 𝕊1\mathbb{S}^{1}-equivariant coordinates (ϑ,z′)(\vartheta,z^{\prime}) on a neighborhood UN⊆NU_{N}\subseteq N of x0x_{0}\,. Now the normal bundle of NN in WW is trivial over UNU_{N}\,,333UN≅𝕊1×ℝd−1U_{N}\cong\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{d-1} is homotopy equivalent to a circle and the normal bundle T​W|UN/T​UNTW|_{U_{N}}/TU_{N} is orientable since T​W|UN≅T​UN⊕(T​W|UN/T​UN)TW|_{U_{N}}\cong TU_{N}\oplus(TW|_{U_{N}}/TU_{N}) and T​UNTU_{N} are respectively. so a tubular neighborhood of UNU_{N} in WW yields globally defined coordinates z′′z^{\prime\prime}\,. ∎

We now fix a tubular neighborhood UU and coordinates (ϑ,z)=(ϑ,z′,z′′)(\vartheta,z)=(\vartheta,z^{\prime},z^{\prime\prime}) as in the above lemma. In these coordinates

{x∈𝒩|x(0)∈U}={x∈C∞(𝕊1,U)|x​(t)=(ϑ~+t,z~′,0)​ for some(ϑ~,z~′,0)∈𝕊1×ℝd−1×ℝ2​n−d}.\left\{\,x\in\mathcal{N}\,\Big|\,x(0)\in U\right\}=\left\{\,x\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},U)\,\Big|\,\begin{array}[]{c}x(t)=(\widetilde{\vartheta}+t,\widetilde{z}\,^{\prime}\,,0)\text{ for some}\\ (\widetilde{\vartheta}\,,\widetilde{z}\,^{\prime}\,,0)\in\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{d-1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-d}\end{array}\right\}\,\,.

We now define the shift operators

𝗌𝗁±:C0​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)→C0​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)\displaystyle\mathsf{sh}^{\pm}:\,C^{0}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})\rightarrow C^{0}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})
(𝗌𝗁±​(x))​(t):=x​(t)±(t,0) for ​t∈𝕊1.\displaystyle(\mathsf{sh}^{\pm}(x))(t):=x(t)\pm(t,0)\quad\text{ for }t\in\mathbb{S}^{1}\,\,.

Clearly 𝗌𝗁−\mathsf{sh}^{-} is the inverse to 𝗌𝗁+\mathsf{sh}^{+} and x¯0:=𝗌𝗁−​(x0)\bar{x}_{0}:=\mathsf{sh}^{-}(x_{0}) is the constant loop sitting at (0,0)∈𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1(0,0)\in\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1}\,. In view of the above, moreover

(5.1) 𝗌𝗁−​({x∈𝒩|x​(0)∈U})={x∈C∞​(𝕊1,U)|x​ constant and ​z′′​(x)≡0}.\displaystyle\mathsf{sh}^{-}\Big(\left\{\,x\in\mathcal{N}\,\Big|\,x(0)\in U\right\}\Big)=\left\{\,x\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},U)\,\big|\,x\text{ constant and }z^{\prime\prime}(x)\equiv 0\right\}\,\,.

Most statements below only make sense for loops in a sufficiently small neighborhood 𝒰​(x0)⊆C∞​(𝕊1,W)\mathcal{U}(x_{0})\subseteq C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W) of x0x_{0}. When making such a statement, we tacitly assume our loops to be sufficiently close to x0x_{0}\,. To begin with, we choose 𝒰​(x0)\mathcal{U}(x_{0}) small enough so that every x∈𝒰​(x0)x\in\mathcal{U}(x_{0}) has image x​(𝕊1)x(\mathbb{S}^{1}) contained in the tubular neighborhood U⊆WU\subseteq W of x0x_{0}\,. In the following we will choose successively smaller neighborhoods of x0x_{0} but, to not clutter notation, we will denote them all by 𝒰​(x0)\mathcal{U}(x_{0}). The final and smallest choice of 𝒰​(x0)\mathcal{U}(x_{0}) will then have the desired property stated in Theorem C.

Remark 5.2.

Every loop x∈C0​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)x\in C^{0}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1}) sufficiently C0C^{0}-close to x¯0=𝗌𝗁−​(x0)≡(0,0)\bar{x}_{0}=\mathsf{sh}^{-}(x_{0})\equiv(0,0) can be lifted to a loop C0​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)C^{0}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) close to the constant loop at the origin in ℝ2​n\mathbb{R}^{2n}. We choose 𝒰​(x0)\mathcal{U}(x_{0}) small enough so that, for every x∈𝒰​(x0)x\in\mathcal{U}(x_{0}), the shift 𝗌𝗁−​(x)\mathsf{sh}^{-}(x) can be lifted to a loop in C0​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)C^{0}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) close to the origin. Abusing notation, we will denote a loop in C0​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)C^{0}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1}) and its lift by the same symbol.

For every cylinder u:[s0,∞)→ℒu:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow\mathscr{L} with image in 𝒰​(x0)\mathcal{U}(x_{0}), we define its shift u¯:[s0,∞)→ℒ\bar{u}:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow\mathscr{L} by u¯s:=𝗌𝗁−​(us),s≥s0\bar{u}_{s}:=\mathsf{sh}^{-}(u_{s})\,,\,s\geq s_{0}\,. Observe that z​(u¯)=z​(u)z(\bar{u})=z(u) and moreover ∂su¯=∂su\partial_{s}\bar{u}=\partial_{s}u and ∂tu¯=∂tu−∂ϑ\partial_{t}\bar{u}=\partial_{t}u-\partial_{\vartheta}\,. Since ∂ϑ−XH=0\partial_{\vartheta}-X_{H}=0 on {z′′=0}\{z^{\prime\prime}=0\}\,, by Hadamard’s lemma (Lemma A.1) we can write

∂ϑ−XH​(ϑ,z)=S​(ϑ,z)⋅z′′∀(ϑ,z)=(ϑ,z′,z′′)∈𝕊1×ℝd−1×ℝ2​n−d\partial_{\vartheta}-X_{H}(\vartheta,z)=S(\vartheta,z)\cdot z^{\prime\prime}\qquad\forall\,(\vartheta,z)=(\vartheta,z^{\prime},z^{\prime\prime})\in\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{d-1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-d}

with a smooth matrix-valued function S:𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1→ℝ2​n×(2​n−d)S:\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{2n\times(2n-d)}\,. Then uu is a solution of the Floer equation (4.3) iff

(5.2) ∂su¯+Jt​(u)​(∂tu¯+S​(u)⋅z′′​(u¯))=0.\partial_{s}\bar{u}+J_{t}(u)\,\big(\partial_{t}\bar{u}+S(u)\cdot z^{\prime\prime}(\bar{u})\big)=0\,\,.

We write

Hk:=Hk​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n),k≥0andL2:=L2​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)=H0H^{k}:=H^{k}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n})\,,\,k\geq 0\quad\text{and}\quad L^{2}:=L^{2}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n})=H^{0}

for the Sobolev spaces. Abbreviating J0​(t):=Jt​(x0​(t))J_{0}(t):=J_{t}(x_{0}(t)) and gt:=ωx0​(t)(⋅,J0(t)⋅)g_{t}:=\omega_{x_{0}(t)}(\,\cdot\,,J_{0}(t)\,\cdot\,)\,, we endow HkH^{k} with the inner product induced by (gt)t(g_{t})_{t}\,, that is

(5.3) ⟨X1,X2⟩L2:=∫01gt​(X1​(t),X2​(t))​𝑑tand⟨X1,X2⟩Hk:=∑j=0k⟨∂tjX1,∂tjX2⟩L2.\displaystyle\langle X_{1},X_{2}\rangle_{L^{2}}:=\int_{0}^{1}g_{t}(X_{1}(t),X_{2}(t))\,dt\quad\text{and}\quad\langle X_{1},X_{2}\rangle_{H^{k}}:=\sum_{j=0}^{k}\langle\partial_{t}^{j}X_{1},\,\partial_{t}^{j}X_{2}\rangle_{L^{2}}\,\,.

These inner products induce norms that are equivalent to the standard norms on HkH^{k}\,, induced by the Euclidean inner product on ℝ2​n\mathbb{R}^{2n} instead of (gt)t(g_{t})_{t}\,. Notice that ⟨⋅,⋅⟩L2=(gJ)x0\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{L^{2}}=(g_{J})_{x_{0}}\,, the metric gJg_{J} being introduced in (4.2).

For every loop x∈Hk​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)x\in H^{k}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1}) we define the bounded linear operator

(5.4) 𝖠x:=𝖠x(k):Hk→Hk−1,𝖠x​X:=−Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(x))​(∂tX+S​(𝗌𝗁+​(x))⋅z′′​(X)),\displaystyle\mathsf{A}_{x}:=\mathsf{A}_{x}^{(k)}:H^{k}\rightarrow H^{k-1}\,,\,\quad\mathsf{A}_{x}\,X:=-J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x))\,\big(\partial_{t}X+S(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x))\cdot z^{\prime\prime}(X)\big)\,\,,

where z′′:ℝ2​n→ℝ2​n−dz^{\prime\prime}:\mathbb{R}^{2n}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{2n-d} denotes the projection to the last coordinates. Clearly 𝖠x(ℓ)=𝖠x(k)|Hℓ\mathsf{A}_{x}^{(\ell)}=\mathsf{A}_{x}^{(k)}|_{H^{\ell}} for k≤ℓk\leq\ell and x∈Hℓ​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)x\in H^{\ell}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})\,, which is why we drop the superscript kk whenever the precise domain of 𝖠x\mathsf{A}_{x} is irrelevant or clear from the context. Consistent with the previous notation, we also abbreviate S0​(t):=S​(x0​(t))S_{0}(t):=S(x_{0}(t))\,, so that the operator 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}} at x¯0≡(0,0)\bar{x}_{0}\equiv(0,0) can be written as

(5.5) (𝖠x¯0​X)​(t)=−J0​(t)​(∂tX​(t)+S0​(t)⋅z′′​(X​(t))).\displaystyle(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\,X)(t)=-J_{0}(t)\,\Big(\partial_{t}X(t)+S_{0}(t)\cdot z^{\prime\prime}(X(t))\Big)\,\,.
Lemma 5.3.

Denote by ℒ​(Hk,Hk−1)\mathcal{L}(H^{k},H^{k-1}) the space of bounded linear operators. For every 1≤k≤ℓ1\leq k\leq\ell the map

𝖠:Hℓ​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)→ℒ​(Hk,Hk−1),𝖠​(x):=𝖠x\displaystyle\mathsf{A}:H^{\ell}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(H^{k},H^{k-1})\,,\,\quad\mathsf{A}(x):=\mathsf{A}_{x}

is smooth.

Proof.

The inclusion Hℓ​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)↪Hk​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)H^{\ell}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})\hookrightarrow H^{k}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1}) is smooth, so it suffices to prove the case k=ℓk=\ell\,. The map

β\displaystyle\beta :(Hk​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n×2​n)⊕Hk​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n×(2​n−d)))×Hk→Hk−1,\displaystyle:\,\Big(H^{k}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n\times 2n})\oplus H^{k}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n\times(2n-d)})\Big)\times H^{k}\rightarrow H^{k-1}\,,\,
β​((R1,R2),X):=R1⋅∂tX+R2⋅z′′​(X)\displaystyle\beta\left((R_{1},R_{2})\,,\,X\right):=R_{1}\cdot\partial_{t}X+R_{2}\cdot z^{\prime\prime}(X)

is a continuous bilinear map. Moreover, one readily checks that

Θ:Hk​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)→Hk​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n×2​n)×Hk​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n×(2​n−d)),\displaystyle\Theta:H^{k}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})\rightarrow H^{k}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n\times 2n})\times H^{k}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n\times(2n-d)})\,,\,
Θ​(x):=(−Jt​(x),−Jt​(x)⋅S​(x))\displaystyle\Theta(x):=(-J_{t}(x)\,,\,-J_{t}(x)\cdot S(x))

and

𝗌𝗁+:Hk​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)→Hk​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)\mathsf{sh}^{+}:H^{k}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})\rightarrow H^{k}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})

are smooth. The lemma follows since 𝖠x=β​(Θ∘𝗌𝗁+​(x),⋅)\mathsf{A}_{x}=\beta(\Theta\circ\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x)\,,\,\cdot\,)\,. ∎

The operators 𝖠x\mathsf{A}_{x} allow us to write the Floer equation very succinctly. Given a cylinder u:[s0,∞)→ℒu:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow\mathscr{L} with image in 𝒰​(x0)\mathcal{U}(x_{0}) and shift u¯=𝗌𝗁−​(u)\bar{u}=\mathsf{sh}^{-}(u)\,, the cylinder uu is a solution to the Floer equation (4.3) iff (5.2) holds iff ∂su¯​(s,⋅)=𝖠u¯s​u¯s\partial_{s}\bar{u}(s,\,\cdot\,)=\mathsf{A}_{\bar{u}_{s}}\,\bar{u}_{s} for all s≥s0s\geq s_{0} iff

(5.6) d​u¯sd​s|s=𝖠u¯s​u¯s∀s≥s0,\frac{d\bar{u}_{s}}{ds}\Big|_{s}=\mathsf{A}_{\bar{u}_{s}}\,\bar{u}_{s}\qquad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,,

where on the left-hand side the derivative of s↦uss\mapsto u_{s} in HkH^{k}\,, for any choice of kk, is taken. Notice that, although u¯s\bar{u}_{s} is an element of Hk​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)H^{k}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})\,, we can still plug it (or rather its lift) into 𝖠u¯s\mathsf{A}_{\bar{u}_{s}} (see Remark 5.2). We now collect some decisive facts about the operators 𝖠x\mathsf{A}_{x}\,.

  1. (Fact 1)

    In the fixed coordinates (ϑ,z)(\vartheta,z), the operator 𝖠x¯0=𝖠x¯0(1)\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}=\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}^{(1)} equals the covariant Hessian ∇2𝒜H​(x0)\nabla^{2}\mathcal{A}_{H}(x_{0}) at x0x_{0}\,.444i.e. the linearization of ∇gJ𝒜H\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H} at its zero x0x_{0} Therefore 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}} is an unbounded selfadjoint operator on L2L^{2} for the inner product (gJ)x0=⟨⋅,⋅⟩L2(g_{J})_{x_{0}}=\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{L^{2}}\,.

  2. (Fact 2)

    Inspecting (5.4), for every loop xx the kernel of 𝖠x\mathsf{A}_{x} contains all constant loops XX with z′′∘X≡0z^{\prime\prime}\circ X\equiv 0\,. Moreover, by the Morse-Bott assumption (MB(H,N)), these are all of the elements in the kernel of 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\,, that is

    ker⁡𝖠x¯0={X∈C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)|∂tX≡0​ and ​z′′​(X)≡0}.\displaystyle\ker\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}=\left\{\,X\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n})\,\big|\,\partial_{t}X\equiv 0\text{ and }z^{\prime\prime}(X)\equiv 0\right\}\,\,.

    In particular the kernel of 𝖠x¯0=𝖠x¯0(k)\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}=\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}^{(k)} is dd-dimensional and independent of kk.

  3. (Fact 3)

    𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}} is an elliptic linear partial differential operator of order 11 that is moreover selfadjoint by (Fact 1). Hence, for every k≥0k\geq 0\,, we have an L2L^{2}-orthogonal splitting of HkH^{k}-closed subspaces555This is the Hodge decomposition of 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\,, see [7, Cor. 10.4.10].

    (5.7) Hk=ker⁡(𝖠x¯0)⊕𝖠x¯0​(Hk+1).H^{k}=\ker(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}})\oplus\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{k+1})\,\,.

    Since ker⁡(𝖠x¯0)\ker(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}) consists of constant loops only, the above splitting also is HkH^{k}-orthogonal.

Although standard, as service to the reader we prove (Fact 1) as Lemma A.3 and the assertion about ker⁡(𝖠x¯0)\ker(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}) in (Fact 2) as Lemma A.4 in the appendix.
Denote by 𝖯=𝖯(k):Hk→ker⁡(𝖠x¯0)\mathsf{P}=\mathsf{P}^{(k)}:H^{k}\rightarrow\ker(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}) and 𝖰=𝖰(k):Hk→𝖠x¯0​(Hk+1)\mathsf{Q}=\mathsf{Q}^{(k)}:H^{k}\rightarrow\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{k+1}) the projections with respect to the splitting (5.7). They are orthogonal projections, so

(5.8) ∥X∥Hk2=∥𝖯​X∥Hk2+∥𝖰​X∥Hk2≥∥𝖰​X∥Hk2∀X∈Hk.\lVert X\rVert_{H^{k}}^{2}=\lVert\mathsf{P}X\rVert_{H^{k}}^{2}+\lVert\mathsf{Q}X\rVert_{H^{k}}^{2}\geq\lVert\mathsf{Q}X\rVert_{H^{k}}^{2}\qquad\forall\,X\in H^{k}\,\,.

It holds 𝖰(ℓ)=𝖰(k)|Hℓ\mathsf{Q}^{(\ell)}=\mathsf{Q}^{(k)}|_{H^{\ell}} and 𝖯(ℓ)=𝖯(k)|Hℓ\mathsf{P}^{(\ell)}=\mathsf{P}^{(k)}|_{H^{\ell}} for k≤ℓk\leq\ell because ker⁡(𝖠x¯0(k))=ker⁡(𝖠x¯0(ℓ))\ker(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}^{(k)})=\ker(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}^{(\ell)}) and 𝖠x¯0​(Hℓ+1)⊆𝖠x¯0​(Hk+1)\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{\ell+1})\subseteq\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{k+1})\,. For this reason we often omit the superscript. The following algebraic relations are direct consequences from the definitions and above facts.

  1. (Fact 4)

    𝖰​𝖠x¯0=𝖠x¯0\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}=\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}} or more precisely 𝖰(k)​𝖠x¯0(k+1)=𝖠x¯0(k+1)\mathsf{Q}^{(k)}\,\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}^{(k+1)}=\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}^{(k+1)}\,.

  2. (Fact 5)

    𝖠x​𝖰=𝖠x\mathsf{A}_{x}\,\mathsf{Q}=\mathsf{A}_{x} for every loop xx. Indeed, 𝖯​X∈ker⁡(𝖠x¯0)⊆ker⁡(𝖠x)\mathsf{P}X\in\ker(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}})\subseteq\ker(\mathsf{A}_{x}) by (Fact 2).

  3. (Fact 6)

    ∂t𝖰=∂t\partial_{t}\,\mathsf{Q}=\partial_{t} since ker⁡(𝖠x¯0)\ker(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}) consists of constant loops only.

  4. (Fact 7)

    The bounded operator

    𝖰​𝖠x¯0=𝖠x¯0:(𝖠x¯0​(Hk+2),∥⋅∥Hk+1)→(𝖠x¯0​(Hk+1),∥⋅∥Hk)\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}=\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}:\,(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{k+2}),\lVert\,\cdot\,\rVert_{H^{k+1}})\rightarrow(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{k+1}),\lVert\,\cdot\,\rVert_{H^{k}})

    is bijective and hence invertible. Consequently, defining the constant c​(k):=∥(𝖰​𝖠x¯0)−1∥−2c(k):=\lVert(\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}})^{-1}\rVert^{-2}\,, we have

    ∥𝖠x¯0​𝖰​X∥Hk2=∥𝖰​𝖠x¯0​𝖰​X∥Hk2≥c​(k)​∥𝖰​X∥Hk+12∀X∈Hk+1.\displaystyle\lVert\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\,\mathsf{Q}\,X\rVert_{H^{k}}^{2}=\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\,\mathsf{Q}\,X\rVert_{H^{k}}^{2}\geq c(k)\,\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,X\rVert_{H^{k+1}}^{2}\qquad\forall\,X\in H^{k+1}\,\,.
Definition 5.4.

A map Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) is called smooth cylinder if the induced map Z:[s0,∞)×𝕊1→ℝ2​nZ:[s_{0},\infty)\times\mathbb{S}^{1}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{2n} is smooth.

The following lemma gives an alternative characterization of smooth cylinders. Its proof is straightforward and left to the reader.

Lemma 5.5.

Let Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) be a map.

  1. (a)

    The following are equivalent.

    1. (1)

      ZZ is a smooth cylinder (in the sense of Definition 5.4).

    2. (2)

      For every k≥0k\geq 0 the induced map Z:[s0,∞)→HkZ:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow H^{k} is smooth (as map into a Banach space).

  2. (b)

    Suppose ZZ is a smooth cylinder. For every k≥0k\geq 0 the derivative of Z:[s0,∞)→HkZ:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow H^{k} at s≥s0s\geq s_{0} is Z′​(s)=∂sZ​(s,⋅)∈HkZ^{\prime}(s)=\partial_{s}Z(s,\,\cdot\,)\in H^{k}\,, where on the right-hand side we mean the partial derivative of Z:[s0,∞)×𝕊1→ℝ2​nZ:[s_{0},\infty)\times\mathbb{S}^{1}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{2n}\,.

Motivated by part (b) of the previous lemma, for a smooth cylinder ZZ we will write ∂sZ​(s)∈C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)\partial_{s}Z(s)\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) for the derivative of Z:[s0,∞)→HkZ:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow H^{k} at ss, for any choice of k≥0k\geq 0\,. (This is independent of kk.) Notice that ∂sZ\partial_{s}Z is again a smooth cylinder.

The next proposition is essentially a shifted version of Theorem C. Justified by Remark 5.2, we consider x¯0\bar{x}_{0} as constant loop at the origin in ℝ2​n\mathbb{R}^{2n}\,.

Proposition 5.6.

There exist a constant B>0B>0\,, a C∞C^{\infty}-open neighborhood 𝒰​(x¯0)⊆C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\subseteq C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) of the constant loop x¯0=𝗌𝗁−​(x0)≡0\bar{x}_{0}=\mathsf{sh}^{-}(x_{0})\equiv 0 at the origin and a C∞C^{\infty}-continuous map Ξ:𝒰​(x¯0)→[0,∞)\Xi:\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow[0,\infty) with

Ξ​(x)=0for every constant loop ​x​ with ​z′′​(x)≡0\Xi(x)=0\quad\text{for every constant loop }x\text{ with }z^{\prime\prime}(x)\equiv 0

having the following significance:
For every s0∈ℝs_{0}\in\mathbb{R} and every smooth cylinder Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) with

  1. (i)

    image contained in 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,,

  2. (ii)

    satisfying the abstract Floer equation

    (5.9) ∂sZ​(s)=𝖠Z​(s)​Z​(s)∀s≥s0,\partial_{s}Z(s)=\mathsf{A}_{Z(s)}\,Z(s)\qquad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,,
  3. (iii)

    lims→∞Z​(s)=x\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}Z(s)=x in the C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n})-topology, where x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) is some constant loop with z′′​(x)≡0z^{\prime\prime}(x)\equiv 0\,,

it holds

∥𝖰​Z​(s)∥H2≤Ξ​(Z​(s0))​e−B​(s−s0)∀s≥s0.\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z(s)\rVert_{H^{2}}\leq\Xi(Z(s_{0}))\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\qquad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,.

Before proving this proposition, which will occupy the rest of the section, we deduce Theorem C from it.

Proof of Theorem C.

Choose the constant B>0B>0, the C∞C^{\infty}-open neighborhood 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) of x¯0=𝗌𝗁−​(x0)\bar{x}_{0}=\mathsf{sh}^{-}(x_{0}) and the continuous map Ξ:𝒰​(x¯0)→[0,∞)\Xi:\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow[0,\infty) as in Proposition 5.6. Since 𝖠:𝒰​(x¯0)→ℒ​(H2,H1)\mathsf{A}:\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(H^{2},H^{1}) is continuous (Lemma 5.3), making 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller if necessary, we can assume that the operator norm of 𝖠x\mathsf{A}_{x} is bounded uniformly for all x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,. Using also the continuity of the Sobolev embedding H1↪C0​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)H^{1}\hookrightarrow C^{0}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n})\,, for a smooth cylinder ZZ, satisfying (i)–(iii) in Proposition 5.6, we estimate666This estimate can similarly be found in the proof of [5, Prop. 32].

∥∂sZ​(s)∥C0\displaystyle\lVert\partial_{s}Z(s)\rVert_{C^{0}} ≤c1​∥∂sZ​(s)∥H1=c1​∥𝖠Z​(s)​Z​(s)∥H1​=(Fact 5)​c1​∥𝖠Z​(s)​𝖰​Z​(s)∥H1\displaystyle\leq c_{1}\,\lVert\partial_{s}Z(s)\rVert_{H^{1}}=c_{1}\,\lVert\mathsf{A}_{Z(s)}\,Z(s)\rVert_{H^{1}}\overset{\text{\ref{it: operators A_x - A Q = A}}}{=}c_{1}\,\lVert\mathsf{A}_{Z(s)}\mathsf{Q}\,Z(s)\rVert_{H^{1}}
≤c1​∥𝖠Z​(s)∥op​∥𝖰​Z​(s)∥H2\displaystyle\leq c_{1}\,\lVert\mathsf{A}_{Z(s)}\rVert_{\mathrm{op}}\,\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z(s)\rVert_{H^{2}}
≤c2​Ξ​(Z​(s0))​e−B​(s−s0),\displaystyle\leq c_{2}\,\Xi(Z(s_{0}))\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\,\,,

with constants c1,c2>0c_{1},c_{2}>0 independent of ZZ. Now shift 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) to the neighborhood 𝒰​(x0):=𝗌𝗁+​(𝒰​(x¯0))\mathcal{U}(x_{0}):=\mathsf{sh}^{+}(\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})) of x0x_{0} and define the continuous function Ξ^:=(c2​Ξ)∘𝗌𝗁−\widehat{\Xi}:=(c_{2}\,\Xi)\circ\mathsf{sh}^{-} on 𝒰​(x0)\mathcal{U}(x_{0})\,, which vanishes on 𝒰​(x0)∩𝒩\mathcal{U}(x_{0})\cap\mathcal{N}\,. Given a Floer cylinder u:[s0,∞)→𝒰​(x0)u:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow\mathcal{U}(x_{0}) as in Theorem C, its shift Z:=u¯=𝗌𝗁−​(u)Z:=\bar{u}=\mathsf{sh}^{-}(u) satisfies the abstract Floer equation (5.9) due to (5.6) and moreover converges asymptotically to a constant loop Z​(∞)Z(\infty) with z′′​(Z​(∞))=0z^{\prime\prime}(Z(\infty))=0 due to (5.1). By the above considerations

∥∂su​(s,⋅)∥C0=∥∂sZ​(s)∥C0≤c2​Ξ​(Z​(s0))​e−B​(s−s0)=Ξ^​(us0)​e−B​(s−s0)\lVert\partial_{s}u(s,\,\cdot\,)\rVert_{C^{0}}=\lVert\partial_{s}Z(s)\rVert_{C^{0}}\leq c_{2}\,\Xi(Z(s_{0}))\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}=\widehat{\Xi}(u_{s_{0}})\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}

and this finishes the proof. ∎

It remains to prove Proposition 5.6 and we will accomplish this in the next two subsections in a very technical manner.

5.2. Exponential Decay Estimate for the L2L^{2}-Norm

In this section we prove an exponential decay estimate of the form described in Proposition 5.6 but for the L2L^{2}-norm instead of the H2H^{2}-norm. This is not quite strong enough but a solid first step. In the subsequent section we will then copy these arguments for higher-order vectors. The proof of the L2L^{2}-norm estimate below is an adaption of [2, Prop. A.1]. We first recapitulate a particularly easy case of a maximum principle.

Lemma 5.7 (Maximum Principle).

Given f∈C2​([s0,∞),ℝ)f\in C^{2}([s_{0},\infty),\,\mathbb{R}) and a constant δ>0\delta>0 with f′′​(s)≥δ2​f​(s)f^{\prime\prime}(s)\geq\delta^{2}\,f(s) for all s≥s0s\geq s_{0}\,. Suppose there exists a sequence s¯n≥s0\bar{s}_{n}\geq s_{0} tending to infinity with f​(s¯n)​→𝑛​0f(\bar{s}_{n})\overset{n}{\rightarrow}0\,. Then

f​(s)≤f​(s0)​e−δ​(s−s0)∀s≥s0.f(s)\leq f(s_{0})\,e^{-\delta(s-s_{0})}\quad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,.
Proof.

Define g​(s):=f​(s0)​e−δ​(s−s0)g(s):=f(s_{0})\,e^{-\delta(s-s_{0})}\,. Then (f−g)′′≥δ2​(f−g)(f-g)^{\prime\prime}\geq\delta^{2}(f-g)\,, which shows that f−gf-g does not have a strictly positive local maximum on (s0,∞)(s_{0},\infty)\,. Using moreover (f−g)​(s0)=0(f-g)(s_{0})=0 and (f−g)​(s¯n)​→𝑛​0(f-g)(\bar{s}_{n})\overset{n}{\rightarrow}0\,, we conclude that f−g≤0f-g\leq 0\,. ∎

Recall that x¯0\bar{x}_{0} is the constant loop at the origin in ℝ2​n\mathbb{R}^{2n}. In the proposition below, let c​(0)>0c(0)>0 be the constant from (Fact 7).

Proposition 5.8.

Let 0<B<c​(0)0<B<\sqrt{c(0)} be arbitrary. There exists a C∞C^{\infty}-open neighborhood 𝒰​(x¯0)⊆C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\subseteq C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) of x¯0\bar{x}_{0} so that for every s0∈ℝs_{0}\in\mathbb{R} and every smooth cylinder Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n})\,, satisfying (i)–(iii) in Proposition 5.6, it holds

∥𝖰​Z​(s)∥L2≤∥𝖰​Z​(s0)∥L2​e−B​(s−s0)∀s≥s0.\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z(s)\rVert_{L^{2}}\leq\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z(s_{0})\rVert_{L^{2}}\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\qquad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,.

The function Ξ:𝒰​(x¯0)→[0,∞)\Xi:\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow[0,\infty) defined by Ξ​(x):=∥𝖰​x∥L2\Xi(x):=\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,x\rVert_{L^{2}} is L2L^{2}-continuous, so a fortiori C∞C^{\infty}-continuous, and moreover vanishes on ker⁡𝖠x¯0\ker\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\,. Since ker⁡𝖠x¯0\ker\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}} consists of the constant loops xx with z′′​(x)≡0z^{\prime\prime}(x)\equiv 0\,, the above inequality is nearly as desired in Proposition 5.6, but unfortunately for the L2L^{2}-norm instead of the H2H^{2}-norm.

Proof of Proposition 5.8.

We define ε:=12​(c​(0)−B2)>0\varepsilon:=\frac{1}{2}(c(0)-B^{2})>0 and start by selecting the neighborhood 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,. We will successively choose 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller below but keep writing the same symbol.

  1. (1)

    Recall from (Fact 7) that 𝖰​𝖠x¯0|𝖠x¯0​(H2)𝖠x¯0​(H1)\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}|_{\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{2})}^{\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{1})} is invertible. Now invertibility is an open condition and taking the inverse of an operator a continuous assignment. Because 𝖠\mathsf{A} is a continuous map from C∞​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1}) into the space of bounded linear operators (Lemma 5.3), we can choose a C∞C^{\infty}-open neighborhood 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) of x¯0\bar{x}_{0} so that for every x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) the operator 𝖰​𝖠x|𝖠x¯0​(H2)𝖠x¯0​(H1)\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{x}|_{\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{2})}^{\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{1})} is invertible with

    0<c(0)−ε=∥(𝖰𝖠x¯0|𝖠x¯0​(H2)𝖠x¯0​(H1))−1∥−2−ε≤∥(𝖰𝖠x|𝖠x¯0​(H2)𝖠x¯0​(H1))−1∥−2.\displaystyle 0<c(0)-\varepsilon=\left\lVert\Big(\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\big|_{\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{2})}^{\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{1})}\Big)^{-1}\right\rVert^{-2}-\varepsilon\leq\left\lVert\Big(\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{x}\big|_{\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{2})}^{\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{1})}\Big)^{-1}\right\rVert^{-2}\,\,.

    By this choice of 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) and (5.8), for every x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) it holds

    (5.10) ∥𝖠x​𝖰​X∥L22≥∥𝖰​𝖠x​𝖰​X∥L22≥(c​(0)−ε)​∥𝖰​X∥H12∀X∈H1.\lVert\mathsf{A}_{x}\,\mathsf{Q}\,X\rVert_{L^{2}}^{2}\geq\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{x}\,\mathsf{Q}\,X\rVert_{L^{2}}^{2}\geq(c(0)-\varepsilon)\,\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,X\rVert_{H^{1}}^{2}\qquad\forall\,X\in H^{1}\,\,.
  2. (2)

    Denote by 𝖠x∗:H1→L2\mathsf{A}_{x}^{*}:H^{1}\rightarrow L^{2} the adjoint of 𝖠x\mathsf{A}_{x} with respect to the inner product (gJ)x0=⟨⋅,⋅⟩L2(g_{J})_{x_{0}}=\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{L^{2}}\,.777We emphasize that we define the domain of the adjoint to be H1H^{1}, see Definition A.5 in the appendix. Being a partial differential operator of order 11, the operator 𝖠x\mathsf{A}_{x} has an adjoint which lies in ℒ​(H1,L2)\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})\,, see Appendix A.2. The map 𝒰​(x¯0)→ℒ​(H1,L2),x↦𝖠x∗\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})\,,\,x\mapsto\mathsf{A}_{x}^{*}\,, is continuous (by Proposition A.7 in the appendix) and hence so is

    𝒰​(x¯0)→[0,∞),x↦∥𝖠x∥ℒ​(H1,L2)​∥𝖠x∗−𝖠x∥ℒ​(H1,L2).\displaystyle\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow[0,\infty)\,,\,\quad x\mapsto\lVert\mathsf{A}_{x}\rVert_{\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})}\,\lVert\mathsf{A}^{*}_{x}-\mathsf{A}_{x}\rVert_{\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})}\,\,.

    Moreover, this map vanishes at x¯0\bar{x}_{0} because 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}} is selfadjoint, see (Fact 1). Thus we can choose 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller so that

    (5.11) ∥𝖠x∥ℒ​(H1,L2)​∥𝖠x∗−𝖠x∥ℒ​(H1,L2)≤ε∀x∈𝒰​(x¯0).\lVert\mathsf{A}_{x}\rVert_{\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})}\,\lVert\mathsf{A}^{*}_{x}-\mathsf{A}_{x}\rVert_{\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})}\leq\varepsilon\qquad\forall\,x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,\,.
  3. (3)

    We choose 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller, so that every smooth cylinder Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) with image contained in 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) satisfies

    (5.12) ∂sZ=𝖠Z​Z⟹∥(∂s𝖠Z​(s))​(s1)∥ℒ​(H1,L2)<ε∀s1≥s0.\partial_{s}Z=\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z\quad\Longrightarrow\quad\big\lVert(\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z(s)})(s_{1})\big\rVert_{\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})}<\varepsilon\quad\forall\,s_{1}\geq s_{0}\,\,.

    This is possible, as we now explain. Recall from Lemma 5.3 that the map 𝖠:H1​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)→ℒ​(H1,L2)\mathsf{A}:H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2}) is smooth. We denote by

    D∙​𝖠:H1​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)→ℒ​(H1,ℒ​(H1,L2)),x↦Dx​𝖠,D_{\bullet}\mathsf{A}:H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(H^{1},\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2}))\,,\,\quad x\mapsto D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,,

    its differential. Then the map

    Θ:𝒰​(x¯0)→[0,∞),Θ​(x):=‖Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x]‖=∥Dx​𝖠(1)​[𝖠x(2)​x]⏟∈H1∥ℒ​(H1,L2)\displaystyle\Theta:\,\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow[0,\infty)\,,\,\quad\Theta(x):=\left\lVert D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}\,x]\right\rVert=\Big\lVert D_{x}\mathsf{A}^{(1)}\,\underbrace{[\mathsf{A}_{x}^{(2)}\,x]}_{\in H^{1}}\Big\rVert_{\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})}

    is continuous, where as usual 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) carries the C∞C^{\infty}-topology. Moreover Θ​(x¯0)=0\Theta(\bar{x}_{0})=0 since 0≡x¯0∈ker⁡(𝖠x¯0)0\equiv\bar{x}_{0}\in\ker(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}})\,. Thus we can choose 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller so that 0≤Θ​(x)<ε0\leq\Theta(x)<\varepsilon for every x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,. This choice of 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) now satisfies (5.12): Given a smooth cylinder ZZ with image in 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) and ∂sZ=𝖠Z​Z\partial_{s}Z=\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z\,, we compute

    (∂s𝖠Z)​(s)=DZ​(s)​𝖠​[∂sZ​(s)]=DZ​(s)​𝖠​[𝖠Z​(s)​Z​(s)](\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z})(s)=D_{Z(s)}\mathsf{A}\,[\partial_{s}Z(s)]=D_{Z(s)}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z(s)}\,Z(s)]

    and hence ∥(∂s𝖠Z)​(s)∥=Θ​(Z​(s))<ε\lVert(\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z})(s)\rVert=\Theta(Z(s))<\varepsilon\,.

Having chosen 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,, we now verify that it has the desired property. Let Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) be an arbitrary smooth cylinder satisfying (i)–(iii) in Proposition 5.6. We define the smooth function

f:[s0,∞)→[0,∞),f​(s):=12​∥𝖰​Z​(s)∥L22.f:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow[0,\infty)\,,\,\quad f(s):=\frac{1}{2}\,\big\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z(s)\big\rVert_{L^{2}}^{2}\,\,.

Notice that lims→∞f​(s)=0\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}f(s)=0 since lims→∞Z​(s)∈ker⁡𝖠x¯0=ker⁡𝖰\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}Z(s)\in\ker\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}=\ker\mathsf{Q} by (iii) and (Fact 2). We aim to show

(5.13) f′′​(s)≥4​B2​f​(s)∀s≥s0.f^{\prime\prime}(s)\geq 4B^{2}\,f(s)\qquad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,.

If this has been shown, the maximum principle (Lemma 5.7) yields

∥𝖰​Z​(s)∥L22≤∥𝖰​Z​(s0)∥L22​e−2​B​(s−s0)∀s≥s0,\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z(s)\rVert_{L^{2}}^{2}\leq\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z(s_{0})\rVert_{L^{2}}^{2}\,e^{-2B(s-s_{0})}\qquad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,,

which is our ultimate goal. We now show (5.13). To this end, we use that 𝖰​∂s=∂s𝖰\mathsf{Q}\,\partial_{s}=\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{Q} (because 𝖰\mathsf{Q} is a bounded linear operator), that 𝖠Z​𝖰=𝖠Z\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}=\mathsf{A}_{Z} by (Fact 5), that ⟨𝖰​X1,𝖰​X2⟩L2=⟨𝖰​X1,X2⟩L2\langle\mathsf{Q}X_{1},\,\mathsf{Q}\,X_{2}\rangle_{L^{2}}=\langle\mathsf{Q}X_{1},\,X_{2}\rangle_{L^{2}} for all X1,X2X_{1},X_{2} (because 𝖰\mathsf{Q} is an orthogonal projection) and the abstract Floer equation

∂sZ=𝖠Z​Z=𝖠Z​𝖰​Z,\partial_{s}Z=\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z=\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\,\,,

which holds due to assumption (ii). Let s≥s0s\geq s_{0} be arbitrary but fixed (below we drop ss in the notation). We compute

f′′​(s)\displaystyle f^{\prime\prime}(s) =∥∂s(𝖰​Z)​(s)∥L22+⟨𝖰​Z​(s),∂s2(𝖰​Z)​(s)⟩L2\displaystyle=\lVert\partial_{s}(\mathsf{Q}\,Z)(s)\rVert^{2}_{L^{2}}+\big\langle\mathsf{Q}\,Z(s),\,\partial_{s}^{2}(\mathsf{Q}\,Z)(s)\big\rangle_{L^{2}}
=∥𝖰​𝖠Z​𝖰​Z∥L22+⟨𝖰​Z,𝖰​∂s(𝖠Z​𝖰​Z)⟩L2\displaystyle=\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\rVert^{2}_{L^{2}}+\big\langle\mathsf{Q}\,Z,\,\mathsf{Q}\,\partial_{s}(\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z)\big\rangle_{L^{2}}
=∥𝖰𝖠Z𝖰Z∥L22+⟨𝖰Z,(∂s𝖠Z)𝖰Z+𝖠Z𝖰∂sZ)⟩L2\displaystyle=\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\rVert^{2}_{L^{2}}+\big\langle\mathsf{Q}\,Z,\,(\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z})\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z+\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}\,\partial_{s}Z)\big\rangle_{L^{2}}
=∥𝖰𝖠Z𝖰Z∥L22+⟨𝖰Z,(∂s𝖠Z)𝖰Z⟩L2+⟨𝖰Z,𝖠Z2𝖰Z)⟩L2\displaystyle=\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\rVert^{2}_{L^{2}}+\big\langle\mathsf{Q}\,Z,\,(\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z})\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\big\rangle_{L^{2}}+\big\langle\mathsf{Q}\,Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{2}\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z)\big\rangle_{L^{2}}
=∥𝖰​𝖠Z​𝖰​Z∥L22+⟨𝖰​Z,(∂s𝖠Z)​𝖰​Z⟩L2\displaystyle=\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\rVert^{2}_{L^{2}}+\big\langle\mathsf{Q}\,Z,\,(\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z})\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\big\rangle_{L^{2}}
(5.14) +⟨(𝖠Z∗−𝖠Z)​𝖰​Z,𝖠Z​𝖰​Z⟩L2+∥𝖠Z​𝖰​Z∥L22.\displaystyle\hskip 8.53581pt+\big\langle(\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{*}-\mathsf{A}_{Z})\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\big\rangle_{L^{2}}+\lVert\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\rVert_{L^{2}}^{2}\,\,.

Since ZZ has image contained in 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}), the first and the fourth term on the right can be bounded below by (5.10), so that

∥𝖰​𝖠Z​𝖰​Z∥L22+∥𝖠Z​𝖰​Z∥L22≥2​(c​(0)−ε)​∥𝖰​Z∥H12.\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\rVert^{2}_{L^{2}}+\lVert\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\rVert_{L^{2}}^{2}\geq 2(c(0)-\varepsilon)\,\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z\rVert_{H^{1}}^{2}\,\,.

On the other hand, using (5.11) and (5.12), the second and third term can be bounded above by

|⟨𝖰​Z,(∂s𝖠Z)​𝖰​Z⟩L2|+|⟨(𝖠Z∗−𝖠Z)​𝖰​Z,𝖠Z​𝖰​Z⟩L2|≤2​ε​∥𝖰​Z∥H12.\displaystyle\big|\big\langle\mathsf{Q}\,Z,\,(\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z})\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\big\rangle_{L^{2}}\big|+\big|\big\langle(\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{*}-\mathsf{A}_{Z})\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}\,Z\big\rangle_{L^{2}}\big|\leq 2\,\varepsilon\,\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z\rVert^{2}_{H^{1}}\,\,.

Thus

f′′​(s)≥(2​c​(0)−4​ε)​∥𝖰​Z∥H12≥(2​c​(0)−4​ε)​∥𝖰​Z∥L22=4​B2​f​(s),\displaystyle f^{\prime\prime}(s)\geq(2c(0)-4\varepsilon)\,\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z\rVert_{H^{1}}^{2}\geq(2c(0)-4\varepsilon)\,\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z\rVert_{L^{2}}^{2}=4B^{2}\,f(s)\,\,,

showing (5.13). ∎

Remark 5.9.

In the proof of [2, Prop. A.1] it is claimed that the above estimates not only hold for the L2L^{2}-norm but for every HkH^{k}-norm. If this was true, replacing the L2L^{2}-norm with the H2H^{2}-norm in the above proof would directly prove Proposition 5.6. However, we believe this to be a mistake. Even if working with the Euclidean L2L^{2}-inner product, the standard almost complex structure JstdJ_{\mathrm{std}} on ℝ2​n\mathbb{R}^{2n} and a loop of symmetric asymptotic matrices S∞​(t)S_{\infty}(t)\,, which is the setting in [2], the asymptotic operator 𝖠∞:=Jstd​∂t+S∞\mathsf{A}_{\infty}:=J_{\mathrm{std}}\,\partial_{t}+S_{\infty} (which, up to sign, corresponds to our operator 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}) is L2L^{2}-selfadjoint but not H1H^{1}-selfadjoint. Indeed,

⟨𝖠∞​X1,X2⟩H1−⟨X1,𝖠∞​X2⟩H1\displaystyle\langle\mathsf{A}_{\infty}X_{1},\,X_{2}\rangle_{H^{1}}-\langle X_{1},\mathsf{A}_{\infty}\,X_{2}\rangle_{H^{1}} =⟨S˙∞​X1,∂tX2⟩L2−⟨∂tX1,S˙∞​X2⟩L2\displaystyle=\langle\dot{S}_{\infty}\,X_{1},\,\partial_{t}X_{2}\rangle_{L^{2}}-\langle\partial_{t}X_{1},\,\dot{S}_{\infty}\,X_{2}\rangle_{L^{2}}
=⟨−S¨∞​X1−2​S˙∞​∂tX1,X2⟩L2\displaystyle=\langle-\ddot{S}_{\infty}\,X_{1}-2\,\dot{S}_{\infty}\,\partial_{t}X_{1},\,X_{2}\rangle_{L^{2}}

in general does not vanish for all X1,X2∈H2X_{1},X_{2}\in H^{2}\,. It is therefore unclear to us how the analog of (5.11) for the H1H^{1}-adjoint should be accomplished.

5.3. Proof of Proposition 5.6

We now prove Proposition 5.6 by imitating the proof of Proposition 5.8, replacing ZZ by the vector (Z,∂sZ,∂s2Z)(Z,\partial_{s}Z,\partial_{s}^{2}Z) and the operators 𝖠x\mathsf{A}_{x} by operators on (L2)⊕3(L^{2})^{\oplus 3}\,. This trick seems to have its origins in the analysis of the asymptotic behavior of pseudoholomorphic curves by Hofer-Wysocki-Zehnder, cf. [6]. It was pointed out and explained to me in great detail by Richard Siefring, for which I would like to express my sincere gratitude.

Contrary to the original trick however, we have not fixed a single Floer cylinder but instead investigate all asymptotic cylinders whose loops lie close to a fixed “asymptotic loop”. For this reason, the terms we want to bound should not depend on ss-derivatives ∂sZ\partial_{s}Z of a cylinder Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) but only on the loop Z​(s)Z(s)\,. The general philosophy of the proof below therefore is to replace all ss-derivatives by a loop-dependent expression via the abstract Floer equation (5.9).

As before, we denote by D∙​𝖠D_{\bullet}\mathsf{A} the differential of the smooth map 𝖠=𝖠(1):H1​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)→ℒ​(H1,L2)\mathsf{A}=\mathsf{A}^{(1)}:H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})\,, by D∙2​𝖠D^{2}_{\bullet}\mathsf{A} its second derivative and so on. By the chain rule, for all k≤ℓk\leq\ell and x∈Hℓ​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)x\in H^{\ell}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})\,, the diagram

commutes, where the unlabeled arrows are the obvious linear maps. An analogous statement holds for higher order derivatives. For this reason we usually omit the superscript when working with the derivatives of the operators 𝖠x\mathsf{A}_{x}\,. As last notational preparation, we introduce

L^2:=L2⊕L2⊕L2 and ​H^k:=Hk⊕Hk⊕Hk\widehat{L}^{2}:=L^{2}\oplus L^{2}\oplus L^{2}\quad\text{ and }\widehat{H}^{k}:=H^{k}\oplus H^{k}\oplus H^{k}

and endow them with the direct sum inner products respectively, where L2L^{2} and HkH^{k} carry the inner products from before (defined in (5.3)).

Proof of Proposition 5.6.

We consider the neighborhood 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) of x¯0\bar{x}_{0} either as subset of C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) or of C∞​(𝕊1,𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1)C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1})\,, whatever is more convenient, which is justified by Remark 5.2. We will make 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller in the course of the proof but keep writing 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,. The proof of the proposition is subdivided into two parts.

Part I: Let Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) be a smooth cylinder with image contained in 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) and satisfying the abstract Floer equation ∂sZ=𝖠Z​Z\partial_{s}Z=\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z\,. Deriving the abstract Floer equation twice, we obtain

(5.15) (∂sZ∂s2Z∂s3Z)=(𝖠Z00∂s𝖠Z𝖠Z0∂s2𝖠Z2​∂s𝖠Z𝖠Z)​(Z∂sZ∂s2Z).\displaystyle\left(\begin{matrix}\partial_{s}Z\\ \partial_{s}^{2}Z\\ \partial_{s}^{3}Z\end{matrix}\right)=\left(\begin{matrix}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&0&0\\ \partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&\mathsf{A}_{Z}&0\\ \partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&2\,\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&\mathsf{A}_{Z}\end{matrix}\right)\,\left(\begin{matrix}Z\\ \partial_{s}Z\\ \partial_{s}^{2}Z\end{matrix}\right)\quad.

Using the abstract Floer equation, we eliminate the ss-derivatives:

(5.16) ∂s𝖠Z\displaystyle\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z} =DZ​𝖠​[∂sZ]=DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]\displaystyle=D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\partial_{s}Z]=D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z]
∂s2𝖠Z\displaystyle\partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{A}_{Z} =∂s(DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z])\displaystyle=\partial_{s}\big(D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z]\big)
=(∂sDZ​𝖠)​[𝖠Z​Z]+DZ​𝖠​[∂s(𝖠Z​Z)]\displaystyle=(\partial_{s}D_{Z}\mathsf{A})\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z]+D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\partial_{s}(\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z)]
(5.17) =DZ2​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z,𝖠Z​Z]+DZ​𝖠​[DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]​Z]+DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z2​Z].\displaystyle=D^{2}_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z]+D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z]\,Z]+D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{2}\,Z]\,\,.

The last expression depends only on the value of Z​(s)Z(s) in the loopspace. Introducing the loop-dependent operator 𝖡x∈ℒ​(H1,L2)\mathsf{B}_{x}\in\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})\,, for x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,, by

(5.18) 𝖡x:=Dx2​𝖠​[𝖠x​x,𝖠x​x]+Dx​𝖠​[Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x]​x]+Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x2​x],\mathsf{B}_{x}:=D^{2}_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}\,x,\,\mathsf{A}_{x}\,x]+D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}\,x]\,x]+D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}^{2}\,x]\,\,,

we therefore have ∂s2𝖠Z​(s)=𝖡Z​(s)\partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{A}_{Z}(s)=\mathsf{B}_{Z(s)}\,. Similarly, we define the loop-dependent operator 𝖠^x∈ℒ​(H^1,L^2)\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{x}\in\mathcal{L}(\widehat{H}^{1},\widehat{L}^{2})\,, for every x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,, by

(5.19) 𝖠^x:=(𝖠x00Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x]𝖠x0𝖡x2​Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x]𝖠x).\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{x}:=\left(\begin{matrix}\mathsf{A}_{x}&0&0\\ D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}\,x]&\mathsf{A}_{x}&0\\ \mathsf{B}_{x}&2\,D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}\,x]&\mathsf{A}_{x}\end{matrix}\right)\,\,.

We have defined 𝖠^x\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{x} in such a way that, for a cylinder ZZ satisfying ∂sZ=𝖠Z​Z\partial_{s}Z=\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z\,, it holds

(5.20) 𝖠^Z​(s)=(𝖠Z​(s)00∂s𝖠Z​(s)𝖠Z​(s)0∂s2𝖠Z​(s)2​∂s𝖠Z​(s)𝖠Z​(s)).\displaystyle\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z(s)}=\left(\begin{matrix}\mathsf{A}_{Z(s)}&0&0\\ \partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z}(s)&\mathsf{A}_{Z(s)}&0\\ \partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{A}_{Z}(s)&2\,\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z}(s)&\mathsf{A}_{Z(s)}\end{matrix}\right)\,\,.

Abbreviating

Z^​(s):=(Z​(s),∂sZ​(s),∂s2Z​(s))∈C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)⊕3=⋂k≥0H^k,\widehat{Z}(s):=(Z(s),\,\partial_{s}Z(s),\,\partial_{s}^{2}Z(s))\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n})^{\oplus 3}=\bigcap_{k\geq 0}\widehat{H}^{k}\,\,,

we can now rewrite (5.15) very succinctly as

(5.21) ∂sZ^​(s)=𝖠^Z​(s)​Z^​(s)∀s≥s0.\partial_{s}\widehat{Z}(s)=\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z(s)}\,\widehat{Z}(s)\qquad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,.

Since x¯0∈ker⁡(𝖠x¯0)\bar{x}_{0}\in\ker(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}})\,, the operator 𝖠^x¯0\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{\bar{x}_{0}} at x¯0\bar{x}_{0} is

𝖠^x¯0=diag​(𝖠x¯0,𝖠x¯0,𝖠x¯0)=(𝖠x¯0000𝖠x¯0000𝖠x¯0),\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{\bar{x}_{0}}=\mathrm{diag}(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}},\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}},\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}})=\left(\begin{matrix}\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}&0&0\\ 0&\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}&0\\ 0&0&\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\end{matrix}\right)\,\,,

that is 𝖠^x¯0\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{\bar{x}_{0}} is a diagonal operator of 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\,. Therefore, the diagonal operators

𝖯^=𝖯^(k):=diag​(𝖯(k),𝖯(k),𝖯(k))and𝖰^=𝖰^(k):=diag​(𝖰(k),𝖰(k),𝖰(k))\displaystyle\widehat{\mathsf{P}}=\widehat{\mathsf{P}}^{(k)}:=\mathrm{diag}(\mathsf{P}^{(k)},\mathsf{P}^{(k)},\mathsf{P}^{(k)})\quad\text{and}\quad\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}=\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}^{(k)}:=\mathrm{diag}(\mathsf{Q}^{(k)},\mathsf{Q}^{(k)},\mathsf{Q}^{(k)})

are the projections with respect to the L^2\widehat{L}^{2}- and H^k\widehat{H}^{k}-orthogonal splitting

H^k=ker(𝖠^x¯0)⊕𝖠^x¯0(H^k+1)=ker(𝖠x¯0)⊕3⊕𝖠x¯0(Hk+1)⊕3.\widehat{H}^{k}=\ker(\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{\bar{x}_{0}})\oplus\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(\widehat{H}^{k+1})=\ker(\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}})^{\oplus 3}\oplus\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(H^{k+1})^{\oplus 3}\,\,.

Since 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}} is ⟨⋅,⋅⟩L2\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{L^{2}}-selfadjoint by (Fact 1), the diagonal operator 𝖠^x¯0\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{\bar{x}_{0}} is selfadjoint for

⟨⋅,⋅⟩L^2=⟨⋅,⋅⟩L2⊕⟨⋅,⋅⟩L2⊕⟨⋅,⋅⟩L2.\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{\widehat{L}^{2}}=\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{L^{2}}\oplus\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{L^{2}}\oplus\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{L^{2}}\,\,.
Claim.

𝖠^Z​𝖰^=𝖠^Z\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}=\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z} for every smooth cylinder Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) with image contained in 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) and satisfying ∂sZ=𝖠Z​Z\partial_{s}Z=\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z\,.

Proof of the claim.

It holds 𝖠Z​𝖰=𝖠Z\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,\mathsf{Q}=\mathsf{A}_{Z} by (Fact 5). Derive this twice to obtain

(∂s𝖠Z)​𝖰=∂s𝖠Zand(∂s2𝖠Z)​𝖰=∂s2𝖠Z\displaystyle(\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z})\,\mathsf{Q}=\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z}\quad\text{and}\quad(\partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{A}_{Z})\,\mathsf{Q}=\partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{A}_{Z}

and therefore

𝖠^Z​𝖰^=(𝖠Z00∂s𝖠Z𝖠Z0∂s2𝖠Z2​∂s𝖠Z𝖠Z)​(𝖰000𝖰000𝖰)=(𝖠Z00∂s𝖠Z𝖠Z0∂s2𝖠Z2​∂s𝖠Z𝖠Z)=𝖠^Z.\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}=\left(\begin{matrix}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&0&0\\ \partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&\mathsf{A}_{Z}&0\\ \partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&2\,\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&\mathsf{A}_{Z}\end{matrix}\right)\,\left(\begin{matrix}\mathsf{Q}&0&0\\ 0&\mathsf{Q}&0\\ 0&0&\mathsf{Q}\end{matrix}\right)=\left(\begin{matrix}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&0&0\\ \partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&\mathsf{A}_{Z}&0\\ \partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&2\,\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&\mathsf{A}_{Z}\end{matrix}\right)=\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}\,\,.

∎

For given cylinder Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) satisfying the abstract Floer equation, as before, set Z^=(Z,∂sZ,∂s2Z)\widehat{Z}=(Z,\partial_{s}Z,\partial_{s}^{2}Z) and define

f=fZ:[s0,∞)→[0,∞),f​(s):=12​∥𝖰^​Z^​(s)∥L^22.f=f_{Z}:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow[0,\infty)\,,\,\quad f(s):=\frac{1}{2}\,\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}(s)\rVert_{\widehat{L}^{2}}^{2}\,\,.

Due to (5.21), using also that 𝖠^Z​𝖰^=𝖠^Z\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}=\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z} by the claim and that 𝖰^\widehat{\mathsf{Q}} is an L^\widehat{L}-orthogonal projection, the computation in (5.14) now goes through verbatim. This yields

fZ′′​(s)\displaystyle f_{Z}^{\prime\prime}(s) =∥𝖰^​𝖠^Z​𝖰^​Z^∥L^22+⟨𝖰^​Z^,(∂s𝖠^Z)​𝖰^​Z^⟩L^2\displaystyle=\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}\rVert^{2}_{\widehat{L}^{2}}+\big\langle\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z},\,(\partial_{s}\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z})\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}\big\rangle_{\widehat{L}^{2}}
(5.22) +⟨(𝖠^Z∗−𝖠^Z)​𝖰^​Z^,𝖠^Z​𝖰^​Z^⟩L^2+∥𝖠^Z​𝖰^​Z^∥L^22.\displaystyle\hskip 8.53581pt+\big\langle(\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}^{*}-\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z})\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z},\,\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}\big\rangle_{\widehat{L}^{2}}+\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}\rVert_{\widehat{L}^{2}}^{2}\,\,.

Here 𝖠^x∗∈ℒ​(H^1,L^2)\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{x}^{*}\in\mathcal{L}(\widehat{H}^{1},\widehat{L}^{2}) denotes the L^2\widehat{L}^{2}-adjoint of 𝖠^x\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{x}\,, for every loop x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,. The adjoint of 𝖠^x\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{x} exists and lies in ℒ​(H^1,L^2)\mathcal{L}(\widehat{H}^{1},\widehat{L}^{2}) because it is a matrix of partial differential operators of order 11.888To see this, stare at (5.19) and recall the proof of Lemma 5.3 to compute the derivatives Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x]D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}x]\,. Notice moreover that the map s↦𝖠^Z​(s)∈ℒ​(H^1,L^2)s\mapsto\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z(s)}\in\mathcal{L}(\widehat{H}^{1},\widehat{L}^{2}) is smooth. This follows from its representation in (5.20) and from s↦𝖠Z​(s)s\mapsto\mathsf{A}_{Z(s)} being smooth by Lemma 5.3 and Lemma 5.5.

We are now in a position completely analogous to the one in the proof of Proposition 5.8 and we continue by ensuring the analogs of (5.10), (5.11) and (5.12).

Claim.

We can choose 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller and constants c>0c>0 and ε∈(0,c2)\varepsilon\in(0,\frac{c}{2}) so that the following hold.

  1. (1)

    For all x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) it holds

    (5.23) ∥𝖠^x​𝖰^​X^∥L^22≥∥𝖰^​𝖠^x​𝖰^​X^∥L^22≥(c−ε)​∥𝖰^​X^∥H^12∀X^∈H^1.\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{x}\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{X}\rVert_{\widehat{L}^{2}}^{2}\geq\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{x}\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{X}\rVert_{\widehat{L}^{2}}^{2}\geq(c-\varepsilon)\,\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{X}\rVert_{\widehat{H}^{1}}^{2}\qquad\forall\,\widehat{X}\in\widehat{H}^{1}\,\,.
  2. (2)

    For all x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) it holds

    (5.24) ∥𝖠^x∥ℒ​(H^1,L^2)​∥𝖠^x∗−𝖠^x∥ℒ​(H^1,L^2)≤ε.\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{x}\rVert_{\mathcal{L}(\widehat{H}^{1},\widehat{L}^{2})}\,\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{A}}^{*}_{x}-\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{x}\rVert_{\mathcal{L}(\widehat{H}^{1},\widehat{L}^{2})}\leq\varepsilon\,\,.
  3. (3)

    Every smooth cylinder Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) with image in 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) satisfies

    (5.25) ∂sZ=𝖠Z​Z⟹∥(∂s𝖠^Z​(s))​(s1)∥ℒ​(H^1,L^2)<ε∀s1≥s0.\partial_{s}Z=\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z\quad\Longrightarrow\quad\big\lVert(\partial_{s}\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z(s)})(s_{1})\big\rVert_{\mathcal{L}(\widehat{H}^{1},\widehat{L}^{2})}<\varepsilon\quad\forall\,s_{1}\geq s_{0}\,\,.
Proof of the claim.

We will successively choose 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller.
We start by ensuring (1). This follows as in the proof of (5.10). Just notice that

𝖰^​𝖠^x¯0=diag​(𝖰​𝖠x¯0):𝖠^x¯0​(H^2)→𝖠^x¯0​(H^1)\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{\bar{x}_{0}}=\mathrm{diag}(\mathsf{Q}\,\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}})\,:\,\,\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(\widehat{H}^{2})\rightarrow\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{\bar{x}_{0}}(\widehat{H}^{1})

is invertible. Now define c−1/2c^{-1/2} to be the operator norm of its inverse and choose 0<ε<c20<\varepsilon<\frac{c}{2} arbitrary.
Next (2) follows similar to (5.11). Indeed, 𝖠^x¯0\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{\bar{x}_{0}} is L^2\widehat{L}^{2}-selfadjoint and the assignment 𝒰​(x¯0)→ℒ​(H^1,L^2),x↦𝖠^x∗\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(\widehat{H}^{1},\widehat{L}^{2})\,,\,x\mapsto\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{x}^{*}\,, is continuous. To see the latter, it suffices to show that the adjoints of the matrix components of 𝖠^x\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{x}\,, given in (5.19), depend continuously on xx. To this end, observe that each component is a first-order partial differential operator with coefficient functions varying continuously in the C∞​(𝕊1)C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1})-topology if xx varies in the C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n})-topology. Now apply Proposition A.7 in the appendix.
Lastly, we deal with (3). This is the cumbersome part because we have to eliminate ss-derivatives again. For a smooth cylinder ZZ satisfying the abstract Floer equation (5.9), we have

∂s𝖠^Z=∂s(𝖠Z00∂s𝖠Z𝖠Z0∂s2𝖠Z2​∂s𝖠Z𝖠Z)=(DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]00𝖡ZDZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]0∂s𝖡Z2​𝖡ZDZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z])\displaystyle\partial_{s}\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}=\partial_{s}\left(\begin{matrix}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&0&0\\ \partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&\mathsf{A}_{Z}&0\\ \partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&2\,\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z}&\mathsf{A}_{Z}\end{matrix}\right)=\left(\begin{matrix}D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z]&0&0\\ \mathsf{B}_{Z}&D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z]&0\\ \partial_{s}\mathsf{B}_{Z}&2\,\mathsf{B}_{Z}&D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z]\end{matrix}\right)

by (5.16), (5.17), (5.18) and (5.20). Repeatedly invoking the abstract Floer equation to eliminate ss-derivatives, one can see (see Appendix A.3) that ∂s𝖡Z​(s)=𝖢Z​(s)\partial_{s}\mathsf{B}_{Z}(s)=\mathsf{C}_{Z(s)}\,, where 𝖢:𝒰​(x¯0)→ℒ​(H1,L2),x↦𝖢x\mathsf{C}:\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})\,,\,x\mapsto\mathsf{C}_{x}\,, is the continuous map defined by the following intimidating formula:

𝖢x\displaystyle\mathsf{C}_{x} :=Dx3​𝖠​[𝖠x​x,𝖠x​x,𝖠x​x]+3​Dx2​𝖠​[𝖠x​x,𝖠x2​x]+3​Dx2​𝖠​[𝖠x​x,Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x]​x]\displaystyle:=D_{x}^{3}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}x,\,\mathsf{A}_{x}x,\,\mathsf{A}_{x}x]+3\,D_{x}^{2}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}x,\,\mathsf{A}_{x}^{2}x]+3\,D_{x}^{2}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}x,\,D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}x]\,x]
+2​Dx​𝖠​[Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x]​𝖠x​x]+Dx​𝖠​[Dx​𝖠​[Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x]​x]​x]+Dx​𝖠​[Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x2​x]​x]\displaystyle\hskip 8.53581pt+2\,D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}x]\,\mathsf{A}_{x}x]+D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,\left[D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,\left[D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}x]\,x\right]\,x\right]+D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}^{2}\,x]\,x]
(5.26) +Dx​𝖠​[Dx2​𝖠​[𝖠x​x,𝖠x​x]​x]+Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x​Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x]​x]+Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x3​x].\displaystyle\hskip 8.53581pt+D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,\left[D_{x}^{2}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}x,\,\mathsf{A}_{x}x]\,x\right]+D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}\,D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}x]\,x]+D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}^{3}\,x]\,\,.

The only relevant property of 𝖢\mathsf{C}\,, beside its continuity, is that its evaluation at x¯0\bar{x}_{0} is 𝖢x¯0=0∈ℒ​(H1,L2)\mathsf{C}_{\bar{x}_{0}}=0\in\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2}) because every summand contains an application of a linear operator to the zero loop x¯0\bar{x}_{0}\,. For the same reason, also 𝖡x¯0=0\mathsf{B}_{\bar{x}_{0}}=0\,. Consequently, the map

Θ:𝒰​(x¯0)→[0,∞),Θ​(x):=‖(Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x]00𝖡xDx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x]0𝖢x2​𝖡xDx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x])‖ℒ​(H^1,L^2)\displaystyle\Theta:\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow[0,\infty)\,,\,\quad\Theta(x):=\left\lVert\left(\begin{matrix}D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}\,x]&0&0\\ \mathsf{B}_{x}&D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}\,x]&0\\ \mathsf{C}_{x}&2\,\mathsf{B}_{x}&D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}\,x]\end{matrix}\right)\right\rVert_{\mathcal{L}(\widehat{H}^{1},\widehat{L}^{2})}

is continuous and vanishes at x¯0\bar{x}_{0}\,. We can therefore choose 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller, so that Θ​(x)<ε\Theta(x)<\varepsilon for every x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,. If ZZ is an arbitrary cylinder satisfying the abstract Floer equation (5.9) and having image in 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,, then the above calculation shows

∥(∂s𝖠^Z)​(s)∥ℒ​(H^1,L^2)=Θ​(Z​(s))<ε,\lVert(\partial_{s}\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z})(s)\rVert_{\mathcal{L}(\widehat{H}^{1},\widehat{L}^{2})}=\Theta(Z(s))<\varepsilon\,\,,

so that we have established (5.25). ∎

Choose 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) and constants c>0c>0 and 0<ε<c/20<\varepsilon<c/2 as in the previous claim and define

B:=c−2​ε>0.B:=\sqrt{c-2\varepsilon}>0\,\,.

Define moreover a continuous map 𝖤:𝒰​(x¯0)→L^2\mathsf{E}:\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow\widehat{L}^{2} by

𝖤​(x):=(x,𝖠x​x,Dx​𝖠​[𝖠x​x]​x+𝖠x2​x)∈C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)⊕3.\mathsf{E}(x):=\big(x,\,\mathsf{A}_{x}x,\,D_{x}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{x}x]\,x+\mathsf{A}_{x}^{2}\,x\big)\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n})^{\oplus 3}\,\,.

Observe that

(5.27) 𝖰^​𝖤​(x)=0if ​x​ is a constant loop with ​z′′​(x)≡0\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\mathsf{E}(x)=0\quad\text{if }x\text{ is a constant loop with }z^{\prime\prime}(x)\equiv 0

because for every constant loop xx with z′′​(x)≡0z^{\prime\prime}(x)\equiv 0 we have x∈ker⁡𝖠x∩ker⁡𝖠x¯0x\in\ker\mathsf{A}_{x}\cap\ker\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}} by (Fact 2) and hence 𝖤​(x)=(x,0,0)∈ker⁡𝖰^\mathsf{E}(x)=(x,0,0)\in\ker\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,.

Claim.

Let Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) be a smooth cylinder with image in 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\, and satisfying the abstract Floer equation (5.9). Define Z^=(Z,∂sZ,∂s2Z)\widehat{Z}=(Z,\partial_{s}Z,\partial_{s}^{2}Z)\,.

  1. (a)

    It holds Z^​(s)=𝖤​(Z​(s))\widehat{Z}(s)=\mathsf{E}(Z(s)) for every s≥s0s\geq s_{0}\,.

  2. (b)

    Suppose additionally that the limit Z​(∞):=lims→∞Z​(s)∈C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z(\infty):=\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}Z(s)\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) (in the C∞C^{\infty}-topology) exists and that

    Z​(∞)​ is a constant loop with ​z′′∘Z​(∞)≡0.\displaystyle Z(\infty)\text{ is a constant loop with }z^{\prime\prime}\circ Z(\infty)\equiv 0\,\,.

    Then

    (5.28) ∥𝖰^​Z^​(s)∥L^22≤∥𝖰^​𝖤​(Z​(s0))∥L^22​e−2​B​(s−s0)∀s≥s0.\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}(s)\rVert_{\widehat{L}^{2}}^{2}\leq\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\mathsf{E}(Z(s_{0}))\rVert_{\widehat{L}^{2}}^{2}\,e^{-2B(s-s_{0})}\qquad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,.
Proof of the claim.

Part (a) follows directly from (5.15) and (5.16). Now we show part (b). Define the smooth function f:[s0,∞)→[0,∞)f:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow[0,\infty) by

f​(s):=12​∥𝖰^​Z^​(s)∥L^2=12​∥𝖰^​𝖤​(Z​(s))∥L^2.f(s):=\frac{1}{2}\,\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}(s)\rVert_{\widehat{L}}^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\,\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\mathsf{E}(Z(s))\rVert_{\widehat{L}}^{2}\,\,.

We have seen in (5.22) that

f′′​(s)\displaystyle f^{\prime\prime}(s) =∥𝖰^​𝖠^Z​𝖰^​Z^∥L^22+⟨𝖰^​Z^,(∂s𝖠^Z)​𝖰^​Z^⟩L^2\displaystyle=\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}\rVert^{2}_{\widehat{L}^{2}}+\big\langle\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z},\,(\partial_{s}\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z})\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}\big\rangle_{\widehat{L}^{2}}
+⟨(𝖠^Z∗−𝖠^Z)​𝖰^​Z^,𝖠^Z​𝖰^​Z^⟩L^2+∥𝖠^Z​𝖰^​Z^∥L^22.\displaystyle\hskip 8.53581pt+\big\langle(\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}^{*}-\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z})\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z},\,\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}\big\rangle_{\widehat{L}^{2}}+\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{A}}_{Z}\,\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}\rVert_{\widehat{L}^{2}}^{2}\,\,.

Completely analogous to the proof of Proposition 5.8, we can estimate the first and fourth summand from below by (5.23) and the second and third summand from above by (5.25) and (5.24), yielding

f′′​(s)≥(2​c−4​ε)​∥𝖰^​Z^​(s)∥H^12≥(2​c−4​ε)​∥𝖰^​Z^​(s)∥L^22=4​B2​f​(s).f^{\prime\prime}(s)\geq(2c-4\varepsilon)\,\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}(s)\rVert_{\widehat{H}^{1}}^{2}\geq(2c-4\varepsilon)\,\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\widehat{Z}(s)\rVert_{\widehat{L}^{2}}^{2}=4B^{2}\,f(s)\,\,.

The asymptotic condition on ZZ and (5.27) now imply

lims→∞𝖰^​𝖤​(Z​(s))=𝖰^​𝖤​(Z​(∞))=0\lim_{s\rightarrow\infty}\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\mathsf{E}(Z(s))=\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\mathsf{E}(Z(\infty))=0

and therefore f​(s)​⟶s→∞​0f(s)\overset{s\rightarrow\infty}{\longrightarrow}0\,. The maximum principle (Lemma 5.7) now gives the claimed inequality (5.28). ∎

Part II: Define

Ξ1:𝒰​(x¯0)→[0,∞),Ξ1​(x):=∥𝖰^​𝖤​(x)∥L^2.\Xi_{1}:\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow[0,\infty)\,,\,\quad\Xi_{1}(x):=\lVert\widehat{\mathsf{Q}}\,\mathsf{E}(x)\rVert_{\widehat{L}^{2}}\,\,.

Note that Ξ1\Xi_{1} is continuous in the C∞C^{\infty}-topology and that it vanishes on constant loops xx with z′′​(x)≡0z^{\prime\prime}(x)\equiv 0 by (5.27). For a smooth cylinder Z:[s0,∞)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)Z:[s_{0},\infty)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) satisfying assumptions (i)–(iii) in Proposition 5.6, writing out (5.28) we have

(5.29) ∥𝖰​Z​(s)∥L22+∥∂s𝖰​Z​(s)∥L22+∥∂s2𝖰​Z​(s)∥L22≤Ξ1​(Z​(s0))2​e−2​B​(s−s0)∀s≥s0.\displaystyle\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z(s)\rVert_{L^{2}}^{2}+\lVert\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{Q}Z(s)\rVert_{L^{2}}^{2}+\lVert\partial_{s}^{2}\,\mathsf{Q}Z(s)\rVert_{L^{2}}^{2}\leq\Xi_{1}(Z(s_{0}))^{2}\,e^{-2B(s-s_{0})}\quad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,.

In the second part we now transform this estimate into an estimate for the H2H^{2}-norm of Z​(s)Z(s) by repeatedly invoking the Floer equation.999How this can be accomplished was again explained to me by Richard Siefring. That is, for a given cylinder ZZ as above, we must find exponential decay estimates for the L2L^{2}-norm of

(5.30) ∂t(𝖰​Z)=∂tZand∂t2(𝖰​Z)=∂t2Z,\partial_{t}(\mathsf{Q}\,Z)=\partial_{t}Z\quad\text{and}\quad\partial_{t}^{2}(\mathsf{Q}\,Z)=\partial_{t}^{2}Z\,\,,

where we have made use of (Fact 6). For convenience, we work with the shift u:=𝗌𝗁+​(Z)u:=\mathsf{sh}^{+}(Z)\,, so that Z=u¯=𝗌𝗁−​(u)Z=\bar{u}=\mathsf{sh}^{-}(u)\,. Of course, more precisely we should write u​(s,t)=𝗌𝗁+​(Z​(s))​(t)u(s,t)=\mathsf{sh}^{+}(Z(s))(t)\,. Notice that uu is a solution of the classical Floer equation (4.3) since ZZ is a solution of the abstract Floer equation ∂sZ=𝖠Z​Z\partial_{s}Z=\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z\,.

We have 𝖯​𝖠x¯0=0\mathsf{P}\,\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}=0 by (Fact 4) and moreover z′′​(𝖰​X)=z′′​(X)z^{\prime\prime}(\mathsf{Q}X)=z^{\prime\prime}(X) for every X∈L2X\in L^{2} because z′′​(𝖯​X)≡0z^{\prime\prime}(\mathsf{P}X)\equiv 0 as 𝖯​X∈ker⁡𝖠x¯0\mathsf{P}X\in\ker\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\,. Using this and the definition of the operators 𝖠x\mathsf{A}_{x} in (5.4) and (5.5), we compute

∂s𝖯​Z\displaystyle\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{P}Z =𝖯​∂sZ=𝖯​𝖠Z​Z=𝖯​(𝖠Z−𝖠x¯0)​Z\displaystyle=\mathsf{P}\,\partial_{s}Z=\mathsf{P}\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z=\mathsf{P}(\mathsf{A}_{Z}-\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}})\,Z
(5.31) =𝖯​((J0​(t)−Jt​(u))​∂tu¯+(J0​(t)​S0​(t)−Jt​(u)​S​(u))⋅z′′​(𝖰​Z)).\displaystyle=\mathsf{P}\Big((J_{0}(t)-J_{t}(u))\,\partial_{t}\bar{u}+(J_{0}(t)\,S_{0}(t)-J_{t}(u)\,S(u))\cdot z^{\prime\prime}(\mathsf{Q}\,Z)\Big)\,\,.

Rearranging (5.2) yields

∂tu¯\displaystyle\partial_{t}\bar{u} =Jt​(u)​∂su¯−S​(u)​z′′​(u¯)\displaystyle=J_{t}(u)\,\partial_{s}\bar{u}-S(u)\,z^{\prime\prime}(\bar{u})
(5.32) =Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(u¯))​(∂s(𝖰​Z)+∂s(𝖯​Z))−S​(𝗌𝗁+​(u¯))​z′′​(𝖰​Z).\displaystyle=J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(\bar{u}))\,(\partial_{s}(\mathsf{Q}Z)+\partial_{s}(\mathsf{P}Z))-S(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(\bar{u}))\,z^{\prime\prime}(\mathsf{Q}Z)\,\,.

Now insert (5.32) into (5.31) and rearrange so that the terms 𝖰​Z,∂s𝖰​Z\mathsf{Q}Z\,,\,\partial_{s}\mathsf{Q}Z and ∂s𝖯​Z\partial_{s}\mathsf{P}Z are collected. This can be written as

(5.33) ∂s𝖯​Z−𝖯​((J0​(t)−Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(u¯)))⋅Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(u¯))⋅∂s(𝖯​Z))=𝖢Z1​(𝖰​Z)+𝖢Z2​(∂s𝖰​Z),\displaystyle\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{P}Z-\mathsf{P}\Big((J_{0}(t)-J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(\bar{u})))\cdot J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(\bar{u}))\cdot\partial_{s}(\mathsf{P}Z)\Big)=\mathsf{C}^{1}_{Z}(\mathsf{Q}Z)+\mathsf{C}^{2}_{Z}(\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{Q}Z)\,\,,

where the argument of 𝖯\mathsf{P} on the left-hand side is a loop, so tt is not fixed therein, and where 𝖢x1,𝖢x2∈ℒ​(L2,L2)\mathsf{C}^{1}_{x}\,,\,\mathsf{C}^{2}_{x}\in\mathcal{L}(L^{2},L^{2}) are loop-dependent operators such that the maps 𝖢j:𝒰​(x¯0)→ℒ​(L2,L2),x↦𝖢xj\mathsf{C}^{j}:\,\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(L^{2},L^{2})\,,\,x\mapsto\mathsf{C}^{j}_{x} are continuous for j=1,2j=1,2\,. The precise formulae for 𝖢x1\mathsf{C}^{1}_{x} and 𝖢x2\mathsf{C}^{2}_{x} will be irrelevant subsequently but, to allow the reader to double-check, we nonetheless state them in (A.6) and (A.7) in the appendix. By continuity, choosing 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller if necessary, we may assume that the operator norms of 𝖢x1\mathsf{C}^{1}_{x} and 𝖢x2\mathsf{C}^{2}_{x} are bounded by some constant c1>0c_{1}>0 uniformly for all x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,. We can express the left-hand side of (5.33) in terms of a loop-depending operator as well: For x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) define 𝖣x∈ℒ​(L2,L2)\mathsf{D}_{x}\in\mathcal{L}(L^{2},L^{2}) by

(5.34) 𝖣x​(X):=X−𝖯​((J0​(t)−Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(x)))​Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(x))⋅X)∀X∈L2.\displaystyle\mathsf{D}_{x}(X):=X-\mathsf{P}\Big((J_{0}(t)-J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x)))\,J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x))\cdot X\Big)\qquad\forall\,X\in L^{2}\,\,.

Then the left-hand side of (5.33) is just 𝖣Z​(s)​(∂s(𝖯​Z)​(s))\mathsf{D}_{Z(s)}(\partial_{s}(\mathsf{P}Z)(s))\,. Clearly

𝖣:𝒰​(x¯0)→ℒ​(L2,L2),x↦𝖣x,\mathsf{D}:\,\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(L^{2},L^{2})\,,\,\quad x\mapsto\mathsf{D}_{x}\,\,,

is continuous and moreover its evaluation at x¯0\bar{x}_{0} is the identity, 𝖣x¯0=idL2\mathsf{D}_{\bar{x}_{0}}=\mathrm{id}_{L^{2}}\,, because

J0​(t)−Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(x¯0))=J0​(t)−J0​(t)=0.J_{0}(t)-J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(\bar{x}_{0}))=J_{0}(t)-J_{0}(t)=0\,\,.

It follows that, for any fixed choice of constant c2>1c_{2}>1\,, we can assume, after making 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller if necessary, that for all x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) the operator 𝖣x\mathsf{D}_{x} is invertible with

(5.35) ∥X∥L2≤c2​∥𝖣x​X∥L2∀X∈L2.\lVert X\rVert_{L^{2}}\leq c_{2}\,\lVert\mathsf{D}_{x}X\rVert_{L^{2}}\qquad\forall\,X\in L^{2}\,\,.

Since ZZ has image in 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,, for every s≥s0s\geq s_{0} (suppressing ss in the notation below)

∥∂s𝖯​Z∥L2\displaystyle\lVert\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{P}Z\rVert_{L^{2}} ≤c2​∥𝖣Z​∂s𝖯​Z∥L2​=(5.33)​c2​∥𝖢Z1​(𝖰​Z)+𝖢Z2​(∂s𝖰​Z)∥L2\displaystyle\leq c_{2}\,\lVert\mathsf{D}_{Z}\,\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{P}Z\rVert_{L^{2}}\overset{\text{\eqref{eq: partial_s P Z = C^1_Z(QZ) + C^2_Z(partial_s QZ)}}}{=}c_{2}\,\lVert\mathsf{C}_{Z}^{1}(\mathsf{Q}Z)+\mathsf{C}^{2}_{Z}(\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{Q}Z)\rVert_{L^{2}}
≤c2​(∥𝖢Z1​(𝖰​Z)∥L2+∥𝖢Z2​(∂s𝖰​Z)∥L2)\displaystyle\leq c_{2}\,\big(\lVert\mathsf{C}_{Z}^{1}(\mathsf{Q}Z)\rVert_{L^{2}}+\lVert\mathsf{C}_{Z}^{2}(\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{Q}Z)\rVert_{L^{2}}\big)
≤c1​c2​(∥𝖰​Z∥L2+∥∂s𝖰​Z∥L2)\displaystyle\leq c_{1}c_{2}\,\big(\lVert\mathsf{Q}Z\rVert_{L^{2}}+\lVert\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{Q}Z\rVert_{L^{2}}\big)
(5.36) ≤(5.29)​2​c1​c2​Ξ1​(Z​(s0))​e−B​(s−s0).\displaystyle\overset{\text{\eqref{eq: exponential decay - Lhat^2 estimate Qsfhat Zhat rewritten}}}{\leq}2c_{1}c_{2}\,\Xi_{1}(Z(s_{0}))\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\,\,.

We now have exponential decay estimates for 𝖰​Z\mathsf{Q}Z and ∂s𝖰​Z\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{Q}Z by (5.29) and for ∂s𝖯​Z\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{P}Z by (5.36).
