Returning to (5.32), this gives an exponential decay estimate for ∂tu¯\partial_{t}\bar{u}\,. In detail, we can bound the Euclidean operator norms of the matrices Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(x)​(t))J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x)(t)) and S​(𝗌𝗁+​(x)​(t))S(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x)(t)) uniformly for every t∈𝕊1t\in\mathbb{S}^{1} and every loop x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) sufficiently close to the constant loop at the origin x¯0\bar{x}_{0}\,. Using that ∥⋅∥L2\lVert\,\cdot\,\rVert_{L^{2}} is equivalent to the Euclidean L2L^{2}-norm, we estimate

(5.37) ∥∂tu¯∥L2​≤(5.32)​c3​(∥𝖰​Z∥L2+∥∂s𝖰​Z∥L2+∥𝖯​Z∥L2)≤c4​Ξ1​(Z​(s0))​e−B​(s−s0),\displaystyle\lVert\partial_{t}\bar{u}\rVert_{L^{2}}\overset{\text{\eqref{eq: partial_t Bar(u)}}}{\leq}c_{3}\,\big(\lVert\mathsf{Q}Z\rVert_{L^{2}}+\lVert\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{Q}Z\rVert_{L^{2}}+\lVert\mathsf{P}Z\rVert_{L^{2}}\big)\leq c_{4}\,\Xi_{1}(Z(s_{0}))\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\,\,,

with constant c3>0c_{3}>0 just depending on the equivalence of L2L^{2}-norms and the uniform bound on the operator norm of the matrices Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(x))J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x)) and S​(𝗌𝗁+​(x))S(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x)) and with c4=c4​(c1,c2,c3)>0c_{4}=c_{4}(c_{1},c_{2},c_{3})>0 a combinatorial constant.

We emphasize that the constants c1,…,c4>0c_{1},\ldots,c_{4}>0 and the size of the neighborhood 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) have been chosen independently of the cylinder ZZ. We could have defined the maps 𝖢1,𝖢2\mathsf{C}^{1}\,,\,\mathsf{C}^{2} and 𝖣\mathsf{D} before fixing the cylinder ZZ and make the appropriate choices. (For the sake of exposition we decided against this.) So (5.37) is an exponential decay estimate for ∂tZ=∂tu¯\partial_{t}Z=\partial_{t}\bar{u} of the desired form.

The exponential decay estimate for ∂t2Z=∂t2u¯\partial_{t}^{2}Z=\partial_{t}^{2}\bar{u} now works completely analogously. Derive (5.32) in ss-direction to obtain

∂s∂tu¯\displaystyle\partial_{s}\partial_{t}\bar{u} =Jt​(u)​(∂s2(𝖰​Z)+∂s2(𝖯​Z))−S​(u)​z′′​(∂s𝖰​Z)\displaystyle=J_{t}(u)\,\big(\partial_{s}^{2}(\mathsf{Q}Z)+\partial_{s}^{2}(\mathsf{P}Z)\big)-S(u)\,z^{\prime\prime}(\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{Q}Z)
(5.38) +(∂sJt​(u))​(∂s(𝖰​Z)+∂s(𝖯​Z))−(∂sS​(u))​z′′​(𝖰​Z).\displaystyle\hskip 8.53581pt+(\partial_{s}J_{t}(u))\,\big(\partial_{s}(\mathsf{Q}Z)+\partial_{s}(\mathsf{P}Z)\big)-(\partial_{s}S(u))\,z^{\prime\prime}(\mathsf{Q}Z)\,\,.

As before, we can use the abstract Floer equation to replace the ss-derivatives ∂s(Jt​(u))\partial_{s}(J_{t}(u)) and ∂s(S​(u))\partial_{s}(S(u)) by an expression depending only on the loop Z​(s)Z(s)\,. We carry this out exemplarily for ∂s(S​(u))\partial_{s}(S(u)) in Subsection A.5 in the appendix. In particular, after choosing 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller if necessary and using that Z​(s)∈𝒰​(x¯0)Z(s)\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,, we can assume that the matrix norms of ∂s(Jt​(u))\partial_{s}(J_{t}(u)) and ∂s(S​(u))\partial_{s}(S(u)) are uniformly bounded by a constant independent of the cylinder ZZ.
Similarly, deriving (5.31) in ss-direction and applying the Floer equation to replace ss-derivatives of Jt​(u)J_{t}(u) respectively S​(u)S(u)\,, will give on the right-hand side an expression in which every summand contains one of 𝖰​Z,∂s𝖰​Z,∂tu¯\mathsf{Q}Z\,,\,\partial_{s}\mathsf{Q}Z\,,\,\partial_{t}\bar{u} or ∂s∂tu¯\partial_{s}\partial_{t}\bar{u} and otherwise depends just on the loop Z​(s)Z(s). Substituting ∂s∂tu¯\partial_{s}\partial_{t}\bar{u} in this formula with the right-hand side in (5.38) and collecting the terms ∂s2𝖯​Z\partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{P}Z\,, we can write

𝖣Z​(∂s2𝖯​Z)\displaystyle\mathsf{D}_{Z}(\partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{P}Z) =∂s2𝖯​Z−𝖯​((J0​(t)−Jt​(u))​Jt​(u)⋅(∂s2𝖯​Z))\displaystyle=\partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{P}Z-\mathsf{P}\Big((J_{0}(t)-J_{t}(u))\,J_{t}(u)\cdot(\partial_{s}^{2}\mathsf{P}Z)\Big)
=𝖢Z3​(𝖰​Z)+𝖢Z4​(∂s𝖰​Z)+𝖢Z5​(∂s2𝖰​Z)+𝖢Z6​(∂s𝖯​Z)+𝖢Z7​(∂tu¯)\displaystyle=\mathsf{C}^{3}_{Z}(\mathsf{Q}Z)+\mathsf{C}^{4}_{Z}(\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{Q}Z)+\mathsf{C}^{5}_{Z}(\partial_{s}^{2}\,\mathsf{Q}Z)+\mathsf{C}^{6}_{Z}(\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{P}Z)+\mathsf{C}^{7}_{Z}(\partial_{t}\bar{u})

with certain loop-dependent operators 𝖢x3,…,𝖢x7∈ℒ​(L2,L2)\mathsf{C}^{3}_{x},\ldots,\mathsf{C}^{7}_{x}\in\mathcal{L}(L^{2},L^{2})\,, so that the maps 𝖢j:𝒰​(x¯0)→ℒ​(L2,L2),x↦𝖢xj\mathsf{C}^{j}:\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(L^{2},L^{2})\,,\,x\mapsto\mathsf{C}^{j}_{x}\,, are continuous respectively. The operator 𝖣x\mathsf{D}_{x} is the one defined in (5.34) above. Keeping (5.35) in mind and the already established exponential decay estimates in (5.29), (5.36) and (5.37), we can proceed similar to (5.36) and estimate

∥∂s2𝖯​Z∥L2\displaystyle\lVert\partial_{s}^{2}\,\mathsf{P}Z\rVert_{L^{2}} ≤c5​(∥𝖰​Z∥L2+∥∂s𝖰​Z∥L2+∥∂s2𝖰​Z∥L2+∥∂s𝖯​Z∥L2+∥∂tu¯∥L2)\displaystyle\leq c_{5}\,\big(\lVert\mathsf{Q}Z\rVert_{L^{2}}+\lVert\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{Q}Z\rVert_{L^{2}}+\lVert\partial_{s}^{2}\,\mathsf{Q}Z\rVert_{L^{2}}+\lVert\partial_{s}\,\mathsf{P}Z\rVert_{L^{2}}+\lVert\partial_{t}\bar{u}\rVert_{L^{2}}\big)
≤c6​Ξ1​(Z​(s0))​e−B​(s−s0),\displaystyle\leq c_{6}\,\Xi_{1}(Z(s_{0}))\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\,\,,

with constants c5,c6>0c_{5},c_{6}>0 independent of the cylinder ZZ. We therefore have an exponential decay estimate for ∂s2𝖯​Z\partial_{s}^{2}\,\mathsf{P}Z\,. In view of (5.38) and because the matrix norms of ∂s(Jt​(u))\partial_{s}(J_{t}(u)) and ∂s(S​(u))\partial_{s}(S(u)) have been bounded by a constant independent of the cylinder ZZ, we now also have an exponential decay estimate

∥∂s∂tu¯∥L2≤c7​Ξ1​(Z​(s0))​e−B​(s−s0)∀s≥s0.\lVert\partial_{s}\partial_{t}\bar{u}\rVert_{L^{2}}\leq c_{7}\,\Xi_{1}(Z(s_{0}))\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\qquad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,.

Finally, we derive (5.32) in tt-direction

∂t2u¯\displaystyle\partial_{t}^{2}\bar{u} =Jt​(u)​∂s∂tu¯−S​(u)​z′′​(∂tu¯)+∂t(Jt​(u))​∂su¯−∂t(S​(u))​z′′​(u¯)\displaystyle=J_{t}(u)\,\partial_{s}\partial_{t}\bar{u}-S(u)\,z^{\prime\prime}(\partial_{t}\bar{u})+\partial_{t}(J_{t}(u))\,\partial_{s}\bar{u}-\partial_{t}(S(u))\,z^{\prime\prime}(\bar{u})
=Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(u¯))​∂s∂tu¯−S​(𝗌𝗁+​(u¯))​z′′​(∂tu¯)\displaystyle=J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(\bar{u}))\,\partial_{s}\partial_{t}\bar{u}-S(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(\bar{u}))\,z^{\prime\prime}(\partial_{t}\bar{u})
+∂t(Jt∘𝗌𝗁+​(u¯))​(∂s(𝖰​Z)+∂s(𝖯​Z))−∂t(S∘𝗌𝗁+​(u¯))​z′′​(𝖰​Z).\displaystyle\hskip 8.53581pt+\partial_{t}(J_{t}\circ\mathsf{sh}^{+}(\bar{u}))\,\,\big(\partial_{s}(\mathsf{Q}Z)+\partial_{s}(\mathsf{P}Z)\big)-\partial_{t}(S\circ\mathsf{sh}^{+}(\bar{u}))\,\,z^{\prime\prime}(\mathsf{Q}Z)\,\,.

The matrix norms of Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(x))J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x)) and S​(𝗌𝗁+​(x))S(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x)) and its tt-derivatives can be uniformly bounded for every t∈𝕊1t\in\mathbb{S}^{1} and every loop x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,, by making 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller if necessary. Every of the above summands contains one of

∂s∂tu¯,∂tu¯,𝖰​Z,∂s(𝖰​Z),∂s(𝖯​Z)\partial_{s}\partial_{t}\bar{u}\,,\,\,\,\partial_{t}\bar{u}\,,\,\,\,\mathsf{Q}Z\,,\,\,\,\partial_{s}(\mathsf{Q}Z)\,,\,\,\,\partial_{s}(\mathsf{P}Z)

and, up to a constant independent of ZZ, we can bound the L2L^{2}-norm of each of them by Ξ1​(Z​(s0))​e−B​(s−s0)\Xi_{1}(Z(s_{0}))\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\,. Hence

(5.39) ∥∂t2u¯∥L2≤c8​Ξ1​(Z​(s0))​e−B​(s−s0),\lVert\partial_{t}^{2}\bar{u}\rVert_{L^{2}}\leq c_{8}\,\Xi_{1}(Z(s_{0}))\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\,\,,

which is the desired exponential decay estimate for ∂t2Z=∂t2u¯\partial_{t}^{2}Z=\partial_{t}^{2}\bar{u}\,. All in all, combining (5.29), (5.37), (5.39) and (5.30), for a suitable combinatorial constant c9=c9​(c1,…,c8)>0c_{9}=c_{9}(c_{1},\ldots,c_{8})>0 independent of the cylinder ZZ, we have

∥𝖰​Z​(s)∥H2≤c9​Ξ1​(Z​(s0))​e−B​(s−s0)∀s≥s0.\displaystyle\lVert\mathsf{Q}\,Z(s)\rVert_{H^{2}}\leq c_{9}\,\Xi_{1}(Z(s_{0}))\,e^{-B(s-s_{0})}\qquad\forall\,s\geq s_{0}\,\,.

Thus the continuous function Ξ:=c9​Ξ1:𝒰​(x¯0)→[0,∞)\Xi:=c_{9}\,\Xi_{1}:\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow[0,\infty)\,, which vanishes at constant loops xx with z′′​(x)≡0z^{\prime\prime}(x)\equiv 0 since Ξ1\Xi_{1} does, has the desired property claimed in Proposition 5.6. This finishes the second part and the proof of the proposition. ∎

We have successfully proven Proposition 5.6 and therefore also Theorem C.

Remark 5.10.
  1. (i)

    Considering the larger vector (Z,∂sZ,…,∂skZ)(Z,\partial_{s}Z,\ldots,\partial_{s}^{k}Z)\,, one analogously gets an exponential decay estimate for the HkH^{k}-norm of 𝖰​Z\mathsf{Q}\,Z and thus, as in the proof of Theorem C, also exponential decay estimates for ∂su,…,∂s∂tk−2u\partial_{s}u,\ldots,\,\partial_{s}\partial_{t}^{k-2}u\,. Using the Floer equation, from this one obtains for every k,ℓ≥0k,\ell\geq 0 an exponential decay estimate for ∂sk+1∂tℓu\partial_{s}^{k+1}\partial_{t}^{\ell}u\,. Here the higher-order partial derivatives of uu are understood in the coordinates of Lemma 5.1.

  2. (ii)

    The exponential coefficient B>0B>0 only depends on the operator 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\,.

Appendix A Supplements to the Proof of Theorem C

A.1. 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}} is the covariant Hessian

We show what we claimed in (Fact 1), namely that the operator 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}} is the covariant Hessian of 𝒜H\mathcal{A}_{H} at the loop x0x_{0}\,. A similar argument (for the Rabinowitz action functional) appears in [5, Lemma 30]. First we recall Hadamard’s lemma.

Lemma A.1 (Hadamard’s lemma).

Let f:ℝm→ℝkf:\mathbb{R}^{m}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{k} be a smooth function. For i=1,…,mi=1,\ldots,m define gi:ℝm→ℝkg_{i}:\mathbb{R}^{m}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{k} by

gi​(y):=∫01∂f∂yi​(r​y)​𝑑r.g_{i}(y):=\int_{0}^{1}\frac{\partial f}{\partial y_{i}}(ry)\,dr\,\,.

Then f​(y)=f​(0)+∑i=1myi​gi​(y)f(y)=f(0)+\sum_{i=1}^{m}y_{i}\,g_{i}(y)\,.

Remark A.2.
  1. (i)

    Observe that the differential at the origin is d0​f​[v]=∑i=1mvi​gi​(0)d_{0}f\,[v]=\sum_{i=1}^{m}v_{i}\,g_{i}(0)\,.

  2. (ii)

    Let MM be a smooth manifold and f:M×ℝm→ℝkf:M\times\mathbb{R}^{m}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{k} be a smooth function with f​(p,0)=0f(p,0)=0 for every p∈Mp\in M\,. Applying Hadamard’s lemma fiberwise, we can write f​(p,y)=∑i=1myi​gi​(p,y)f(p,y)=\sum_{i=1}^{m}y_{i}\,g_{i}(p,y) with smooth functions gi:M×ℝm→ℝkg_{i}:M\times\mathbb{R}^{m}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{k}\,. The differential of ff at (p,0)(p,0) is d(p,0)​f​[u,v]=∑i=1mvi​gi​(p,0)d_{(p,0)}f\,[u,v]=\sum_{i=1}^{m}v_{i}\,g_{i}(p,0)\,.

Lemma A.3.

In the fixed coordinates (ϑ,z)(\vartheta,z), the covariant Hessian at the loop x0x_{0}

∇2𝒜H​(x0):H1​(𝕊1,x0∗​T​W)→L2​(𝕊1,x0∗​T​W)\nabla^{2}\mathcal{A}_{H}(x_{0}):\,\,H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},x_{0}^{*}TW)\rightarrow L^{2}(\mathbb{S}^{1},x_{0}^{*}TW)

equals the operator 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}} in (5.5).

Proof.

The gJg_{J}-gradient ∇gJ𝒜H\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H} is a section of the Banach bundle

⨆x∈H1​(𝕊1,W)L2​(𝕊1,x∗​T​W)⟶H1​(𝕊1,W).\bigsqcup_{x\in H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W)}L^{2}(\mathbb{S}^{1},x^{*}TW)\longrightarrow H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},W)\,\,.

The coordinates (ϑ,z):U​→∼​𝕊1×ℝ2​n−1(\vartheta,z):U\overset{\sim}{\rightarrow}\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{2n-1} induce a local trivialization of this bundle over H1​(𝕊1,U)H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},U)\,. The covariant derivative ∇2𝒜H​(x0)\nabla^{2}\mathcal{A}_{H}(x_{0}) is equal to the vertical differential of ∇gJ𝒜H\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H} at its zero x0x_{0}\,, which, in the trivialization determined by (ϑ,z)(\vartheta,z)\,, is just the ordinary derivative Dx0​∇gJ𝒜HD_{x_{0}}\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H} at x0x_{0} of the principal part of ∇gJ𝒜H\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H} in this trivialization. We show that Dx0​∇gJ𝒜HD_{x_{0}}\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H} is equal to 𝖠x¯0∈ℒ​(H1,L2)\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\in\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})\,. To this end, let (−ε,ε)→H1​(𝕊1,U),a↦xa(-\varepsilon,\varepsilon)\rightarrow H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},U)\,,\,a\mapsto x_{a}\,, be an arbitrary path of loops through x0x_{0} with derivative X:=∂xa∂a​(0,⋅)X:=\frac{\partial x_{a}}{\partial a}(0,\,\cdot\,) at a=0a=0\,. Then

Dx0​∇gJ𝒜H​[X]\displaystyle D_{x_{0}}\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H}\,[X] =dd​a|a=0​∇gJ𝒜H​(xa)=dd​a|a=0​(−Jt​(xa)​(∂txa−XH​(xa)))\displaystyle=\frac{d}{da}\Big|_{a=0}\,\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H}(x_{a})=\frac{d}{da}\Big|_{a=0}\Big(-J_{t}(x_{a})\,(\partial_{t}x_{a}-X_{H}(x_{a}))\Big)
=−Jt​(x0)​(∂tX−dx0​XH​[X]),\displaystyle=-J_{t}(x_{0})\,(\partial_{t}X-d_{x_{0}}X_{H}\,[X])\,\,,

where d​XHdX_{H} denotes the differential of the vector-valued function XH:U→ℝ2​nX_{H}:U\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{2n}\,. For the last equality we used that ∂tx0−XH​(x0)=0\partial_{t}x_{0}-X_{H}(x_{0})=0\,, so that no derivative of JJ appears. Now recall that, since ∂ϑ−XH\partial_{\vartheta}-X_{H} vanishes on {z′′=0}\{z^{\prime\prime}=0\}\,, we could apply Hadamard’s lemma in the z′′z^{\prime\prime}-coordinate and write

∂ϑ−XH​(ϑ,z′,z′′)=S​(ϑ,z,z′′)⋅z′′.\partial_{\vartheta}-X_{H}(\vartheta,z^{\prime},z^{\prime\prime})=S(\vartheta,z,z^{\prime\prime})\cdot z^{\prime\prime}\,\,.

By Remark A.2 (ii), the differential of ∂ϑ−XH\partial_{\vartheta}-X_{H} at the point x0​(t)x_{0}(t) is

(A.1) −dx0​(t)​XH=S​(x0​(t))⋅z′′=S0​(t)⋅z′′,-d_{x_{0}(t)}X_{H}=S(x_{0}(t))\cdot z^{\prime\prime}=S_{0}(t)\cdot z^{\prime\prime}\,\,,

where z′′:ℝ2​n→ℝ2​n−dz^{\prime\prime}:\mathbb{R}^{2n}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^{2n-d} designates the projection to the last components. Hence

Dx0​∇gJ𝒜H​[X]=−J0​(t)​(∂tX+S0​(t)⋅z′′​(X))=𝖠x¯0​X.D_{x_{0}}\nabla^{g_{J}}\mathcal{A}_{H}\,[X]=-J_{0}(t)\,(\partial_{t}X+S_{0}(t)\cdot z^{\prime\prime}(X))=\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}}\,X\,\,.

∎

Lemma A.4.

Under assumption (MB(H,N)), the kernel of 𝖠x¯0\mathsf{A}_{\bar{x}_{0}} consists precisely of the constant loops XX with z′′​(X)≡0z^{\prime\prime}(X)\equiv 0\,.

Proof.

That the constant loops XX with z′′​(X)≡0z^{\prime\prime}(X)\equiv 0 lie in the kernel is clear. Conversely, suppose XX is in the kernel, so

0=−J0​(t)​(∂tX+S0​(t)⋅z′′​(X)).0=-J_{0}(t)\,(\partial_{t}X+S_{0}(t)\cdot z^{\prime\prime}(X))\,\,.

This implies, using that J0​(t)J_{0}(t) is invertible and (A.1), that ∂tX​(t)=dx0​(t)​XH⋅X​(t)\partial_{t}X(t)=d_{x_{0}(t)}X_{H}\cdot X(t)\,. In other words, XX is a solution of the linearized differential equation along x0x_{0}\,, so that

(A.2) X​(t)=dx0​(0)​ϕXHt⋅X​(0).X(t)=d_{x_{0}(0)}\phi_{X_{H}}^{t}\cdot X(0)\,\,.

From this and assumption (MB(H,N)), we infer X​(0)=X​(1)∈Tx0​(0)​NX(0)=X(1)\in T_{x_{0}(0)}N\,. Now XX is constant since dx0​(0)​ϕXHt|T​N=idd_{x_{0}(0)}\phi_{X_{H}}^{t}|_{TN}=\mathrm{id} and by (A.2) again. ∎

A.2. Adjoints depends continuously on base loop

In this subsection we show that the adjoints 𝖠x∗\mathsf{A}_{x}^{*} and 𝖠^x∗\widehat{\mathsf{A}}^{*}_{x} depend continuously on the loop xx. This will follow from the more general Proposition A.7 below.

Usually, the domain of the adjoint of an unbounded operator is defined to be the maximal possible domain. We deviate from this and for this reason specify what we mean by the adjoint.

Definition A.5 (Adjoint).

Given a Hilbert space (H,⟨⋅,⋅⟩H)(H,\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{H}) and a dense linear subspace D⊆HD\subseteq H\,. Let A:D→HA:D\rightarrow H be an unbounded linear operator. There exists at most one unbounded linear operator A∗:D→HA^{*}:D\rightarrow H satisfying

⟨A​x,y⟩H=⟨x,A∗​y⟩H∀x,y∈D.\langle Ax,y\rangle_{H}=\langle x,A^{*}y\rangle_{H}\qquad\forall\,x,y\in D\,\,.

If existent, this operator A∗A^{*} is called the ⟨⋅,⋅⟩H\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{H}-adjoint of AA.

Remark A.6.
  1. (i)

    A:D→HA:D\rightarrow H has an adjoint if and only if for every y∈Dy\in D there exists a constant cy≥0c_{y}\geq 0 with |⟨A​x,y⟩H|≤cy​|x|H|\langle Ax,y\rangle_{H}|\leq c_{y}\,|x|_{H} for all x∈Dx\in D\,.

  2. (ii)

    Suppose A:D→HA:D\rightarrow H has an adjoint A∗A^{*}\,. Let ϕ:H→H′\phi:H\rightarrow H^{\prime} be an isometry between the Hilbert spaces HH and H′H^{\prime} and set D′:=ϕ​(D)⊆H′D^{\prime}:=\phi(D)\subseteq H^{\prime}\,. Then ϕ​A​ϕ−1:D′→H′\phi\,A\,\phi^{-1}:D^{\prime}\rightarrow H^{\prime} has the H′H^{\prime}-adjoint (ϕ​A​ϕ−1)∗=ϕ​A∗​ϕ−1(\phi\,A\,\phi^{-1})^{*}=\phi\,A^{*}\,\phi^{-1}\,.

Consider the trivial bundle E:=𝕊1×ℝm→𝕊1E:=\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{m}\rightarrow\mathbb{S}^{1} over the circle. Every first-order linear partial differential operator (short: PDO) A:C∞​(E)→C∞​(E)A:C^{\infty}(E)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(E) can be written uniquely as

(A.3) A​X=R​(t)⋅∂tX+S​(t)⋅X∀X∈C∞​(𝕊1,ℝm),AX=R(t)\cdot\partial_{t}X+S(t)\cdot X\qquad\forall\,X\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{m})\,\,,

where R,S∈C∞​(𝕊1,ℝm×m)R,S\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}) are matrix-valued functions. Now let g=(gt)t∈𝕊1g=(g_{t})_{t\in\mathbb{S}^{1}} be an arbitrary smooth bundle metric on EE. It defines an L2L^{2}-inner product on the L2L^{2}-sections of EE as usual via

⟨X1,X2⟩L2g:=∫01gt​(X1​(t),X2​(t))​𝑑t.\langle X_{1},X_{2}\rangle_{L^{2}}^{g}:=\int_{0}^{1}g_{t}(X_{1}(t),X_{2}(t))\,dt\,\,.

We abbreviate H1=H1​(𝕊1,ℝm)H^{1}=H^{1}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{m}) and L2=L2​(𝕊1,ℝm)L^{2}=L^{2}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{m})\,. Because the bundle base 𝕊1\mathbb{S}^{1} is compact, the norm induced by the above L2L^{2}-inner product is equivalent to the standard (Euclidean) L2L^{2}-norm. The PDO AA induces a map A∈ℒ​(H1,L2)A\in\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})\,, which we also consider as unbounded linear operator on L2L^{2} with dense domain H1⊆L2H^{1}\subseteq L^{2}\,. We denote by A∗A^{*} its ⟨⋅,⋅⟩L2g\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{L^{2}}^{g}-adjoint. This exists and coincides with the formal adjoint of a PDO, described in [7, §10.1.3]. So A∗A^{*} is itself a first-order PDO and therefore A∗∈ℒ​(H1,L2)A^{*}\in\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})\,.

Proposition A.7.

For every R,S∈C∞​(𝕊1,ℝm×m)R,S\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}) define the PDO A(R,S):C∞​(E)→C∞​(E)A_{(R,S)}:C^{\infty}(E)\rightarrow C^{\infty}(E) by (A.3). Then the map

C∞​(𝕊1,ℝm×m)×C∞​(𝕊1,ℝm×m)→ℒ​(H1,L2),(R,S)↦(A(R,S))∗C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{m\times m})\times C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{m\times m})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})\,,\,\quad(R,S)\mapsto(A_{(R,S)})^{*}

is continuous, where each factor in the domain carries the C∞C^{\infty}-topology.

Remark A.8.

As the proof will show, the above map is even continuous if we endow the domain with the (C1×C0)(C^{1}\times C^{0})-topology

Proof of Proposition A.7.

The proof has two steps.

Step 1: We first consider the special case in which gg is the Euclidean bundle metric, that is gt=⟨⋅,⋅⟩eucg_{t}=\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{\mathrm{euc}} for every t∈𝕊1t\in\mathbb{S}^{1}\,. Integration by parts immediately shows that, in this case, the adjoint is given by

(A(R,S))∗​X=−RT⋅∂tX+(ST−∂tRT)⋅X,(A_{(R,S)})^{*}X=-R^{\mathrm{T}}\cdot\partial_{t}X+(S^{\mathrm{T}}-\partial_{t}R^{\mathrm{T}})\cdot X\,\,,

where R​(t)T,S​(t)TR(t)^{\mathrm{T}},S(t)^{\mathrm{T}} are the matrix transposes respectively. This shows that, if (R,S)(R,S) varies in the (C1×C0)(C^{1}\times C^{0})-topology, the coefficient functions of (A(R,S))∗(A_{(R,S)})^{*} vary continuously in the (C1×C0)(C^{1}\times C^{0})-topology, so that (A(R,S))∗(A_{(R,S)})^{*} varies continuously in ℒ​(H1,L2)\mathcal{L}(H^{1},L^{2})\,.

Step 2: Now we deal with the general case. Choose a global gg-orthonormal frame e1,…,em∈C∞​(𝕊1,ℝm)e_{1},\ldots,e_{m}\in C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{m}) of E=𝕊1×ℝmE=\mathbb{S}^{1}\times\mathbb{R}^{m}\,, which exists by Gram-Schmidt. The frame induces a global orthogonal trivialization Φ:(E,g)​→∼​(E,⟨⋅,⋅⟩euc)\Phi:(E,g)\overset{\sim}{\rightarrow}(E,\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{\mathrm{euc}})\,. The pushforward via Φ\Phi gives an isometry of Hilbert spaces

Φ∗:(L2,⟨⋅,⋅⟩L2g)​⟶∼​(L2,⟨⋅,⋅⟩L2euc),(Φ∗​(X))​(t):=Φt⋅X​(t),\Phi_{*}:\,\,(L^{2},\,\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{L^{2}}^{g})\overset{\sim}{\longrightarrow}(L^{2},\,\langle\,\cdot\,,\,\cdot\,\rangle_{L^{2}}^{\mathrm{euc}})\,,\,\quad(\Phi_{*}(X))(t):=\Phi_{t}\cdot X(t)\,\,,

which preserves regularity, Φ∗​(H1)=H1\Phi_{*}(H^{1})=H^{1} and Φ∗|H1∈ℒ​(H1,H1)\Phi_{*}|_{H^{1}}\in\mathcal{L}(H^{1},H^{1})\,. Using Step 1 and Remark A.6 (ii), it therefore suffices to show that the coefficient functions of the PDO Φ∗​A(R,S)​(Φ−1)∗\Phi_{*}A_{(R,S)}\,(\Phi^{-1})_{*} vary continuously in the (C1×C0)(C^{1}\times C^{0})-topology if (R,S)(R,S) varies in the (C1×C0)(C^{1}\times C^{0})-topology. And this is indeed the case, as a straightforward calculation shows that Φ∗​A(R,S)​(Φ−1)∗=A(R~,S~)\Phi_{*}\,A_{(R,S)}\,(\Phi^{-1})_{*}=A_{(\widetilde{R},\widetilde{S})}\,, where

R~​(t):=Φt⋅R​(t)⋅Φt−1andS~​(t):=Φt⋅S​(t)⋅Φt−1+Φt⋅R​(t)⋅∂t(Φt−1)​(t).\displaystyle\widetilde{R}(t):=\Phi_{t}\cdot R(t)\cdot\Phi_{t}^{-1}\quad\text{and}\quad\widetilde{S}(t):=\Phi_{t}\cdot S(t)\cdot\Phi_{t}^{-1}+\Phi_{t}\cdot R(t)\cdot\partial_{t}(\Phi_{t}^{-1})(t)\,\,.

∎

A.3. Equality ∂s𝖡Z=𝖢Z\partial_{s}\mathsf{B}_{Z}=\mathsf{C}_{Z}

We show that ∂s𝖡Z​(s)=𝖢Z​(s)\partial_{s}\mathsf{B}_{Z}(s)=\mathsf{C}_{Z(s)} for every ss\,, where 𝖡x\mathsf{B}_{x} and 𝖢x\mathsf{C}_{x} were defined in (5.18) and (5.26) respectively. We will only use the abstract Floer equation ∂sZ=𝖠Z​Z\partial_{s}Z=\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z and the usual rules for differentiation.

∂s𝖡Z=∂s(DZ2​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z,𝖠Z​Z]⏟=⁣:I​(s))+∂s(DZ​𝖠​[DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]​Z]⏟=⁣:II​(s))+∂s(DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z2​Z]⏟=⁣:III​(s)).\displaystyle\partial_{s}\mathsf{B}_{Z}=\partial_{s}\Big(\underbrace{D^{2}_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]}_{=:\mathrm{I}(s)}\Big)+\partial_{s}\Big(\underbrace{D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z]\,Z]}_{=:\mathrm{II}(s)}\Big)+\partial_{s}\Big(\underbrace{D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{2}\,Z]}_{=:\mathrm{III}(s)}\Big)\,\,.

For auxiliary purposes, we first compute separately

(A.4) ∂s(𝖠Z​Z)\displaystyle\partial_{s}(\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z) =DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]⋅Z+𝖠Z2​Z\displaystyle=D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z]\cdot Z+\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{2}\,Z
∂s(𝖠Z2​Z)\displaystyle\partial_{s}(\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{2}\,Z) =𝖠Z​(DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]⋅Z+𝖠Z2​Z)+(∂s𝖠Z)​𝖠Z​Z\displaystyle=\mathsf{A}_{Z}\big(D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z]\cdot Z+\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{2}\,Z\big)+(\partial_{s}\mathsf{A}_{Z})\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z
(A.5) =(5.16)​𝖠Z​(DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]⋅Z+𝖠Z2​Z)+DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]⋅𝖠Z​Z.\displaystyle\hskip-5.69054pt\overset{\text{\eqref{eq: exponetial decay - partial_s A_z}}}{=}\mathsf{A}_{Z}\big(D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\,Z]\cdot Z+\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{2}\,Z\big)+D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]\cdot\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z\,\,.

We now derive I\mathrm{I} with the help of (A.4):

∂sI\displaystyle\partial_{s}\mathrm{I} =DZ3​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z,𝖠Z​Z,𝖠Z​Z]+2​DZ2​𝖠​[∂s(𝖠Z​Z),𝖠Z​Z]\displaystyle=D_{Z}^{3}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]+2\,D^{2}_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\partial_{s}(\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z),\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]
=DZ3​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z,𝖠Z​Z,𝖠Z​Z]+2​DZ2​𝖠​[DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]⋅Z,𝖠Z​Z]+2​DZ2​𝖠​[AZ2​Z,𝖠Z​Z].\displaystyle=D_{Z}^{3}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]+2\,D_{Z}^{2}\mathsf{A}\,[D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]\cdot Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]+2\,D_{Z}^{2}\mathsf{A}\,[A_{Z}^{2}Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]\,\,.

Using (A.4) again, we next derive II\mathrm{II}

∂sII\displaystyle\partial_{s}\mathrm{II} =(∂sDZ​𝖠)​[DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]⋅Z]+DZ​𝖠​[DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]⋅∂sZ]+DZ​𝖠​[∂s(DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z])⋅Z]\displaystyle=(\partial_{s}D_{Z}\mathsf{A})\,[D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]\cdot Z]+D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,\Big[D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]\cdot\partial_{s}Z\Big]+D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,\Big[\partial_{s}\Big(D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]\Big)\cdot Z\Big]
=DZ2​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z,DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]⋅Z]+DZ​𝖠​[DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]⋅𝖠Z​Z]\displaystyle=D_{Z}^{2}\mathsf{A}\,\Big[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z,\,D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]\cdot Z\Big]+D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,\Big[D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]\cdot\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z\Big]
+DZ​𝖠​[DZ2​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z,𝖠Z​Z]⋅Z]+DZ​𝖠​[DZ​𝖠​[DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]⋅Z+𝖠Z2​Z]⋅Z]\displaystyle\hskip 11.38109pt+D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,\Big[D_{Z}^{2}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]\cdot Z\Big]+D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,\Big[D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,\Big[D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]\cdot Z+\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{2}Z\Big]\cdot Z\Big]

and finally, using (A.5), also compute

∂sIII\displaystyle\partial_{s}\mathrm{III} =(∂sDZ​𝖠)​[𝖠Z2​Z]+DZ​𝖠​[∂s(𝖠Z2​Z)]\displaystyle=(\partial_{s}D_{Z}\mathsf{A})\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{2}\,Z]+D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\partial_{s}(\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{2}\,Z)]
=DZ2​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z,𝖠Z2​Z]+DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​(DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]⋅Z)+𝖠Z3​Z+DZ​𝖠​[𝖠Z​Z]⋅𝖠Z​Z].\displaystyle=D_{Z}^{2}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z,\,\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{2}Z]+D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,\Big[\mathsf{A}_{Z}\big(D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]\cdot Z\big)+\mathsf{A}_{Z}^{3}Z+D_{Z}\mathsf{A}\,[\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z]\cdot\mathsf{A}_{Z}Z\Big]\,\,.

Thus 𝖢Z​(s)=∂sI​(s)+∂sII​(s)+∂sIII​(s)\mathsf{C}_{Z(s)}=\partial_{s}\mathrm{I}(s)+\partial_{s}\mathrm{II}(s)+\partial_{s}\mathrm{III}(s) in view of the definition of 𝖢\mathsf{C} in (5.26).

A.4. The operators 𝖢x1\mathsf{C}^{1}_{x} and 𝖢x2\mathsf{C}^{2}_{x}

We give explicit formulae for the loop-depending operators 𝖢x1,𝖢x2∈ℒ​(L2,L2)\mathsf{C}^{1}_{x}\,,\,\mathsf{C}^{2}_{x}\in\mathcal{L}(L^{2},L^{2}) appearing in (5.33). For x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) and X∈L2X\in L^{2} set

(A.6) 𝖢x1​(X)\displaystyle\mathsf{C}_{x}^{1}(X) :=𝖯​(J0​(t)⋅(S0​(t)−S​(𝗌𝗁+​(x)))⋅z′′​(X))\displaystyle:=\mathsf{P}\Big(J_{0}(t)\cdot\big(S_{0}(t)-S(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x))\big)\cdot z^{\prime\prime}(X)\Big)
(A.7) 𝖢x2​(X)\displaystyle\mathsf{C}_{x}^{2}(X) :=𝖯​((J0​(t)−Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(x)))⋅Jt​(𝗌𝗁+​(x))⋅X).\displaystyle:=\mathsf{P}\Big(\big(J_{0}(t)-J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x))\big)\cdot J_{t}(\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x))\cdot X\Big)\,\,.

Here the arguments of 𝖯\mathsf{P} are loops in L2L^{2} themselves. Clearly, for j=1,2j=1,2\,, the map

𝒰​(x¯0)→ℒ​(L2,L2),x↦𝖢xj,\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow\mathcal{L}(L^{2},L^{2})\,,\,\quad x\mapsto\mathsf{C}^{j}_{x}\,\,,

is continuous, where as usual 𝒰​(x¯0)⊆C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\subseteq C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n}) carries the C∞C^{\infty}-topology.

A.5. Eliminating ss-derivatives via the Floer equation

We show how to transform ∂s(S​(u))\partial_{s}(S(u)) into a term just depending on the loop 𝗌𝗁−​(us)=Z​(s)\mathsf{sh}^{-}(u_{s})=Z(s) by using the abstract Floer equation (5.9). The ss-derivative in ∂s(Jt​(u))\partial_{s}(J_{t}(u)) can be eliminated analogously. Recall that ∂su=∂su¯=∂sZ\partial_{s}u=\partial_{s}\bar{u}=\partial_{s}Z\,. We compute

∂s(S∘u)​(s,t)\displaystyle\partial_{s}(S\circ u)\,(s,t) =d​Su​(s,t)⋅∂su​(s,t)=(d​S)𝗌𝗁+​(Z​(s))​(t)⋅(∂sZ​(s))​(t)\displaystyle=dS_{u(s,t)}\cdot\partial_{s}u(s,t)=(dS)_{\mathsf{sh}^{+}(Z(s))(t)}\cdot(\partial_{s}Z(s))(t)
=(d​S)𝗌𝗁+​(Z​(s))​(t)⋅(𝖠Z​(s)​Z​(s))​(t)\displaystyle=(dS)_{\mathsf{sh}^{+}(Z(s))(t)}\cdot(\mathsf{A}_{Z(s)}Z(s))(t)

so that

∂s(S∘u)​(s,⋅)=(d​S)𝗌𝗁+​(Z​(s))⋅(𝖠Z​(s)​Z​(s)).\partial_{s}(S\circ u)(s,\,\cdot\,)=(dS)_{\mathsf{sh}^{+}(Z(s))}\cdot(\mathsf{A}_{Z(s)}Z(s))\,\,.

The loop on the right-hand side is the evaluation at Z​(s)Z(s) under the continuous map

C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n)⊇𝒰​(x¯0)→C∞​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n×(2​n−d)),x↦(d​S)𝗌𝗁+​(x)⋅(𝖠x​x).\displaystyle C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n})\supseteq\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\rightarrow C^{\infty}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n\times(2n-d)})\,,\,\quad x\mapsto(dS)_{\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x)}\cdot(\mathsf{A}_{x}\,x)\,\,.

By continuity, we can choose 𝒰​(x¯0)\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0}) smaller, so that we have a uniform C0​(𝕊1,ℝ2​n×(2​n−d))C^{0}(\mathbb{S}^{1},\mathbb{R}^{2n\times(2n-d)})-norm bound for (d​S)𝗌𝗁+​(x)⋅(𝖠x​x)(dS)_{\mathsf{sh}^{+}(x)}\cdot(\mathsf{A}_{x}x) for all x∈𝒰​(x¯0)x\in\mathcal{U}(\bar{x}_{0})\,.

References

  • [1] P. Albers and D. Hein (2016) Cuplength estimates in Morse cohomology. J. Topol. Anal. 8 (2), pp. 243–272. External Links: ISSN 1793-5253,1793-7167, Document, Link, MathReview (Darko Milinković) Cited by: §1.2, §2.4, §3.1, Lemma 3.2.
  • [2] F. Bourgeois and A. Oancea (2009) Symplectic homology, autonomous Hamiltonians, and Morse-Bott moduli spaces. Duke Math. J. 146 (1), pp. 71–174. External Links: ISSN 0012-7094,1547-7398, Document, Link, MathReview (Tobias Ekholm) Cited by: §4.2, §4.2, §4.2, §5.2, Remark 5.9, §5.
  • [3] A. Cannas da Silva (2001) Lectures on symplectic geometry. Lecture Notes in Mathematics, Vol. 1764, Springer-Verlag, Berlin. Cited by: §5.1.
  • [4] K. Cieliebak and U. Frauenfelder (2011) Morse homology on noncompact manifolds. J. Korean Math. Soc. 48 (4), pp. 749–774. External Links: ISSN 0304-9914,2234-3008, Document, Link, MathReview (David E. Hurtubise) Cited by: §2.4.
  • [5] A. Fauck (2016) Rabinowitz-floer homology on brieskorn manifolds. Ph.D. Thesis. External Links: Link Cited by: §A.1, §4.2, §5.1, §5, footnote 6.
  • [6] H. Hofer, K. Wysocki, and E. Zehnder (1996) Properties of pseudoholomorphic curves in symplectisation. IV. Asymptotics with degeneracies. In Contact and symplectic geometry (Cambridge, 1994), Publ. Newton Inst., Vol. 8, pp. 78–117. External Links: ISBN 0-521-57086-7, MathReview (Karl Friedrich Siburg) Cited by: §5.3.
  • [7] L. I. Nicolaescu (2021) Lectures on the geometry of manifolds. 3rd ed edition, World Scientific Publishing Co. Pte. Ltd., Hackensack, NJ. Cited by: §A.2, footnote 5.
  • [8] R. S. Palais (1966) Homotopy theory of infinite dimensional manifolds. Topology 5, pp. 1–16. External Links: ISSN 0040-9383, Document, Link, MathReview (A. Dold) Cited by: §3.2.3.
  • [9] M. Schwarz (1993) Morse homology. Progress in Mathematics, Vol. 111, Birkhäuser Verlag, Basel. Cited by: §3.2.1, §3.2.3, §3.2, §3.
  • [10] P. Seidel (2008) A biased view of symplectic cohomology. In Current developments in mathematics, 2006, pp. 211–253. External Links: ISBN 978-1-57146-167-4, MathReview (Timothy Perutz) Cited by: §4.2, §4.2, §4.2.

Instructions for reporting errors

We are continuing to improve HTML versions of papers, and your feedback helps enhance accessibility and mobile support. To report errors in the HTML that will help us improve conversion and rendering, choose any of the methods listed below:

  • Click the "Report Issue" () button, located in the page header.

Tip: You can select the relevant text first, to include it in your report.

Our team has already identified the following issues. We appreciate your time reviewing and reporting rendering errors we may not have found yet. Your efforts will help us improve the HTML versions for all readers, because disability should not be a barrier to accessing research. Thank you for your continued support in championing open access for all.

Have a free development cycle? Help support accessibility at arXiv! Our collaborators at LaTeXML maintain a list of packages that need conversion, and welcome developer contributions.
